##### ↳ ↳ Re: evaluate problem

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NOSPAM-hot.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-01T01:13:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<a87los$q1r5j$1@ID-63521.news.dfncis.de> <20020331183715.26426.00000628@mb-ff.aol.com>`

```
Not required anywhere that I know of, but putting it in is both good
practice and -
more importantly - will probably show up the real problem in his code. (A
period
where one should not be, or some other error in scoping.)

-Paul

"Jnjsle" <jnjsle@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20020331183715.26426.00000628@mb-ff.aol.com...
> Hi Bjorn,
>
> I speak as an IBM MVS Cobol user when I say that end-eval is not required
to
> end an eval stmt. I'm not sure of other Cobol implementations.
>
> In addition, MVS Cobol will recognize only the first 8 characters of a pgm
name
> used in a CALL stmt. I noticed that at least one of your CALLs uses a 9
> character module name. If your version of Cobol truncates the pgm name to
8
> chars, and your system carries multiple modules that begin with those 8
chars,
> this may be the cause of the error and accompanying message.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
Hi Bjorn,

It would be worth taking a look at the GO TO hv.

This may be taking you back into the evaluate while the evaluate thinks it
still has the scope of control.

If you execute the go to you do not complete the evaluate.  Many bad things
then start to happen.

Best Of luck

Cookie
```

#### ↳ Re: evaluate problem

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2002-04-01T10:18:04-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CA8A45C.149DFEA3@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<a87los$q1r5j$1@ID-63521.news.dfncis.de>`

```
Bjoern,
I know you didn't ask us to criticize your code, but the EVALUATE statement is
how COBOL supports the Structured Programming CASE construct.  If you are
using it, why are you using the non-structured GO statement?

I agree with the others who say they need to know more about the platform and
compiler you are using before we can help with the "not unique" problem.  Do
you know what the 119 refers to?  Is it the run time error message number?  Is
it the source code statement number?  What is the value of the "fun" variable
the first time?  How about the second time?
Colin

"Bjï¿½rn Mï¿½ller" wrote:

> hi everybody,
>
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> bjoern
```

#### ↳ Re: evaluate problem

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-04-01T20:05:10+00:00

- **References:** `<a87los$q1r5j$1@ID-63521.news.dfncis.de>`

```
Some runtimes on some platforms use
file names when searching for a program
on disk, but use program id when searching
memory, for a 2cd execution or to cancel.

Check the program id's.  Are they all the same?

news:a87los$q1r5j$1@ID-63521.news.dfncis.de...
```

<a id="t-8873d28df4"></a>
## Thread: tiny-cobol: bug with char-length OCCURS data — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### tiny-cobol: bug with char-length OCCURS data

- **From:** jvstrangio@ozemail.com.au (Jack Strangio)
- **Date:** 2002-04-01T08:18:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Gtvr34.22M@ozemail.com.au>`

```
Noticed this bug/feature in tiny-cobol 0.57 yesterday when 
trying to recompile a cobol program written about 10-15 
years ago.

It seems that in the source code the C language type 'char' is 
being promoted to an 'int'. (Or something like that. Anyway, 
what should be ONE ascii byte is being interpreted to be TWO
ascii bytes. Even if we assume that we're trying to include
the ability in Tiny-Cobol to use unicode, it is not performing
consistently at all.

I have condensed the  original cobol program  code  into the
code below in order to demonstrate the incorrect behaviour.

This is actually off-topic for this newsgroup and I haven't
had a chance to look at the Tiny-Cobol source code myself yet,
but I was wondering if any of you guys had seen this before
and had a fix for it.


OUTPUT FROM tiny-cobol:
=======================
input string 
12345678
decrement-counter: char-counter  08 >  <
decrement-counter: char-counter  07 >  <
decrement-counter: char-counter  06 >  <
decrement-counter: char-counter  05 >  <
decrement-counter: char-counter  04 >78<
decrement-counter: char-counter  03 >56<
decrement-counter: char-counter  02 >34<
decrement-counter: char-counter  01 >12<


OUTPUT from Utah Cobol:
=======================
INPUT STRING 12345678

DECREMENT-COUNTER: CHAR-COUNTER 08 >8<
DECREMENT-COUNTER: CHAR-COUNTER 07 >7<
DECREMENT-COUNTER: CHAR-COUNTER 06 >6<
DECREMENT-COUNTER: CHAR-COUNTER 05 >5<
DECREMENT-COUNTER: CHAR-COUNTER 04 >4<
DECREMENT-COUNTER: CHAR-COUNTER 03 >3<
DECREMENT-COUNTER: CHAR-COUNTER 02 >2<
DECREMENT-COUNTER: CHAR-COUNTER 01 >1<


PROGRAM LISTING
================
000001	 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000002	 PROGRAM-ID. INPUT-DATA.
000003	 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000004	 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
000005	 SOURCE-COMPUTER. C1.
000006	 OBJECT-COMPUTER. C1.
000007	 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
000008	 DATA DIVISION.
000009	 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000009
000010	 77  CHAR-COUNTER PIC S99V.
000011
000011	 01  INPUT-STRING.
000012	     02  CHAR        PIC X(1), OCCURS 8 TIMES.
000012
000013	 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000014	 MAIN.
000015	     DISPLAY "INPUT STRING ".
000016	     ACCEPT INPUT-STRING.
000017	     MOVE 8 TO CHAR-COUNTER.
000018	     PERFORM DECREMENT-COUNTER UNTIL CHAR-COUNTER < 1 .
000019	     GO TO EOJ.
000020
000020	 DECREMENT-COUNTER.
000021	     DISPLAY "DECREMENT-COUNTER: CHAR-COUNTER " CHAR-COUNTER " >"
000022	              CHAR (CHAR-COUNTER) "<".
000023	     SUBTRACT 1 FROM CHAR-COUNTER.
000024
000024	 EOJ.    
000025	      STOP RUN.
000026	     END PROGRAM INPUT-DATA.



Jack
```

#### ↳ Re: tiny-cobol: bug with char-length OCCURS data

- **From:** jvstrangio@ozemail.com.au (Jack Strangio)
- **Date:** 2002-04-02T14:45:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Gty3o2.5KB@ozemail.com.au>`
- **References:** `<Gtvr34.22M@ozemail.com.au>`

```
jvstrangio@ozemail.com.au (Jack Strangio) writes:

> 000011	 01  INPUT-STRING.
> 000012	     02  CHAR        PIC X(1), OCCURS 8 TIMES.


As David Essex and Bill Klein emailed me, it was the
comma at the end of the "PIC X(1), " which seems to have caused
the problem. They stated that the line should have read

 000012             02  CHAR        PIC X(1) OCCURS 8 TIMES.


As I wrote to David, my recollection was that the comma was more
or less optional.

I used to put it in because it seemed  "tidier" to me. After the
emails, I rummaged through my bookshelf and hauled out the software
manual. I found that my memory actually had been correct regarding the
comma, but that the syntax sequence within the line was wrong. It should
have read something like this:

 000012             02  CHAR, OCCURS 8 TIMES	PIC X.


Ah, well......


Thanks, guys. 
(Perhaps I should apologise to the Tiny-Cobol folks. :-)    )

Jack
```

## Thread: Cobol to Java migration — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Cobol to Java migration

- **From:** diogocalvario@clix.pt (Diogo Calvário)
- **Date:** 2002-04-01T02:12:50-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8b6dcb8b.0204010212.57b23ce6@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi

I would like to migrate a Cobol application to Java (EJB). Is there
any tool/framework/best practices out there (free or not)? Wich are
the best ones, in your experience?

Thank you

Diogo Calvï¿½rio
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol to Java migration

- **From:** "Ira D. Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-01T08:05:20-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ca866e5$1@giga.realtime.net>`
- **References:** `<8b6dcb8b.0204010212.57b23ce6@posting.google.com>`

```
We build highly automated translation tools for carrying out large scale
conversions.
They are almost always custom, because customers usually
have different starting points (OS, configuration, additional
applications and 3rd party packages) and always want
a fairly unique combination of the above as targets.
EJBs are probably a bit better, but even then the
question of *which* beans makes the result unique.

To see how our translation technology works,
see http://www.semdesigns.com/Products/DMS/Porting/Porting_files/frame.htm
The example shown is being done for a large
US defense contractor.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol to Java migration

- **From:** Doug Scott <dwscott@ieee.org>

- **References:** `<8b6dcb8b.0204010212.57b23ce6@posting.google.com>`

```
Diogo,

> I would like to migrate a Cobol application to Java (EJB).

Is this something you want to do, or have to do? Cobol's pretty fast, and 
Java's pretty slow. I'm not sure if you'd like the result.

---

Doug
```

<a id="t-4f9b0db8be"></a>
## Thread: SYNCSORT returns negative numbers BEFORE positive? — 3 msgs

### SYNCSORT returns negative numbers BEFORE positive?

- **From:** hlngus@hotmail.com (Yvonne G)
- **Date:** 2002-04-01T09:29:07-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d999b872.0204010929.59911a42@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,
I'm getting negative numbers at the beginning of a descending sort,
instead of at the end. The columns 576 - 579 are PIC S9(09) COMP .

I'm sure it's a basic error on my part, but can someone enlighten me
as to what can be modified?

* STEP 020 SORT HLDHIST FILE BY YTD-DLY-DRAW    
// DLBL HLDHIST,'HLDHIST.FILE',7                
// EXTENT ,POOL01,1,0,1,100                     
// DLBL HLDOUT1,'HLDHIST.SORTED',7              
// EXTENT ,POOL01,1,0,1,100                     
// EXEC SORT,SIZE=256K                          
 OPTION PRINT=CRITICAL,FILNM=(HLDOUT1,HLDHIST)  
 SORT FIELDS=(576,4,D),FORMAT=BI,FILES=1,WORK=1 
 RECORD TYPE=F,LENGTH=(600)                     
 INPFIL BLKSIZE=30000                           
 OUTFIL BLKSIZE=30000                           
 MODS PH1=(SORTMOD,L500,E15)

Thanks in advance.
```

#### ↳ Re: SYNCSORT returns negative numbers BEFORE positive?

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-02T10:17:06+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gty54i0.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE>`
- **References:** `<d999b872.0204010929.59911a42@posting.google.com>`

```
On 1 Apr 2002 09:29:07 -0800, Yvonne G wrote:

>Hi,
>I'm getting negative numbers at the beginning of a descending sort,
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks in advance.

Hi,

Negative numers have their first bit on, so sorting binary descending will
order them first.
To get around this, specify AQ as fieldtype and add ALTSEQ control cards to
specify the correct order.

SyncSort used to have an option to specify bits in the sort fileds card. If
your version allows this, sort ascending on the first bit and descending on
the rest

Hope this helps




Willem Clements
Brainbench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com
```

<a id="t-8f2fab1689"></a>
## Thread: UNSTRING DELMITED BY ALL SPACES, SPACES? — 1 this month / 11 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

_Thread started [2002-03](2002-03.md#t-8f2fab1689)._

#### ↳ Re: UNSTRING DELMITED BY ALL SPACES, SPACES?

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2002-04-01T10:32:49-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CA8A7D1.EC188F14@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<20020326234354.14073.00000278@mb-ce.aol.com>`

```
YM,
As you describe the data, it contains delimiters (spaces) before the first data,
between each data, and after the last data.  In this case, you'd need another
receiving field for the null item _before_ your first data.  I would expect
WS-PART-1 to be null (have whatever was in it before the UNSTRING), WS-PART-2 to
have the first data, and WS-PART-3 to have the second data.  The UNSTRING would be
"unfinished"; that is, if you had a POINTER, and had set it to 1 before executing
the UNSTRING, it would be at a value less than the total length of WS-PART-NO after
execution (actually, pointing at the start of the third data).
Colin

YukonMama wrote:

> Well one of the Big 3 has come up with a better idea and is now having their
> suppliers download new pricing information from their web site into CSV files.
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> separated?
```

## Thread: OS/VS COBOL and z/OS Version 1 Release 3 — 2 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

_Thread started [2002-03](2002-03.md#t-09e1226ebb)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OS/VS COBOL and z/OS Version 1 Release 3

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-01T13:21:56-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a8ac0q$358$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<1de0eb0f.0203151001.7ce5c4c7@posting.google.com> <3CA7CC07.8FE1C894@ix.netcom.com>`

```
Steve,
  Are you looking for any "help" on your problem, or are you able to solve
them (one by one).

Personally, I would be VERY surprised if this is a problem with any specific
release of LE (or OS/390 - z/OS).

There are a couple of things that I can think of that MIGHT impact a
"translation" from DOS/VS COBOL to OS/VS COBOL (which I think is what your
compiler change is).  If you are actually converting between other
compilers, can you tell us from which to which.

Things to look at:

1) What was the LANGLVL setting under VSE?  If it was LANGLVL(1) are you
using LANGLVL(1) or (2) with OS/VS COBOL?  If not, some problems may occur.

2) Are any/all of the "problem" files multi-volume tapes?  I remember there
being some "problems" in the VS COBOL II days of VSE with end-of-volume
processing.  This may WELL cause problems when changing OS.

3) Are any of the problems with VSAM files?  If so, you might want to check
not only the IDCAMS info (to insure they are identical in the 2
environments) - but also whether or not there are DOCUMENTED restrictions
that might have been ignored under VSE, but enforced under z/OS.

***

If you are actually doing a conversion from DOS/VS COBOL to a "currently
supported" compiler under z/OS - which compiler are you using?  There are
LOTS of migration issues there.

If you are staying with OS/VS COBOL, does the program work when STEPLIBing
to the OS/VS COBOL run-time library (or is that what you are using already -
in which case how does it work with the LE run-time library).

Finally, if you are able to "fix" the problems "one by one" - what type of
underlying fixes are you finding?  This may give me (us) an idea of what the
general problem is.
```

## Thread: IBM offers "resource analyzer" - per COBOL Statement — 1 msgs

### IBM offers "resource analyzer" - per COBOL Statement

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-01T13:43:27-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a8adea$3l$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In case other have missed it, IBM (which is already competing with such
products as File-Aid, Xpediter, InterTest, etc) has announced a product that
(as I read it) is intended to compete with STROBE and InTune.  See:

 http://www.ibmlink.ibm.com/usalets&parms=H_202-065

which says (in part),

"Application Monitor collects samples from the monitored address space and
analyzes the system or resource application in the following categories:

  a.. Total address space utilization of all modules in the address space
  b.. CSECT utilization within each load module
  c.. Instruction or statement utilization within each CSECT
  d.. COBOL statements utilization within each COBOL module
If the DB2� option is installed, Application Monitor analyzes the following
DB2 categories for a DB2 application:

  a.. Total address space utilization of all modules in the address space
  b.. CSECT and SQL utilization within each load module
  c.. Detailed DB2 delay information "
```

<a id="t-829a231b0e"></a>
## Thread: tiny-cobol: bug with char-length OCCURS data — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Re: tiny-cobol: bug with char-length OCCURS data

- **From:** David Essex <dessex@arvotek.net>
- **Date:** 2002-04-01T18:04:39-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CA8E787.47326961@arvotek.net>`

```
Jack Strangio <jvstrangio@ozemail.com.au> wrote:

> Noticed this bug/feature in tiny-cobol 0.57 yesterday when
> trying to recompile a cobol program written about 10-15
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> ascii bytes. 

The problem appears to be in the PIC clause, specifically the ','.

TC is parsing the 'X(1),' as having a length of 2 characters, or
equivalent to X(2). 

Was this intentional ?

In any case if you modify the code as follows it works as expected.

01  INPUT-STRING.
    02  CHAR PIC X(1), OCCURS 8 TIMES.

01  INPUT-STRING.
    02  CHAR PIC X(1) OCCURS 8 TIMES.


> Even if we assume that we're trying to include the ability in 
> Tiny-Cobol to use unicode, it is not performing consistently 
> at all.

AFAIK, no work as been done yet with ALPHABET or foreign character sets,
so you are working on the cutting edge.


> This is actually off-topic for this newsgroup and I haven't
> had a chance to look at the Tiny-Cobol source code myself yet,
> but I was wondering if any of you guys had seen this before
> and had a fix for it.

Well it is COBOL related. 

However addressing TC specific comments and/or bug reports to the TC
mailing list, would almost guarantee a reply. 



David Essex
```

#### ↳ Re: tiny-cobol: bug with char-length OCCURS data

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-01T17:32:56-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a8aqne$e15$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3CA8E787.47326961@arvotek.net>`

```
Concerning

02  CHAR        PIC X(1), OCCURS 8 TIMES.

There are some pretty specific rules in the ANSI Standard about
  - when it is legal to end a Picture clause with either a comma or a period
  - when (if used) the comma and period are editing symbols and when they
are separators
  - what clauses are allowed AFTER a picture clause that ends with a comma
or period

I tried (quickly) to find the exact 85 Standard rule that would "apply" to
this statement.  I think (but am not positive) that it is NON-CONFORMING
source code.

See page VI-33 which states (in part),

"If the insertion character ',' (comma) is the last symbol in the PICTURE
character-string, the PICTURE clause must be the last clause of the data
description entry and must be immediately followed by the separator period."
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: tiny-cobol: bug with char-length OCCURS data

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-04-03T15:06:29-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B8D0CAF50C4678EE0F751990@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<3CA8E787.47326961@arvotek.net> <a8aqne$e15$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Mon, 1 Apr 2002 18:32:56 -0500, William M. Klein wrote
> 02  CHAR        PIC X(1), OCCURS 8 TIMES.
> [...]
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> description entry and must be immediately followed by the separator period."

Bill,

I agree with your interpretation. This is non-conforming code. 
Therefore any interpretation by the compiler is legal in the context of 
the standard. This is certainly an ill-advised place for an extension, 
though, as the result can be so confusing. My guess is that it simply 
resulted from a failure to add a check for the condition, rather than 
as an intentional extension. If I were using this compiler, I'd ask for 
a change.

COBOL syntax can be so messy and inconsistent ... anyone who has ever 
written even the simplest COBOL scanner (for limited program analysis) 
knows that you have to use different scanning rules for PIC clauses 
than for anything else in the language.

This is one reason that using commas for "readability" is a bad idea, 
even though they may look natural in many contexts.

Edward Reid
```

- **References:** `<a7ss1q$sli$1@panix1.panix.com> <3CA538FD.D8AF124@optonline.net> <a83f8u$410$1@panix1.panix.com> <3CA790D0.9B24DA4E@optonline.net> <a8885m$1vo$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
I ran several tests of a IEFBR14 job with and without /*SCHEDULE, but the
resulting jes2 job log messages were all the same, so that did not help. So
I called MVS support and they confirmed that /*SCHEDULE  is implemented via
an in-house coded jes2 exit. 

<snip>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 'Sleeping' Jobs - Pausing w/ ILBOWAT0

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 2002-04-06T07:11:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CAE9FDC.DC040E1C@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<a7ss1q$sli$1@panix1.panix.com> <01c1d602$48f0bde0$550a0e3f@chottel> <a7v6aa$rjt$1@panix1.panix.com> <01c1d6c0$30561fa0$5631e641@chottel> <a80kpf$7q4$1@panix1.panix.com> <290320021919218117%josephk@iinet.net.au>`

```
Liam Devlin wrote:
...
> FWIW, I like this approach. I think you could avoid the wait messages to
> Ops if you code your program to wait in small chunks of time until you
> get the time you want, e.g., 3-5 seconds. That'll keep the job active,
> but not chew up much in the way of resources. You may need a TIME= parm
> or find out which test JOBCLASS lets your job hang around for hours.

The problem with this approach is that a JES2 initiator is tied up for
the 5 hours the job is waiting.  In some installations, initiators are a
scarce enough resource that this may delay other productive work from
running. This is probably one reason the operators have been trained to
blow away jobs that appear to be hung in an initiator.  If everyone used
this technique, batch thruput could be seriously impacted.

Joseph Katnic wrote:
> In article <a80kpf$7q4$1@panix1.panix.com>, <docdwarf@panix.com> wrote:
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
True, but JES2 does have the capability of scheduling future execution
of console commands.  Perhaps the way the "/* SCHEDULE" directive is
immplemented is via a customized JES2 exit that processes the SCHEDULE
control card at JCL conversion time, puts the job in HOLD status, and
issues the JES2 command to schedule a RELEASE of the job at the
appointed time -- conceptually not difficult, but implementation might
be another matter, and an intervening system IPL would cause the JES2
scheduled commands to be lost.  Does sound like a fairly simple but
useful extension -- will check out the "usual" places where samples of
exits may be found.

We currently handle "manually" scheduled execution of test jobs by
having the user submit the job with TYPRUN=HOLD and then fill out an
online form under ISPF to request that Operations release the job at a
specific time.  For other cases where the intent is just to run the job
outside of prime shift rather than at a specific time, the job may be
submitted at any time but in a special job class that is only processed
during off-peak hours.
```

```
than that sounds like the best answer.  You can't use a Windows
PC in a production environment, because you would not be able
to trust it.  But a Unix varient would easily be able to do this -
CRON or something similar.
```

```
In article <trPr8.8409$Rw2.724810@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>,
Russell Styles <RWSTYLES@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
>    Can a Unix or Linux PC be made to submit a job?  If yes,
>than that sounds like the best answer.  You can't use a Windows
>PC in a production environment, because you would not be able
>to trust it.  But a Unix varient would easily be able to do this -
>CRON or something similar.

Hmmmmm... *that* would be a laugh!  In my case the WAIT is needed only for 
testing; Prod is handled by a CA7 Scheduler.  I can imagine using my old 
copy of MicroFocus COBOL v1.7 to write a program that just 'hangs 
around'... hell, on a PC who cares how many CPU cycles I burn?... and then 
kick off a .BAT file which invokes ftp and establishes a session to the 
mainframe so that it can submit a job to the INTRDR at the appropriate 
time...

... hoo-wee, talk about reaching over the top of one's head with the left 
hand in order to scratch the right ear!

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 'Sleeping' Jobs - Pausing w/ ILBOWAT0

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-04-08T05:50:59-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a8rp63$o4p$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

YukonMama <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote:
>>From: docdwarf@panix.com
>>Date: 4/7/02 3:49 AM Eastern Daylight Time
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>submitted. I guess they don't have that available where you're at.

Quite the reverse, actually... what has happened is that my test job - 
with non-standard dataset names and a NOTIFY=MYUID still in the jobcard - 
has been put into the PROD.JCLLIB and is scheduled through CA7.

Granted that this is treating a test job just like a Prod one... but to do 
otherwise would, of course, take Work and violate Rule #0.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 'Sleeping' Jobs - Pausing w/ ILBOWAT0

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-04-07T03:44:11-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a8otcb$8bn$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<a7ss1q$sli$1@panix1.panix.com> <a80kpf$7q4$1@panix1.panix.com> <290320021919218117%josephk@iinet.net.au> <3CAE9FDC.DC040E1C@acm.org>`

```
In article <3CAE9FDC.DC040E1C@acm.org>, Joel C. Ewing <jcewing@acm.org> wrote:
>Liam Devlin wrote:
>...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>running.

As my questions about the batch job to submit CEMT (and other) commands 
have shown I'm not the most knowledgeable of system-jockies... this may be 
why I code apps.  Nevertheless... I wonder, given that cost-structures 
have changed since twin, slaved 370s were State O' The Art, whether this 
consideration exists out of anything other than Tradition.

>This is probably one reason the operators have been trained to
>blow away jobs that appear to be hung in an initiator.

There were Good Reasons why programmers were trained to use binary fields 
for computation and packed-decimal for storage... but the days of a 
buck-a-byte are over.

>If everyone used
>this technique, batch thruput could be seriously impacted.

If everyone flushed the toilet at once the town's septic system would be 
damaged, too... should one advocate a return to outdoor plumbing?  The 
calling of ILBOWAT seems to be a technique which, at times, would be of 
value and convenience, especially given that the cost-structure of today's 
installations value human time over machine time.  If threads are so dear 
that they need to be that jealously guarded then perhaps the shop is 
running so 'close to the bone' that things need to be re-evaluated.

Then again... re-evaluating things takes Work, and Rule #0 is... oh, you 
know the song.

DD
```

```
I am developing an application with AcuGT 5.2.0 and trying to use
standard Microsoftw ActiveX controls, such as DTPicker. The interface
seems to works well in a standard Windows environment, but when I try to
implement using AcuCorp's Thin Client, using a RS/6000 running AIX 4.3
as the server, I often have garbage returned via my INQUIRE statements.

Anyone else encountering these kind of problems? Any suggestions?
```

<a id="t-6a40bd7768"></a>
## Thread: RM-85 Cobol — 5 msgs

### RM-85 Cobol

- **From:** "Ken Benson" <ken_no0spam_@bkenbenson.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-02T04:58:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BTaq8.177167$uA5.147030@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net>`

```
Help.

We have a potential customer ... but we need to gain access to his cobol
data files.

He has given us a complete copy of his hard drive .. containing all the
programs and data files ... he runs this software package on a SCO Unix
3.2v4.2 system.  We loaded this stuff on a SCO OpenServer machine and are
able to access most of the data.  However, when accessing some of the files,
we get an error - invalid message file version, then a file error 37,07.

Apparently, this error is caused by the message file and the runtime binary
being of a different version .. .or some such ... we get version 6.09 as the
version of runcobol ... but (as far as I can tell) there is no way to obtain
the version level of the message file.

Does anyone have any ideas?
```

#### ↳ Re: RM-85 Cobol

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-02T16:29:45+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a1jjauk2roegr9r0k0tf2r14h1mumik6fm@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<BTaq8.177167$uA5.147030@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net>`

```
On Tue, 02 Apr 2002 04:58:09 GMT, "Ken Benson"
<ken_no0spam_@bkenbenson.com> wrote:

>Help.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>we get an error - invalid message file version, then a file error 37,07.
37/07 is  "no access to file" (file permissions).

As for the message file it should not be the cause of the error as
long as you can access it (even if the version is incorrect).

Use "strings runcobol" and "strings RUN.MSG" and compare the versions
that will show (you may need to redirect the output to a file).

For you have said I think you are using the runtime that the
customer has on his machine (and therefore on the hard disk copy (it
is a full HD copy is it?)).

Also note that you may have more than one "runcobol" or "RUN.MSG" on
the system and the wrong "RUN.MSG" may be being used. (do a find to
to see if this is the case).

If you still have error I may be of some help in determining further
problems (for free normally) or in doing the conversion (and getting
paid for it).

>
>Apparently, this error is caused by the message file and the runtime binary
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
See above


Frederico Fonseca
Memosis
Liant distributor and Technical support in Portugal
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs RPG

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2002-04-02T08:29:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CA96BD5.59866DE8@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2a5e3706.0203201916.4cf775a2@posting.google.com> <3CA7D081.8966D4BC@ix.netcom.com>`

```
Steve Thompson wrote:
> 
> Samlai wrote:
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> RPGIII.

When I worked for IBM and went thru their programing course, they
explained that RPG (the original one, 1966-67) emulated the punched card
equipment, so it had a built-in cycle like a 402/403. I don't know what
it looks like now, but originally it had no procedural code at all, just
what to do at various levels (like the description above).
```

## Thread: interfacing mfcobol isam to PERL — 1 this month / 3 total

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

_Thread started [2002-03](2002-03.md#t-7f572ea08b)._

#### ↳ Re: interfacing mfcobol isam to PERL

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-02T16:41:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9blq8.6718$GT2.2158193800@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<428ac1d0.0203291715.7d7562e8@posting.google.com>`

```

"mark edwards" <mark_f_edwards@excite.com> wrote in message
news:428ac1d0.0203291715.7d7562e8@posting.google.com...
> hello all -
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> more using perl.

Mark,

Another possibility is to use an ODBC driver for the Micro Focus COBOL files
and use the Perl ODBC library.  This is quite easy on Windows; on Unix it
would require downloading/installing iODBC.  While I haven't tested this
solution with Perl, I have used PHP with success, so it should work with
Perl as well.

Look here for information on the Relativity ODBC middleware for Micro Focus
(which understands all the file formats):
http://www.liant.com/products/relativity/

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

## Thread: FROM LEARNING PROBLEMS TO FAMILY CRISES. — 1 this month / 3 total

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

_Thread started [2002-03](2002-03.md#t-addf2f3527)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: FROM LEARNING PROBLEMS TO FAMILY CRISES.

- **From:** maross@texoma.net (Mike)
- **Date:** 2002-04-02T09:41:37-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2763b81a.0204020941.58108750@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<f7b1078d.0203311603.127726e4@posting.google.com> <928495c6.0203312114.6623867b@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Eva,
   Stephen is correct, you must absolutely have him checked out by a
MD as they can rule out the organic physical problems. You may need to
have a look into yourself as well, and should get the father and a
Psychiatrist (a "MD NOT a psychologist") to evaluate the situation.
Children should never be in your description as "a frightful headache"
which makes be believe your Childs problems may also be your own
perception of what he should be. If he can read at all he is doing
better that 90% of the people in the last 2 centuries have done. In a
normal bell curve children, like adults in their professions or trades
will have individuals that excel with knowledge in a company or
organization that needs them all and should threat them all with
respect.
My brother and his wife whom are well-educated, high-strung people are
faced with massive medical bills from their 12 y/o daughter that has
attempted suicide 6 times. She is on high dose anti-psychotics and
will probably face terrible drug related side effects,
hospitalizations and eventual suicidal success. This is their only
child and knowing my brother's personality I always felt they would
apply his own obsessive-compulsive disorder onto his child and as it
turned out my fears were exceeded. I am sure this story does not apply
to you, however your tone alarmed me and I felt compelled to say
something.
     Mike
```

<a id="t-cabf4caabe"></a>
## Thread: No wonder IBM COBOL programmers feel "left behind" — 2 msgs

### No wonder IBM COBOL programmers feel "left behind"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-02T12:29:18-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a8ct9v$ipe$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
From IBM's announcement 202-068 (today) at:

    http://www.ibmlink.ibm.com/usalets&parms=H_202-068

"VisualAge Enterprise Suite V4 is a bundle of Windows NT/Windows 2000
application development software. Use it to help make the transition from
traditional tooling, such as COBOL, to modern application development tools,
such as Java and WebSphere."
```

```
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

to respond to a question with "even my wife understood......".

hurt.........until I check my bank balance!
```

## Thread: Acucobol Thin Client — 2 this month / 7 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

_Thread started [2002-01](2002-01.md#t-192fc58f19)._

#### ↳ Re: Acucobol Thin Client

- **From:** Ken Webber <webberk@addsys.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-02T19:15:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3caa0367$1@usenetgateway.com>`
- **References:** `<c692d8f3.0201230204.2319140d@posting.google.com>`

```
Andrew, Did you ever get full resolution to your AcuThin problems? We
are also using the AcuThin product in Windows 2000 and Windows XP
environments. We utilize a RS/6000 for the AcuLaunch and Runtime
products. We are also encountering many instances were the application
abnormally terminates. We have been able to document some of the
problems, and have received a few patches from AcuCorp to address those
problems. However, we still have several outstanding problems with
Acucorp Support, and are being told they will not be fixed until the
next release (5.2.1) which has not yet gone into Beta Testing, and is
scheduled for release the last half of 2002.

Did you ever test the products by Flexus? Did they offer any benefit or
cause other problems?

Thanks, Ken Webber
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Acucobol Thin Client

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2002-04-03T14:13:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cab0b4b.2436309@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<c692d8f3.0201230204.2319140d@posting.google.com> <3caa0367$1@usenetgateway.com>`

```
Ken Webber <webberk@addsys.com> wrote:

>Andrew, Did you ever get full resolution to your AcuThin problems? We
>are also using the AcuThin product in Windows 2000 and Windows XP
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>cause other problems?

Hi Ken:

Our Thin Client has been through over 5 years of testing and
production use in the field.  In addition, our Thin Client is 100%
COBOL compiler independent, so you don't get locked into any one COBOL
compiler.

Our thin client product offers:

- Win32 interface or Web Browser based client.
- Automated version control and automated client component
installation.
- download on demand feature which downloads client side components
only as they are needed.
- data compression feature.
- panel (GUI Screen) definitions contained in separate external files
or within the actual source code.
- launching of remote executables on client or server side.
- copy file feature which allows files to be transferred from client
to server or from server to client.
- http tunneling.

In addition, our thin client supports our FormPrint product which also
allows remote printing from the host.

Our server support includes:

- Win32
- UNIX and UNIX derivatives (including OS/390, Linux, etc.)
- VMS and Open VMS

You would need to convert your screen handling code over to the 100%
compiler independent COBOL sp2 GUI, so there would certainly be some
work involved in switching over, but we believe that the benefits of
COBOL compiler independence outweigh the disadvantages of having to
convert the code.

If you have detailed questions, please let me know.  I'll be happy to
help.



Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

<a id="t-b2c5b05ca9"></a>
## Thread: Off-topic: Rephrasing "employment terminated"? — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### Re: Off-topic: Rephrasing "employment terminated"?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-04-04T01:09:08+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cab0062_4@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<a7o5d1$4jga$1@mercury.vcu.edu> <zu6o8.102194$2q2.8621038@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <n49o8.41046$J53.1272791@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <a7t6kk$3dkq$1@mercury.vcu.edu>`

```
Anybody (contractor or permanent employee) who allows a "standard contact" to remove his/her intellectual property rights is an idiot.

By signing this agreement you are allowing someone else to prevent you from implementing whatever innovative solution you came up with, anywhere else, but even worse, you are assigning all your rights so they can make money from your idea. I cross this clause out of any contracts I am presented with that contain it, and when they protest "But it's a standard contract, everybody signs it..." I say, "This is my standard response to people who try and curtail my rights..." So far, I have never had an Agency withdraw the job offer. (If they did, I'd go down the road and get a job somewhere else...you should too.)

So much for "intellectual property"...My intellect remains my property. I don't mind sharing it with you, but there's no way I'll assign it to you <G>.

"To secure a position with better educational advancement"  is simply bullshit, and, as such is best avoided.

As Michael already pointed out, what happened 13 years ago, especially if it was beyond your control, is really irrelevant. If you had a track record which showed you have been fired from EVERY job you ever did, there might be cause for concern (personally, I'd still employ you if you passed my very stringent interview <G>). The fact is that how you come across at interview is 90% of whether you get the job or not.

Things to remember when going for an interview:

1. Don't chew gum while being interviewed.
2. Wear shoes.
3. Wash your hair. (especially if it is long...)
4. Make sure you know how to spell COBOL, even if you can't write it.
5. Look vacant...this makes people think you are intelligent. Running through multiplication tables while the Interviewer outlines the job requirements is quite effective (much the same way as some women do while having sex...ah, you've met my girlfriend...)
6. Fake enthusiasm. Pretend you are really interested in the fact that their overnight Batch run takes 7 hours and they think there is a Sort that needs attention... (Faking enthusiasm is a lot like faking an orgasm...see "When Harry Met Sally").
7. Wear pants. (Even, or especially, if you are female and wear a skirt...)
8. When they ask you about your hobbies don't tell them about the mud wrestling and cross dressing...say you like bungee jumping and hang gliding (it takes guts to write JCL...a job for REAL men...)
9. Never appear desperate.The image to promote is nonchalance and the fact that if they are so stupid they can't see what an asset you could be, there are a dozen firms out there who can...
10. Try and fit the following words into the interview somewhere...it really doesn't matter whether they make sense or not; (probably better if they don't)..: Enterprise, Network, Web Enablement, integrated, compiler entropy, cross-politicization, functional, asynchronization, distribution, mainframe deprecation. (Any word with a "z" is very useful, even "zoo", as in "programmer zoo".)

Good Luck!

Pete.
<bgwillia@vcu.edu> wrote in message news:a7t6kk$3dkq$1@mercury.vcu.edu...

  Ouch!  Don't you just hate those intellectional property agreements? 

  I talked with someone at the University Career Center (not associated with Human Resources) and he suggest replacing "employment terminated" with "To secure a position with better educational advancement."  The hang-up is the word "terminated" that'll send up a red flag with hiring managers.  Do y'all think the phrase "To secure a position with better educational advancement" is better or would sound too "off" in some way when a hiring manager reads it? 

  Boyce Williams
```

#### ↳ Re: Off-topic: Rephrasing "employment terminated"?

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-04-03T08:39:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a8f0m1$kaq$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<a7o5d1$4jga$1@mercury.vcu.edu> <n49o8.41046$J53.1272791@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <a7t6kk$3dkq$1@mercury.vcu.edu> <3cab0062_4@Usenet.com>`

```
In article <3cab0062_4@Usenet.com>,
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snippimente]

>I cross this clause out of any contracts =
>I am presented with that contain it, and when they protest "But it's a =
>standard contract, everybody signs it..." I say, "This is my standard =
>response to people who try and curtail my rights..."

Similarly, when I run into a foolishness that someone attempts to justify

Standard Policy not to be unnecessarily constrained by mere Standard 
Policies.'

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Off-topic: Rephrasing "employment terminated"?

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-04-03T15:04:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a8f5e3$23d$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<a7o5d1$4jga$1@mercury.vcu.edu> <zu6o8.102194$2q2.8621038@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <n49o8.41046$J53.1272791@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <a7t6kk$3dkq$1@mercury.vcu.edu> <3cab0062_4@Usenet.com>`

```

On  3-Apr-2002, "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:

> Anybody (contractor or permanent employee) who allows a "standard contact"
> to remove his/her intellectual property rights is an idiot.

Or has hungry children.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Off-topic: Rephrasing "employment terminated"?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-04-05T10:07:57+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cacd17e_10@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<a7o5d1$4jga$1@mercury.vcu.edu> <zu6o8.102194$2q2.8621038@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <n49o8.41046$J53.1272791@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <a7t6kk$3dkq$1@mercury.vcu.edu> <3cab0062_4@Usenet.com> <a8f5e3$23d$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:a8f5e3$23d$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> > Anybody (contractor or permanent employee) who allows a "standard
contact"
> > to remove his/her intellectual property rights is an idiot.
>
> Or has hungry children.

No Howard.

That was the whole point of my post. Agencies intimidate contractors into
BELIEVING that unless they toe the line (like accepting outrageous
curtailment of their rights), they won't get a job. Part of this is to
exercise control, part of it is to gain competitive advantage over the
contractor.

What I am saying is that a contractual arrangement can be entered into
FAIRLY, and still be of benefit to all parties concerned. (The Client, the
Agency, and the Contractor.)

The fact that you have hungry children (if you do) is even higher motivation
for getting a better deal.

The FACT is that once you have passed the interview (and we don't get around
to signing contracts until this has happened) the Agency are extremely
unlikely to go back to the Client and say "Well, we couldn't get that guy
who had all the skills you required, and who looked like he'd fit right into
your Organization, because he wouldn't accept a restrictive standard clause
we put in all our contracts...". If , as an Employer, an Agent came to me
and said that, I'd be reviewing my list of "Approved Suppliers".... Besides,
by this point, they are already counting the money they are going to make by
placing you and they are not about to incur more expense by repeating the
exercise.

Whether you accept these arguments or not, I can assure you that in 25 years
as a contractor I have never accepted a contract I felt to be unfair or
restrictive, and I have NEVER had an Agency say "OK, no signature, no job".
If they did, I would happily walk to an Agency that was more reasonable.

There are a lot of new, young contractors, who are so thrilled to get a job
that they will accept anything. Some Agencies prey on these guys and bind
them into restrictive contracts that are really extremely unfair.

Anyway, this was a small part of my original post and I notice you have no
comment on my suggested interview rules...<G>

Why is it that people only pick up the serious stuff?

Are you guys losing your sense of humour?

(Easy for me to say that, I'm in Southern California for the next few days
and everything is so laid back I've forgotten what it's like to worry...<G>)

Pete





 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

## Thread: How to Pass parm to PRO*CoBOL program in UNIX environment — 3 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to Pass parm to PRO*CoBOL program in UNIX environment

- **From:** morant@dnr.state.wi.us (Tom Morano)
- **Date:** 2002-04-03T08:34:49-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<55066e.0204030834.366ee0d@posting.google.com>`

```
I'm converting programs from Open VMS to UNIX Compaq Tru64.  I am
having trouble figuring out how to pass parms to the programs, and
read them in the Pro*CoBOL program.

The symbols used in VMS don't work here, and I'm guessing it has to be
changed quite a bit.  I simply want to pass one, two or 3 parms to a
program. For instance I want to pass a year parm, 1984, to a variable
in Cobol, then use it in the 'select' statement in the program.

Here is what I would do in VMS:

* FIRST PARM IS THE Fiscal Year.
    MOVE 'P1'                       TO SYMBOL-NAME.
    CALL 'LIB$GET_SYMBOL'
         USING BY DESCRIPTOR  SYMBOL-NAME
               BY DESCRIPTOR  SYMBOL-EQUIVALENT
               BY REFERENCE   SYMBOL-LENGTH
               GIVING SYMBOL-RETURN-STATUS.
    IF SS$_NORMAL
        MOVE SYMBOL-EQUIVALENT                 TO PARM-DATA-AREA
        MOVE INCOMING-PARM1                    TO CLASS-TYPE-PARM
    ELSE
        DISPLAY 'SERIOUS ERROR GETTING FIRST SYMBOL'
        STOP RUN.
-And the 'SYMBOL-'  variables are defined in the Working-Storage as 01
levels.

Can someone give an example on what the cobol code would look like to
be compatible with UNIX, and how I'd run this in UNIX from the prompt?
```

#### ↳ Re: How to Pass parm to PRO*CoBOL program in UNIX environment

- **From:** Jeff Campbell <jcampbell@ins-msi.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-03T21:37:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CAB6C7A.C852B2F5@ins-msi.com>`
- **References:** `<55066e.0204030834.366ee0d@posting.google.com>`

```
Tom Morano wrote:
> 
> I'm converting programs from Open VMS to UNIX Compaq Tru64.  I am
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> prompt?

I dunno Pro*CoBOL, but Compaq COBOL for Tru64 has both environment and
command line argument passing. Check the Special-Names paragraph in
your Pro*CoBOL documentation.

A different way to read environmental variables is to use the Tru64
getenv(3) routine. I assume that unix COBOLs have a way to deal with
string pointers returned from system calls. Getenv(3) returns a pointer
to the requested variable if it exists.

Assuming that Pro*CoBOL supports ARGUMENT-NUMBER, ARGUMENT-VALUE,
or ENVIRONMENT-NAME, ENVIRONMENT-VALUE you can get the command line
with the ARG* mechanism or use the ENV* mechanism to get the values
of environment variables.

[...]
environment division.
configuration section.
special-names.
    environment-name  is symbol-name
    environment-value is symbol-value
    .

[...]
working-storage section.
01  PARM-DATA-AREA    pic x(4).

procedure division.

    display "year-param-name" upon symbol-name

    accept PARM-DATA-AREA from symbol-value
        on exception
           display "'year-param-name' not defined in environment'"
           stop run
    end-accept

[...]

The command line argument passing would look something like this

[...]
environment division.
configuration section.
special-names.
    argument-number is arg-count
    argument-value  is arg-value
    .
[...]
working-storage section.
01  counter        pic 9(9) comp.
01  cur-arg-num    pic zzz9.
01  dest-item      pic x(80).
01  num-of-args    pic 9(9) comp.

procedure division.

    accept num-of-args from arg-count end-accept

    perform varying counter from 1 by 1 until counter > num-of-args
        accept dest-item from arg-value
           on exception
              move counter to cur-arg-num
              display "Exception reading command line arguement" space
cur-arg-num

           not on exception
              move dest-item to where-ever-it-goes

        end-accept
    end-perform
[...]

From the shell prompt you would enter something like:  "./myprog arg1
arg2 arg3"

Jeff Campbell
n8wxs@arrl.net
```

```
".Gfader.Peter." wrote:

> Hallo,
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Danke Peter

From Thane Hubbell's site, "All things COBOL",  Cutenum.zip :-
--------------------------------------------------
@OPTIONS MAIN,TEST
Identification Division.
Program-Id.  Cutenum.
 By Thane Hubbell - 01/15/1999
 Compiled and Tested under Fujitsu COBOL
Environment Division.
Configuration Section.
Source-Computer.  IBM-PC.
Object-Computer.  IBM-PC.
Data Division.
Working-Storage Section.
01  The-Number            Pic 9(9) Value Zeros.
01  Text-Field            Pic X(200) Value Spaces.
01  Number-Descriptions.
    03  Table-Area-Low.
        05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "One".
        05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Two".
        05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Three".
        05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Four".
        05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Five".
        05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Six".
        05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Seven".
        05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Eight".
        05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Nine".
        05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Ten".
        05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Eleven".
        05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Twelve".
        05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Thirteen".
    03  Low-Numbers Redefines Table-Area-Low.
        05  Low-Number Pic X(8) Occurs 13 Times.
    03  Table-Area-Secondary.
        05  Filler Pic X(7) Value Spaces.
        05  Filler Pic X(7) Value "Twenty".
        05  Filler Pic X(7) Value "Thirty".
        05  Filler Pic X(7) Value "Fourty".
        05  Filler Pic X(7) Value "Fifty".
        05  Filler Pic X(7) Value "Sixty".
        05  Filler Pic X(7) Value "Seventy".
        05  Filler Pic X(7) Value "Eighty".
        05  Filler Pic X(7) Value "Ninety".
    03  Secondary-Numbers Redefines Table-Area-Secondary.
        05  Secondary-Number Pic X(7) Occurs 9 Times.
    03  Table-Area-Ante.
        05  Filler Pic X(8) Value Spaces.
        05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Million".
        05  Filler Pic X(8) Value Spaces.
        05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Thousand".
        05  Filler Pic X(8) Value Spaces.
        05  Filler Pic X(8) Value Spaces.
    03  Ante-Numbers Redefines Table-Area-Ante.
        05  Ante-Number Pic X(8) Occurs 6 Times.
01  Cntr Pic 99 Value Zeros.
01  String-Pointer Pic 99 Value 1.
01  Digits Pic 9 Value Zeros.
01  Offset Pic 9 Value Zeros.
01  Offset-Value Pic 9v9 Value Zeros.
01  Test-Num Pic 99 Value Zeros.
01  Second-Num Pic 99 Value Zeros.
01  Ante-Flag Pic X Value Spaces.
    88 Print-Ante Value "Y".
    88 No-Print-Ante Value Spaces.
Screen Section.
01  Main-Screen
    Blank Screen, Auto, Required,
    Foreground-Color Is 7,
    Background-Color Is 1.
    03  Line 1 Column 1  Value "Enter Amount".
    03  Line 3 Column 1  Value "Result ".
    03  Line 1 Column 14 Pic 9(9) Using The-Number.
    03  Line 4 Column 1  Pic X(80) From Text-Field.
Procedure Division.
Cutenum-Start.
    Display Main-Screen
    Accept Main-Screen
    Perform Varying Cntr From 1 By 1 Until Cntr > 6
      If Digits = 2
         Subtract 1 From Digits
      Else
         Add 1 To Digits
      End-If
      Move Offset-Value to Offset
      Add .5 to Offset-Value
      Compute Test-Num =
       Function Numval (The-Number (Cntr + Offset:Digits))
      Evaluate Digits Also Test-Num
         When 1 Also 1 Thru 9
           String Low-Number (Test-Num)
             Delimited By Space
             " Hundred " Delimited By Size
             Into Text-Field
             Pointer String-Pointer
           Set Print-Ante To True
         When 2 Also 1 Thru 9
           String Low-Number (Test-Num)
             Delimited by Space
             Space Delimited by Size
             Into Text-Field
             Pointer String-Pointer
           Set Print-Ante To True
         When 2 Also 10 Thru 13
           String Low-Number (Test-Num)
             Delimited By Space
             Space Delimited By Size
             Into Text-Field
             Pointer String-Pointer
           Set Print-Ante To True
         When 2 Also 14 Thru 19
             Compute Second-Num =
                Function Rem (Test-Num 10)
             String Low-Number (Second-Num)
                "teen" Delimited By Space
                Space Delimited by Size
                Into Text-Field
                Pointer String-Pointer
             Set Print-Ante To True
         When 2 Also 20 Thru 99
             Move Test-Num To Second-Num
             Move "0" To Second-Num (2:1)
             Subtract Second-Num From Test-Num
             Divide Second-Num By 10 Giving Second-Num
             String Secondary-Number (Second-Num)
                Low-Number (Test-Num)
                Delimited By Space
                Space Delimited By Size
                Into Text-Field
                Pointer String-Pointer
             Set Print-Ante To True
      End-Evaluate
      If Digits = 2 And Print-Ante
       String Ante-Number (Cntr)
        Delimited By Space
        Space Delimited By Size
        Into Text-Field
        Pointer String-Pointer
       Set No-Print-Ante To True
      End-if
    End-Perform
    Display Main-Screen
    Display Text-Field Upon Console
    Stop Run.
---------------------------------------------------------------------
```

#### ↳ Re: Zahlen in Worte konvertieren (DEUTSCH)

- **From:** "Ralph Leiherr" <leiherr@vwda.de>
- **Date:** 2002-04-04T15:39:54+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a8hl38$omc$03$1@news.t-online.com>`
- **References:** `<a8ff30$fjl$1@serv1.albacom.net>`

```
".Gfader.Peter." <e9826521@stud3.tuwien.ac.at> schrieb im Newsbeitrag
news:a8ff30$fjl$1@serv1.albacom.net...
> Hallo,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hallo Peter,

hier ein Musterprogramm wie so etwas funktioniert. Ist in der deutschen
Sprache wirklich nicht ganz einfach, das muster mit den englischen werten
kannste vergessen.

Gruï¿½

Ralph Leiherr
leitender IT-Manager


      ******************************************************************
      * Konvertieren Zahlen in Texte
      * Copyright Versorgungswerk der Architektenkammer Ba.-Wue. 2002
      ******************************************************************
      * Autor: Ralph Leiherr
      ******************************************************************
      * Versorgungswerk der Architektenkammer Baden-Wuerttemberg
      * Stuttgart
      * EMail   : info@vwda.de
      * Homepage: WWW.VWDA.DE
      *
      ******************************************************************
      ******************************************************************
      * Mit Hilfe dieses Programms, kann ein Wert in Buchstaben
      * ausgegeben werden (Scheck).
      *
      * Eingabe:   Konv-Ein-Wert       9(05)
      *
      * Ausgabe:   Konv-Aus-Text       X(200)
      *
      * Aufruf:    Perform Konv-Wert-Text.
      ******************************************************************
       Program-ID. KonvWert.

       Working-Storage Section.
       77  Konv-Ein-Wert               Pic 9(06)   Value   1.
       77  Konv-Aus-Text               Pic X(200)  Value   Space.
      *--
       01  Konv-Work-Felder.
           02  Konv-Ind                Pic 9(02)   Value   Zero.
           02  Konv-Ind-Ein            Pic 9(01)   Value   Zero.
           02  Konv-Ind-Zehn           Pic 9(01)   Value   Zero.
           02  Konv-Ind-Hundert        Pic 9(01)   Value   Zero.
           02  Konv-Ind-Tausend        Pic 9(01)   Value   Zero.
           02  Konv-Ind-ZehnTsd        Pic 9(01)   Value   Zero.
           02  Konv-Ind-HundertTSD     Pic 9(01)   Value   Zero.
      *--
           02  Konv-Zwi-Txt-Zehn       Pic X(50)   Value   Space.
           02  Konv-Zwi-Txt-Hundert    Pic X(50)   Value   Space.
           02  Konv-Zwi-Txt-Tausend    Pic X(50)   Value   Space.
           02  Konv-Zwi-Txt-HundertTSD Pic X(50)   Value   Space.
      *--
       01  Konst-Tabelle.
           02  Konst-TB-Ein.
               04  Filler              Pic X(10)   Value   "ein".
               04  Filler              Pic X(10)   Value   "zwei".
               04  Filler              Pic X(10)   Value   "drei".
               04  Filler              Pic X(10)   Value   "vier".
               04  Filler              Pic X(10)   Value   "fï¿½nf".
               04  Filler              Pic X(10)   Value   "sechs".
               04  Filler              Pic X(10)   Value   "sieben".
               04  Filler              Pic X(10)   Value   "acht".
               04  Filler              Pic X(10)   Value   "neun".
               04  Filler              Pic X(10)   Value   "zehn".
               04  Filler              Pic X(10)   Value   "elf".
               04  Filler              Pic X(10)   Value   "zwï¿½lf".
           02  Konst-TB-Ein-Red      Redefines Konst-TB-Ein
                                       Pic X(10),  Occurs 12 Times.
      *---
           02  Konst-TB-Zehn.
               04  Filler              Pic X(10)   Value   "zehn".
               04  Filler              Pic X(10)   Value   "zwanzig".
               04  Filler              Pic X(10)   Value   "dreisig".
               04  Filler              Pic X(10)   Value   "vierzig".
               04  Filler              Pic X(10)   Value   "fï¿½nfzig".
               04  Filler              Pic X(10)   Value   "sechzig".
               04  Filler              Pic X(10)   Value   "siebzig".
               04  Filler              Pic X(10)   Value   "achtzig".
               04  Filler              Pic X(10)   Value   "neunzig".
           02  Konst-TB-Zehn-Red     Redefines Konst-TB-Zehn
                                       Pic X(10),  Occurs 09 Times.
      *---
           02  Konst-TB-und            Pic X(03)   Value   "und".
           02  Konst-TB-hundert        Pic X(07)   Value   "hundert".
           02  Konst-TB-tausend        Pic X(07)   Value   "tausend".
      *---
       Procedure Division.
       Main Section.
       1.
           Perform Until Konv-Ein-Wert not numeric or
                         Konv-Ein-Wert = Zero
      *--
               Display "Eingabe Wert (z.Bsp. 12345)"
               Accept Konv-Ein-Wert
               Perform Konv-Wert-Text
               If  Konv-Aus-Text not = Space
                   Display Konv-Aus-Text
               End-If
      *--
           End-Perform.
      *---
           Stop Run.
       9.
           Exit.
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
       Konv-Wert-Text Section.
       1.
           Initialize Konv-Work-Felder.
           Move Space          to Konv-Aus-Text.
           Move Konv-Ein-Wert  to Konv-Ind.
      *----
           Move Konv-Ind       to Konv-Ind-Ein.
           Divide 10     into Konv-Ein-Wert giving Konv-Ind-Zehn.
           Divide 100    into Konv-Ein-Wert giving Konv-Ind-Hundert.
           Divide 1000   into Konv-Ein-Wert giving Konv-Ind-Tausend.
           Divide 10000  into Konv-Ein-Wert giving Konv-Ind-Zehntsd.
           Divide 100000 into Konv-Ein-Wert giving Konv-Ind-HundertTSD.
      *----
           If  Konv-Ein-Wert not numeric or Konv-Ein-Wert = Zero
               Go to 9
           End-If.
      *----
           Perform Konv-Wert-Teil-Zehner.
           Perform Konv-Wert-Teil-Hundert.
           Perform Konv-Wert-Teil-Tausend.
           Perform Konv-Wert-Teil-HundertTSD.
      *----
           String  Konv-Zwi-TXT-HundertTSD, Konv-Zwi-TXT-Tausend,
                   Konv-Zwi-TXT-Hundert,    Konv-Zwi-TXT-Zehn
                  Delimited by Space into Konv-Aus-Text
           End-String.
      *----
       9.
           Exit.
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
       Konv-Wert-Teil-Zehner Section.
       1.
           If  Konv-Ind = Zero
               Go to 9
           End-If.
      *---
           If  Konv-Ind < 13
               Move Konst-TB-Ein-Red (Konv-Ind)
                                       to Konv-Zwi-TXT-Zehn
               Go to 9
           End-If.
      *----
           If  Konv-Ind > 12 and < 20
               String Konst-TB-Ein-Red  (Konv-Ind-Ein),
                      Konst-TB-Zehn-Red (Konv-Ind-Zehn)
                   delimited by " " into Konv-Zwi-TXT-Zehn
               End-String
           End-If.
      *--
           If  Konv-Ind > 19
               If  Konv-Ind-Ein not = Zero
                   String Konst-TB-Ein-Red  (Konv-Ind-Ein),
                          Konst-TB-und,
                          Konst-TB-Zehn-Red (Konv-Ind-Zehn)
                       delimited by " " into Konv-Zwi-TXT-Zehn
                   End-String
               else
                   Move Konst-TB-Zehn-Red (Konv-Ind-Zehn)
                                          to Konv-Zwi-TXT-Zehn
               End-If
           End-If.
       9.
           Exit.
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
       Konv-Wert-Teil-Hundert Section.
       1.
           If  Konv-Ind-Hundert not = Zero
               String Konst-TB-Ein-Red (Konv-Ind-Hundert)
                           Konst-TB-hundert
                       delimited by " " into Konv-Zwi-TXT-Hundert
               End-String
           End-If.
       9.
           Exit.
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
       Konv-Wert-Teil-Tausend Section.
       1.
      *---
           Divide 1000  into Konv-Ein-Wert giving Konv-Ind.
      *---
           If  Konv-Ind = Zero
               Go to 9
           End-If.
      *---
           If  Konv-Ind < 13
               String Konst-TB-Ein-Red (Konv-Ind)
                      Konst-TB-Tausend
                   delimited by " " into Konv-Zwi-TXT-Tausend
               Go to 9
           End-If.
      *----
           If  Konv-Ind > 12 and < 20
               String Konst-TB-Ein-Red  (Konv-Ind-Tausend),
                      Konst-TB-Zehn-Red (Konv-Ind-ZehnTSD)
                      Konst-TB-Tausend
                   delimited by " " into Konv-Zwi-TXT-Tausend
               End-String
           End-If.
      *--
           If  Konv-Ind > 19
               If  Konv-Ind-Tausend not = Zero
                   String Konst-TB-Ein-Red  (Konv-Ind-Tausend),
                          Konst-TB-und,
                          Konst-TB-Zehn-Red (Konv-Ind-ZehnTSD)
                      Konst-TB-Tausend
                       delimited by " " into Konv-Zwi-TXT-Tausend
                   End-String
               else
                   String Konst-TB-Zehn-Red (Konv-Ind-ZehnTSD)
                      Konst-TB-Tausend
                       delimited by " " into Konv-Zwi-TXT-Tausend
                   End-String
               End-If
           End-If.
       9.
           Exit.
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
       Konv-Wert-Teil-HundertTSD Section.
       1.
           If  Konv-Ind-HundertTSD not = Zero
               String Konst-TB-Ein-Red (Konv-Ind-HundertTSD)
                           Konst-TB-hundert
                       delimited by " " into Konv-Zwi-TXT-HundertTSD
               End-String
           End-If.
       9.
           Exit.
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
```

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-04-03T14:40:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B8D0C4D00C4507890F751990@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<b7dbb410.0203271409.4a2f358d@posting.google.com> <MGro8.5996$oz6.1720344@news20.bellglobal.com> <3CA3052A.29F72201@Azonic.co.nz> <7f64e2ff.0203280859.7e20c713@posting.google.com> <3CA4286F.7FAED6D7@Azonic.co.nz> <7f64e2ff.0203282106.389a12a0@posting.google.com> <01HW.B8CA771B0ACA16F70F75D0D0@news-east.usenetserver.com>`

```
On Fri, 29 Mar 2002 19:55:07 -0500, Edward Reid wrote
> The current Unisys incarnations of the old Burroughs systems use 
> EBCDIC, and the same zoned signs as the IBM 360 and its successors. 
> There are two major Burroughs lines which are extinct; they used the 
> same representation.

I lied. The V-Series (also known by various model numbers such as 
B2500, B3700, B4900) used a zone on the LEADING character for the sign. 
A standard transformation in converting COBOL from V-Series to 
A-Series/NX/LX (the surviving lines) was to add SIGN LEADING to all 
signed numeric items, to avoid data conversion. Which of course mucked 
up efficiency royally.

Edward Reid
```

---

#### ↳ Re: (OT) This just gets funnier and funnier

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

## Thread: AIX 4.3.3. Microfocus Server Express 2.0.11 Animator Defunct Processes — 5 msgs

### AIX 4.3.3. Microfocus Server Express 2.0.11 Animator Defunct Processes

- **From:** lryan23@yahoo.com (laud)
- **Date:** 2002-04-03T13:55:18-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.unix.aix
- **Message-ID:** `<f31722b4.0204031355.40a18e45@posting.google.com>`

```
We're trying to use MF Server Express 2.0.11 on AIX 4.3.3.  Whenever
we use the animator in the SDE and then exit from the ssh session, a
defunct process (or processes)remains on the server.

>> ps -ef | grep jafante
 jafante 11700     1  90   Dec 31      -  0:00 []
 jafante 48412     1  91   Dec 31      -  0:00 []

This causes the Kernel(gil) process to go wild on CPU usage.

Has anyone experienced this problem before?

Thanks . . . I hope.

-- Laud
```

## Thread: Subject: Table of COBOL/DFSORT data types — 4 msgs

### Subject: Table of COBOL/DFSORT data types

- **From:** Frank Yaeger <fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-03T13:57:21-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CAB7AC1.15D5F70A@vnet.ibm.com>`

```
I've added an "Ask Professor Sort" item to the DFSORT website with a
table I created (with Tom Ross's help) that shows the equivalent DFSORT
lengths and formats for various COBOL data types.  This will help you
answer questions such as "How do I specify a PIC S9(4) COMP field in a
DFSORT INCLUDE statement?".  Here's the URL:

http://www.storage.ibm.com/software/sort/mvs/professor_sort/srtmacfm.html

I hope you all find this helpful.

Also, for a list of  the new functions just added to DFSORT R14 with PTF
UQ99331, see:

http://www.storage.ibm.com/software/sort/mvs/summary_changes/srtmsocc.html#u331

and for links to all of the topics on the DFSORT website, see:

http://www.storage.ibm.com/software/sort/mvs/srtmprd.html
```

#### ↳ Re: Subject: Table of COBOL/DFSORT data types

- **From:** Frank Yaeger <fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-03T16:51:18-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CABA386.71A1D07F@vnet.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<3CAB7AC1.15D5F70A@vnet.ibm.com>`

```
Frank Yaeger wrote:

> I've added an "Ask Professor Sort" item to the DFSORT website with a
> table I created (with Tom Ross's help) that shows the equivalent DFSORT
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> I hope you all find this helpful.

William Klein pointed out that I left the COMP-1 and COMP-2 entries off the table
(thanks,William).   I somehow "lost" these entries when transposing the table for
my Web developer.  I've asked my Web Developer to add these entries to the table
(but it may take a few days).   For those of you who can't wait, COMP-1 is 4,FL and
COMP-2 is 8,FL :-)
```

```
news:3CA79289.DDFA2139@optonline.net: 

<snipped>
>> That is not really practical...
>> VB and FB files have different physical representations... even if a
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> everything for the max? 

Max would be 32760

> 
> I don't think the additional overhead would be a factor in the shops
> where I've worked.

Sure is in places where I work.  I do batch/mainframe optimizations and 
this is one of the first things to review.  Looking at app's where a single 
job could run into millions of EXCPs on a single file.  Tuning, buffers are 
critical.  Best tuning results are from going against QSAM backups of 
VSAM's, FB's against VB's, and so on.

> I need to look into the newest options, but if COBOL's permitting
> changing the filename, maybe it also allows changing some other
> parameters, as well, e.g., LRECL, DSORG? Not very hard to do with
> Assembler, maybe REXX, too.

Stated previously, the fixed attributes can't be changed.
```

```
Gerry Wolfe wrote:
> 
> Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.net> wrote in
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> VSAM's, FB's against VB's, and so on.

It wouldn't be necessary to modify *all* files, just the ones you want
to handle in this way.
 
> > I need to look into the newest options, but if COBOL's permitting
> > changing the filename, maybe it also allows changing some other
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Stated previously, the fixed attributes can't be changed.

Stated incorrectly, then. In Assembler you have access to the
SHOWCB/MODCB macros which permit you to modify the ACB to whatever you
want. A batch application I worked on a few years ago has a file handler
(Asm), which I rewrote to enable 31-bit addressing for COBOL II
programs.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<3CABEA33.7A067595@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com> <3C991FB3.EC98B300@shaw.ca> <Xns91D7D5EF97860gerrywolfesympaticoc@206.172.150.13> <3C99575F.71D83779@shaw.ca> <Xns91D8D72421978gerrywolfesympaticoc@207.35.177.135> <3C9AB725.8B19E215@shaw.ca> <a7fe9s$6lg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3C9B66FF.90509204@shaw.ca> <a7fqcn$qnf$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3C9B7C38.D0AB7E25@shaw.ca> <YzSm8.1250$se.105416@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3C9C275E.36EBEDDC@shaw.ca> <Xns91DBA3C7288EBgerrywolfesympaticoc@207.35.177.134> <3C9FE4C6.3ACD9B94@shaw.ca> <Xns91DFAC4A48430gerrywolfesympaticoc@206.172.150.13> <3CA536E0.39EAD96F@optonline.net> <Xns91E16C3934E02gerrywolfesympaticoc@207.35.177.134> <3CA79289.DDFA2139@optonline.net> <Xns91E5D1963A346gerrywolfesympaticoc@207.35.177.134>`

```
Gerry Wolfe wrote:
> 
> Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.net> wrote in
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> VSAM's, FB's against VB's, and so on.

You wouldn't have to convert all files to VB, just the ones you want to
be able to shuffle.
 
> > I need to look into the newest options, but if COBOL's permitting
> > changing the filename, maybe it also allows changing some other
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Stated previously, the fixed attributes can't be changed.

Not certain what you mean by the above, "fixed attributes can't be
changed"; but the VSAM controls blocks can be tailored to whatever the
use wants dynamically with the GENCB, SHOWCB & MODCB macros. The
following GENCB generates an RPL with the parameters determined
dynamically:

*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*        Time to generate the RPL for this file.                      *
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
	GENCB	BLK=RPL,                                               x
		AM=VSAM,                                               X
		ACB=(ACBREG),                                          X
		LOC=ANY,                                               X
		AREA=(AREAREG),                                        X
		ARG=KEY,                                               X
		AREALEN=(LENREG),                                      X
		RECLEN=(LENREG)         

	STM	R15,R0,SAVEAREA         * SAVE FOR DEBUGGING
	LTR	R15,R15                 * GENCB work?
	BNZ	GENERR                  * NO


This will generate an ACB for a DDNAME supplied by the caller:

	GENCB	BLK=ACB,                * generate ACB                  X
		AM=VSAM,                *                               X
		EXLST=EXLST,            *                               X
		MAREA=MAREA,            *                               X
		MLEN=L'MAREA,           *                               X
		LOC=BELOW,              * keep ACB's under 16-Meg line  X
		RMODE31=ALL,            * gen control blocks & buffers  X
		DDNAME=(*,FCBEXTRN)     * above 16-Meg line

	LTR	R15,R15                 * GENCB work?
	BNZ	GENERR                  * nope


The following MODCB primes the RPL for a Get with Update:

GETU	DS	0H                        
	MODCB	RPL=(RPLREG),           *                               X
		AREA=(AREAREG),         *                               X
		OPTCD=(KEY,DIR,ARD,UPD,KEQ)

	LTR	R15,R15                 
	BNZ	GENERR                  


DCB's are trivial. The DCBD macro maps the DCB and the correct values
can be moved to it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ CICS and the Web was Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-04-11T00:22:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CB4C89A.8DF0D5B6@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com> <c3f04d21.0203210559.256d67f7@posting.google.com> <3c9a5328_5@Usenet.com>`

```
Since CICS can talk to Websphere and has other Web capabilities, I
wouldn't write off CICS just yet.  They are still using transaction
monitors in the Unix environment (including CICS).  Do you know of any
high volume CICS applications that have been moved to Web servers
without using some kind of transaction monitor like CICS or Tuxedo by
Encina?  If so do you know any of the little details like the hardware
needed to support the application under CICS versus the Web version?  

I believe that Java is a good idea on the client.  I am less convinced
that it is the right solution on the server because of the interpretive
nature of the environment. When the new decimal arithmetic capabilities
are approved it actually may have better arithmetic than COBOL and if
there are JAVA compilers that produce executables (not just byte code)
then one of my objections goes away.   

In terms of fast online access reliably, see Howard Brazee's posts. 
Delivering reliability is a discipline that is hard learned for any
environment and I am have not seen the evidence that the monitoring and
failure capture tools are matured yet for many of the Web Server
environments.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services, Inc.
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
> 
> ROFL!

>                 http://www.usenet.com
```
