###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CICS and the Web was Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-04-11T11:10:12+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cb563f5_2@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com> <c3f04d21.0203210559.256d67f7@posting.google.com> <3c9a5328_5@Usenet.com> <3CB4C89A.8DF0D5B6@istar.ca>`

```
While it was not my intention to "write off" CICS in my post (I agree it
still has a useful life, and may yet transform itself successfully to the
Client/Server environment as a competitor for MTS), my post was intended to
show that CICS, in and of itself, will not keep COBOL alive. The use of the
Web is obviating the need for transaction processors altogether. Transaction
processing is implicit in the use of Web Forms, whether they are processed
by CGI code, Java Servlets, or ISAPI on the Server. It comes down to an
argument about server performance, not architecture.

Having just arrived back in the U.K. and looking at opportunities (euphemism
for "looking for a job" <G>), I did a search yesterday of a major European
IT job-finding site on the Web. Out of 4600 jobs posted, there were 8 for
COBOL. Of those 8, 2 were contract.

10 years ago, the same search on the same site would have revealed around
14,000 jobs of which at least 2000 would have been COBOL. Around 800 of
these would have been contract. These days I don't work as a COBOL
programmer and it is most likely that I'll get employed through the network
of previous contacts I have established, but I ran a COBOL filter just for
old times sake...

We have discussed at length the death of COBOL in this group; now we are
seeing the reality.

In Europe, my assessment is as follows:

COBOL is fast becoming a mainframe Batch programming language which will be
used by permanent employees who can be kept to an average salary of around
�25000. (Reporting this gives me no pleasure at all and I won't say "I told
you so" even though I did ...<G>)

The more astute COBOL people have already moved to other skills (I have been
contacted socially since my return by several who I either worked with or
trained in the past, and none of them are using COBOL as their primary means
to make a living any more.) I am also in the same boat, not having made a
living from COBOL for over 10 years now.

So, why is it?

Well, my preliminary analysis does NOT reveal that COBOL has fallen victim
to the whims of fashion, as most of us might expect.

The fact is that the MARKETPLACE (which is always the ultimate driver of
technology) has changed.

More and more Corporations are questioning why they should even attempt to
maintain expensive IT development in house. More and more IT departments are
limiting their activities to the rollout of desktop technology, the
selection of packages and third party suppliers (outsourcing, if you like),
and the Web Enablement of existing systems (read "Converting to Java").
Development in the sense of "computer programming" is becoming non-existent.
Quick build tools like VB are still in use although even this is on the
retreat.

The modern Corporation is saying: "We are NOT an IT Company. We can buy the
service just like we buy any other service."

 In the past this wasn't possible. There wasn't the plethora of "Business
Solutions" which are available today (SAP, Siebel, etc.), they KNEW they had
to have IT technology to remain competitive, and the only way to ensure it
was to do it in-house.

The advent of Client/server brought component based solutions (databases
like ORACLE, INGRES, SQL Server, Server-based shared re-usable services that
can be instantly deployed wherever needed), and, of course, the promotion of
the hardware-based LAN into a Corporate IntraNet with portals to the
Internet (Global connectivity and distribution of Corporate systems).

The main reason for running a mainframe was the power of it. Now the same
throughput can be achieved with hardware at a third of the price and without
the need for an expensive technocracy of system programmers and support
infrastructure. (There is still a need for infrastructure, but it is much
less expensive.) The main reason for running a mainframe NOW, is that most
of the Company's systems are in the form of Legacy code, and HAVE to be
mainframe based! As time goes by these systems will be gradually replaced by
more easily distributed and deployed Client/Server based solutions.

There is a strong market for Datawarehousing and Legacy-leveraging tools at
the moment. However, I predict this is a limited life market (say 10 years)
which contains the seeds of its own destruction (once you have "converted"
there is no "repeat business"...).

So, for many COBOL programmers, coping with the daily problems of file
handling, return codes, printing, and all the things which make "computer
programming" the fascinating art that it is, it is easy to lose sight of the
"big picture" and see what is shaping the future. By the time we become
aware that jobs are evaporating, it is too late.

Once again, I would strongly urge ALL COBOL people to invest some time in
acquiring new skills. Right now Java is good (it also introduces exposure to
Object Orientation and, if you are determined to make a career as a
"programmer", you WILL need this knowledge) and so is some Web
familiarisation (any COBOL programmer worth their salt can teach themselves
HTML in an afternoon...it is just Word Processing).

DON'T Rely on COBOL being enough to feed and clothe you and yours in the
years to come. If you are an experienced Programmer/Analyst try and get on a
SAP course. (Whatever we may think of SAP from a technical point of view, it
is enjoying a rising wave of popularity and "Management" (who are nowhere
near as concerned with technicalities as we are) love it. Besides, as it
succeeds, many of the worst technical flaws in it will get resolved...)

EVERYBODY needs to learn RDB (SQL) and move away from the COBOL file system.
(This factor is one of the most potent in the decline of COBOL - it isolates
the language and applications developed in it.)

All of the above is one man's opinion. Many of you will disagree.

Time will tell.

Pete.

> Clark F. Morris, Jr. <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote in message
news:3CB4C89A.8DF0D5B6@istar.ca...

that

same

it;
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CICS and the Web was Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-04-11T19:02:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CB5DD3A.A4697E58@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com> <c3f04d21.0203210559.256d67f7@posting.google.com> <3c9a5328_5@Usenet.com> <3CB4C89A.8DF0D5B6@istar.ca> <3cb563f5_2@Usenet.com>`

```


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

<below>

Isn't life ironic, (and yes, I'm still depressed !). Yesterday I wrote to
somebody querying OO Developer input. PLONK ! I got his reply before reading
your message below.

While there was an initial surge to learn (PC) OO COBOL at US institutions some
three years ago, that's not the case now; interest has dropped off. And it was
at the J4 meeting at Newbury in May 2000, that a professor from Leicester
University took back a dozen copies of Net Express University Edition, "just in
case somebody was interested in OO" - tho' U of L doesn't teach COBOL !

Well my correspondent's message was shorter than yours, but I think he made me
even more depressed than when I read your message. In essence he sees COBOLers
dabbling in non-OO and using VB and Java to get at the Web, perhaps writing some
COBOL support classes to interface..

This is not the time to engage in "rub their noses in the xxxx ....", but I
can't help but reflect that J4 missed the boat  - had they gone *firm* around
'96 with what was then touted as COBOL '97, (aka COBOL 2002), it might have
stood a chance.
I know for sure that Net Express users are embracing Java, even more so because
N/E has Java support classes. In some instances, (I think Simon is one), more
work is being switched to Java, depending upon the installation's philosophy.

I shudder at the advertising stats in the UK - no 'ifs, buts or maybes' - that
tells you what is happening. I can't recall what it was Bill wrote about, but he
did point out that UK COBOL users were inclined to use particular COBOL
techniques that differed from their US counterparts. Perhaps 'la difference'
applies to the whole of the EU.

You mentioned SAP below. Ever heard of SYNON ? A Web search reveals a good list.
(Don has brushed shoulders with SAP, I wonder if he has heard of SYNON back East
?). I took somebody to hospital emergency on Sunday morning, and seeing me
reading Ed Arranga's "OO COBOL' - another 'taxi driver' in the waiting room,
quizzed me. Jeez !  Somebody who knows COBOL in Calgary ! I quickly whisked
across to the chair next to him to talk shop. He's a marketeer, not a
programmer. His software house in Calgary specializes in Transportation
applications, primarily using DB2

Seems, like SAP, SYNON is an all embracing tool. Essentially, figure out your
Database and the screens you want and it will generate the code (on AS/400)
either in COBOL or RPG. Like any generated source, there is an 'overhead', but
as he pointed out, with today's computers, speed overcomes this, and the
generated source gives you accuracy. True or not, he claims they rarely change
the source.
SYNON started in the UK, but to his knowledge there are about 300 software
houses that use it for development. (He may have said 3,000 software houses -
but we'll stick with the more conservative figure <G>).

As a side issue he mentioned HST (Harmonized Sales Tax). It's a branch off our
GST(VAT). The East Coast provinces, (the have-nots'), have the Feds collect HST,
who then give provinces back a handful of money. Saves them the admin machine
of collecting both GST and PST(Provincial). He quoted one company on the East
Coast that took six weeks to implement HST. By comparison, using SYNON they did
it in two days.

Certainly there is the move to outsource, like SAP, SYNON etc. Can't remember
the company name, but there's a Canadian outfit which it was announced this
week, has entirely outsourced their EDP to IBM ! (For the moment, IBM has taken
on their complete staff - give it time and start doing some head counting.....).

Now the real kicker. You indirectly paint a picture of 'doom and gloom', though
you probably prefer I had written 'a reality check'. OK mainframers, let them
dabble in CICS, LE, SYNCSORT, VSAM etc... and all the other goodies associated
with batch processing. Coupled with the O/S they use an add-on called COBOL,
which in no way represents how we use it on 'desktops'.

Perhaps many of the oldies are just sweating it out, hoping the pink slip
doesn't materialize before their retirement date. Sure, installations will get
involved in Java, but inevitably it will be a 'Java team', composed of the
young, bright-eyed and bushy-tailed. Oldies can beat the pink slip somewhat, by
learning Java, but I'm sure the tendency will be to use them as 'maintainers'
while the nifty stuff in Java gets done by youngsters.

So Pete Dashwood hasn't been using COBOL for 10 years - what the hell has he
been programming in ? Meanwhile, and more important to me, do I continue to use
OO COBOL which is based on an as yet, incomplete Standard ? I've enjoyed it
thoroughly, and I'm not sure I would have got the hang of OO using other PC
languages. (Meanwhile of course I've recently been learning some SQL - that
doesn't hurt).

So. In terms of the future, COBOL seems like doggedly sticking with VCR Beta
tapes when the marketplace has already switched to VHS. (Bugger it ! And now
I'll have to buy a new box for DVDs !). Do I bite the bullet ? Drop COBOL
completely and switch to Java ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB

> still has a useful life, and may yet transform itself successfully to the
```

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-04-12T11:23:38+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cb770bc_5@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com> <c3f04d21.0203210559.256d67f7@posting.google.com> <3c9a5328_5@Usenet.com> <3CB4C89A.8DF0D5B6@istar.ca> <3cb563f5_2@Usenet.com> <3CB5DD3A.A4697E58@shaw.ca>`

```
Jimmy,

I think you are letting your depression affect your perception.

Try re-reading what I wrote. It is NOT a post full of doom and gloom. It is
a statement of the way I perceive the European market and a brief analysis
of what I believe is leading to the "Death of COBOL".

I posted it, not to depress Jimmy Gavan, but because I am genuinely
concerned about young people faced with career decisions and not sure which
way to go. It would be very wrong to advise new people seeking a career in
technology to put all their eggs in the COBOL basket. (Even, or especially,
the OO COBOL basket...)

I'm NOT syaing "Give up on COBOL; don't bother learning it...". I'm simply
saying (to newcomers and existing people alike) "Expand your skill set."
This is a totally consistent position as I have been saying it for the last
8 years in public forums and articles. The difference is that, after looking
at the European market for skill, I have realized that it is becoming much
more critical for COBOL.

The Marketplace that originated COBOL is no longer there. Businesses are
more IT savvy and have more options than they did.

The figures (whether we like them or not) cannot be argued with.

COBOL use IS declining. Severely. (See my previous post).

This is NOT because of an inherent lack in COBOL, it is because the
Marketplace has changed. The FACT is that Corporate IT development is dying.
The days of "do-it-yourself" IT solutions are severely numbered.

So, assuming I am right, what effect will this have on James Gavan, software
developer?

Answer: NONE at all!

You will continue to develop in COBOL as you do now, assured that it meets
your needs and is an ideal Language for the application you earn your living
with.

Even if support was withdrawn for Net Express (extremely unlikely given the
user base) you have enough tools at your disposal at the moment to do the
job.

So, you MIGHT not see the new standard get implemented. So what? We waited
17 years and managed without it.

Where's the Doom and Gloom <G>?

I still believe COBOL is the RIGHT language for commercial component
development (and for writing CGI code for Web Servers) and I intend to
maintain that position in Corporates where I may be consulting. But this is
of interest only to software developers. The Corporates themselves don't
really care because they are moving out of software development. (They are
not now, and are unlikely in the future, to move out of software
deployment....)

Further comments on your post below...

James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3CB5DD3A.A4697E58@shaw.ca...
>
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> somebody querying OO Developer input. PLONK ! I got his reply before
reading
> your message below.
>
> While there was an initial surge to learn (PC) OO COBOL at US institutions
some
> three years ago, that's not the case now; interest has dropped off. And it
was
> at the J4 meeting at Newbury in May 2000, that a professor from Leicester
> University took back a dozen copies of Net Express University Edition,
"just in
> case somebody was interested in OO" - tho' U of L doesn't teach COBOL !
>

I am no longer surprised at anything that happends in J4 meetings. It is a
sorry history of missed opportunities, bungling, corporate boondoggles, and
total lack of accountability to anyone. If I was being kind, I'd say some
well-meaning people put in a lot of time and effort and sadly failed. As I
don't feel kind when I look at the effect this fiasco has had on COBOL, I
have to say a bunch of pompous cretins (there are one or two exceptions to
this generalization; but for the most part it reflects my honest
opinion...<G>) publicly demonstrated their incompetence and then patted each
other on the back for their non-achievement. Bitter...? Me...? <G>

The decline in OO COBOL was predictable because OO methodology was never
embraced by "Fortress COBOL". This has all been covered elsewhere.

> Well my correspondent's message was shorter than yours, but I think he
made me
> even more depressed than when I read your message. In essence he sees
COBOLers
> dabbling in non-OO and using VB and Java to get at the Web, perhaps
writing some
> COBOL support classes to interface..
>

Your correspondent is both well-informed and in touch with reality. This
(along with Batch Processing) is almost certainly the future for COBOL
programmers who have no other skills.

What I don't understand here, Jimmy, is why this depresses you. People have
a choice. They can continue their education or they can sit around with
their thumbs up their asses, convinced that what they know now is more than
adequate for the future, and the way they have always done it is the only
right way, so nothing more is required other than to occasionally "close
ranks" and ensure that anything that attacks the fortress is repulsed.(This
is what happened with OO COBOL. They couldn't be bothered to handle the
learning curve so they wrote it off as "Modular programming re-invented". We
don't want or need it. Result: ...Well, you're describing it. (Note that
this one is NOT the fault of J4 who spent a huge amount of time trying to
come to grips with OO COBOL. In this case it was the "Fortress COBOL"
mentality that destroyed it.)) In the meantime, OOD/OOP was embraced by the
World outside the Fortress so the Fortress was bypassed and isolated.
Eventually it will fall due to its own intolerance and arrogance.

> This is not the time to engage in "rub their noses in the xxxx ....", but
I
> can't help but reflect that J4 missed the boat  - had they gone *firm*
around

Let's not look for cats to whip. J4 are certainly culpable for MANY things

am working on an article that covers ALL the factors and J4 is just part of
it.

> I know for sure that Net Express users are embracing Java, even more so

There is sometimes a style difference between the COBOL used in different
countries (I have worked in most and maintained and written code in Europe,
the USA, and Australasia), but it is ALL COBOL. Style has nothing to do with
the decline of the language, as I hope you are realising by now...

<<<

I haven't personally used SYNON but it is on my list of things to learn this
year...

SAP is enjoying IMMENSE popularity in Europe (and New Zealand, oddly
enough).

I remember back in the early 80s being asked to evaluate a "new" Business
package called SAP. This was in Germany and the product was developed there.
It ran on mainframes. I worked with it with a team of people intensively for
just over 3 weeks and we produced a detailed report showing the technical
flaws and the functional problems in implementing it in our particular
organisation. It was duly rejected by the EDV (IT) department. Six months
later a new Managing Director INSISTED that it be implemented  (I suspect
the "old boy" network...<G>) and justified it by "downsizing" (read,
"massacring" <G>) the EDV department... It cost more than even we could have
forseen and the Company posted its first loss (over DM20,000,000) in its
history. (Not entirely due to SAP, but it was a major contributing factor.
All modifications to the package had to be done in BAL (mainframe assembler)
and the people to do this had to be contracted from the SAP vendor for an
hourly  rate which was equivalent to 3 days pay for most IT people...

SAP has come a long way and is now ORACLE based, and running on
Client/Server. There is a VERY good market for SAP skills in Europe and I
intend to do a refresher course in it as soon as I can.

<snipped HST stuff>

> Certainly there is the move to outsource, like SAP, SYNON etc. Can't
remember

this

taken
> on their complete staff - give it time and start doing some head
counting.....).
>

Yes, this is becoming a frequent scenario. I posted such an event in this
forum in connection with an Australian Insurance Company, many moons ago. It
is a sound business decision and you cannot blame Companies for doing it.

> Now the real kicker. You indirectly paint a picture of 'doom and gloom',
though

> OK mainframers, let them

Sorry, Jimmy, I vehemently disagree here. You are speaking about things
which you really don't understand. CICS has NOTHING to do with Batch
Processing, the mainframe Run Time and File System are simply that, and
COBOL can be and IS used across platforms. The similarities far outweigh the
differences. OK, they are not running GUI COBOL on the mainframe (yet) but
Standard COBOL is Standard COBOL  and the PC is an excellent mainframe
development environment. COBOL is far from being an "add-on" to the
mainframe environment. I believe you may have been bewildered by some of the
posts in this forum...


> Perhaps many of the oldies are just sweating it out, hoping the pink slip

and bushy-tailed" in order to learn Java (or anything else). I am living
proof that even "old farts" can do so if their attitude is right.

ANYBODY (irrespective of their age) who is "sweating it out" in their IT job
should NOT be there.

is minimal anyway - it is an OO Language) is shared equally by all.

> So Pete Dashwood hasn't been using COBOL for 10 years - what the hell has
he

>

Of course! Why would you discontinue using something that works perfectly
well for you? That would be stupid, and you're not.


> So. In terms of the future, COBOL seems like doggedly sticking with VCR
Beta
> tapes when the marketplace has already switched to VHS. (Bugger it ! And
now
> I'll have to buy a new box for DVDs !). Do I bite the bullet ? Drop COBOL
> completely and switch to Java ?
>
The tape format is a tempting analogy, but it is not apposite in this case.
It would be more correct to say that the whole marketplace for VCR tape has
been replaced by on-demand video...<G>

Said it before, I'll say it again... Java is NOT a replacement for COBOL. It
is a complement to it. You would be INSANE to try and convert all your
exisiting code to Java. (Products like per-COBOL will do it for you, but the
jury is still out on how WELL they do it for you... Having achieved a
"generated Java" base, how do you maintain it?) The core business processing
of your application is definitely best performed in COBOL. (This is true at
the corporate level also, it's just that most Companies are simply
leveraging their Legacy applications and Web Enabling them with Java.)

I honestly hope that the above will help to lighten some of your depression,
Jimmy.

There is no point in getting emotional about a Computer Programming
Language. It had its day, it served us well. The reasons for its decline are
manifold. It certainly didn't happen without warning...

And just as some people choose to drive a Classic car to get them from A to
B, so some individuals (me for one...<G>) will continue to use COBOL for
solving business problems in computer programming. The rest of the world can
whizz by in their cars, buses, helicopters, whatever... I like the drive.

<snipped all my previous post - see above in this thread if you want to
check it out>

Pete.




 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CICS and the Web was Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-13T01:21:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wKLt8.47283$wt5.1882241@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com> <c3f04d21.0203210559.256d67f7@posting.google.com> <3c9a5328_5@Usenet.com> <3CB4C89A.8DF0D5B6@istar.ca> <3cb563f5_2@Usenet.com> <3CB5DD3A.A4697E58@shaw.ca> <3cb770bc_5@Usenet.com>`

```
Oh, that real answer to that question is pretty simple. They need to learn COBOL.

And Java
And Basic
And SQL
And C
And Perl 
And HTML
And UNIX
And ....
[ 20 or 30 lines later... ]
And PalmOS. 

Obviously not a complete list. 

This sounds flippant on the surface, and I hope I did inject at least a little humor into
it, because it is a very unfunny truth. There are days when I LONG for the simple times
when all I had to know was 1 language and one system. At least, one at a time. 
Today, my greatest frustration, as well as my greatest value, is that I know and can
determine / decide what tool best fits an project. It is frustrating because often I 
need to do something (for example, loading a darn VSAM file) that I haven't had to 
do in say, 2 years, and my notes are not complete. And I have to switch between 
systems several times a day. 

My current schedule has me switching between:
  Wang Assembler (basically 3270 Assembler) 
  Cobol ReSource running under AIX
  An emulated Wang running under AIX
  PERL  CGI programs running under Apache/Linux
  Java programs that are really network servers running under Linux and Windows
  Windows 2K
   Firewall-1 & Cisco routers
  Citrix under Windows 2K with about 100 Linux workstations as clients
   OS/390 
   Obnoxious phone systems (Mitel in particular) 
   Mainframe Cobol
   JCL
   Wang Procedure Language 
   UNIX  / Linux C programs
   Mainframe Assembler
   Mainframe CICS 
   SCO Unix and AcuCOBOL 
   AS/400 RPG 
   AS/400 COBOL

For fun I like to play with MVT and MVS, VM, Vax/VMS, and ancient PDP-11 
code on PC simulators. I also have a System-36 sitting right next to me that I 
use to support a food bank organization. I won't even begin to tell you what my 
garage looks like... 

I certainly hit all of those at least twice in a week, and often most of them on the same day. I bet
a lot of people reading this group are in similar situations to. 

It *is* fun, and it really helps with the employability and job security thing, but it is tiring. I really need a 
computer implant in my brain to help with the memory. A direct 'neural' interface to avoid typing 
would be nice too... <grin>  

But absolutely seriously, folks need to be able to deal with a wildy variant set of tools these days, 
and it is only going to get worse. That is what we need to be training the young folks to be able to 
do. I do see a lot of young people coming out of school today with the capability to do that. I think a 
great deal of he reason I see it at all it is the emphasis on Software Engineering that has come into 
place during the last 15 years or so. (Well, I am  a Software Engineer, despite whatever title my 
employers insist on hanging on me. :) 

And yes, COBOL will have a place in that world. It will be near impossible to get rid of 
COBOL in any case, not that any sane individual would want to! 


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:3cb770bc_5@Usenet.com...
> Jimmy,
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: Selling copies of Micro Focus (eBay, via CLC, wherever) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Selling copies of Micro Focus (eBay, via CLC, wherever)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-04T01:49:58-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a8h0j9$ng3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Given several recent (and not so recent) threads about "acquiring" copies of
Micro Focus products OTHER THAN FROM Micro Focus,  I thought that I would
pass along (in this new thread as well as in response to another), the
comment that I received "privately" from someone in the legal department at
Micro Focus.

"Micro Focus takes support and maintenance of users of Micro Focus products
as well as the protection of its Intellectual Property Rights very
seriously. In our efforts to do the preceding, Micro Focus's standard
software license does not allow licenses for Micro Focus products to be
transferred without the parties notifying Micro Focus. Hopefully, this will
allow Micro Focus to continue to provide its users with support and new
products in the future."

    ***

My personal suggestion is that anyone wanting to "sell" an existing LICENSED
(*not* purchased) copy of any Micro Focus product, read their licensing
documentation and/or get appropriate legal advice.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cc07162.0204041353.35e6c318@posting.google.com>`

```
Is there a way other than using the "WITH CONTROL IS ... " clause to
allow each individual user to use his/her personal profile to assign
colors to DISPLAYS/ACCEPTS used throughout an application?

For example:

DISPLAY "HELLO"
     AT LINE 01 COL 01
   WITH FOREGROUND-COLOR <user preference 1>
        BACKGROUND-COLOR <user preference 2>
        HIGHLIGHT
END-DISPLAY

Where <user preference x> could be picked up out of a .ini file or
some other sort of personal application initialization file when the
application starts up.

Any ideas on this are most welcome.

Thanks,
Chris
```

#### ↳ Re: SYNCSORT returns negative numbers BEFORE positive?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-04T16:46:57-06:00

```
I have no "vested interest" in either Syncsort *or* DFSORT, but the DFSORT
manuals are "online" - and there is LOTS of "useful programming tips"
available online as well.

As far as a "reference manual" goes, the 2 products TEND to be so similar,
that I would think that looking at a DFSORT manual would answer MOST
questions about Syncsort (features and syntax).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SYNCSORT returns negative numbers BEFORE positive?

- **From:** Frank Yaeger <fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-04T15:52:43-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CACE74B.EC686430@vnet.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<ebsoau0j2tfkpu1oahnt63pv46eotubl4c@4ax.com> <20020404173106.07672.00000298@mb-mp.aol.com> <a8il55$soc$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:

> I have no "vested interest" in either Syncsort *or* DFSORT, but the DFSORT
> manuals are "online" - and there is LOTS of "useful programming tips"
> available online as well.

Right.   See my other post for details.

> As far as a "reference manual" goes, the 2 products TEND to be so similar,
> that I would think that looking at a DFSORT manual would answer MOST
> questions about Syncsort (features and syntax).

That's a very iffy proposition since each product has exclusives that the other
doesn't.

Looking at the DFSORT books, you would NOT be able to tell which documented
features were also available in Syncsort . For example, you could read about the
VLTRIM=byte, TRAN=ALTSEQ, TRAN=UTOL, TRAN=LTOU, REMOVECC, FTOV, DATEn, DATEn(c),
DATEnP, TIMEn, TIMEn(c), TIMEnP, DTn, TMn, etc options in the DFSORT book and
not know they weren't available in Syncsort.

Or you could read about the DISCARD, FIRSTDUP, LASTDUP, INDENT, BETWEEN,
STATLEFT, E'pattern', etc options of DFSORT's ICETOOL and not know that they
weren't available in "SYNCTOOL".  Of course, you could read the Syncsort book
and still not know what was available in "SYNCTOOL" since it's undocumented.

Obviously, the online DFSORT books and website document what's available with
DFSORT, not with Syncsort, so you'd be going down a lot of blind alleys by
trying to use the DFSORT books to figure out how to use Syncsort.   On the other
hand, the DFSORT books and website are freely available so there's no guessing
about what's supported by DFSORT.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SYNCSORT returns negative numbers BEFORE positive?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-04-05T18:07:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a8kos6$8hq$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<ebsoau0j2tfkpu1oahnt63pv46eotubl4c@4ax.com> <20020404173106.07672.00000298@mb-mp.aol.com> <a8il55$soc$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3CACE74B.EC686430@vnet.ibm.com> <a8kden$1u6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3CADD98C.98578389@vnet.ibm.com>`

```

On  5-Apr-2002, Frank Yaeger <fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com> wrote:

> > I am in a DFSort environment but have an old Syncsort manual which I
> > sometimes use.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> The DFSORT books are out there on the Web, so why not use them?

I do use them - but manuals are much easier to use.  I have printed out
several DFSORT manuals from the web as well, but even there, they aren't
organized as one source book is.   The Web has too big advantages:
1.  It can be current.
2.  I can access it wherever I am, without carrying a manual with me.
It has a smaller advantage:
3.  I can do word searches - if I can guess the correct word to search on.

But manuals are far easier to use.

If anybody at my work but me was aware of ICETOOL, I would go that way, but
having one person use a tool that nobody else knows how to maintain is
asking for trouble.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SYNCSORT returns negative numbers BEFORE positive?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Frank Yaeger <fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-05T10:56:14-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CADF34E.8A3FEB41@vnet.ibm.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

> I do use them - but manuals are much easier to use.  I have printed out
> several DFSORT manuals from the web as well, but even there, they aren't
> organized as one source book is.

Yes, I agree that printed manuals can be easier to use, but as you say you CAN
print the DFSORT manuals.   I don't know what you mean by "they aren't
organized as one source book is".   Can you elaborate?

> If anybody at my work but me was aware of ICETOOL, I would go that way, but
> having one person use a tool that nobody else knows how to maintain is
> asking for trouble.

So why not teach others about ICETOOL?   You could print off  copies of  my
"ICETOOL Mini-User Guide" and hand them out.  You can find the PDF version at:

http://www.storage.ibm.com/software/sort/mvs/icetool/index.html
```

#### ↳ Re: SYNCSORT returns negative numbers BEFORE positive?

- **From:** Frank Yaeger <fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-04T15:39:33-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CACE435.2EC7FB1C@vnet.ibm.com>`

```
Sff5ky wrote:

> It still would have been nice to have handy for reference and
> maybe something to show to the new management what power
> is out there for Sort utility programs.

Well, since you guys brought up pubs for Sort products:

If you want to show them what power is out there for Sort utility programs, you
can do that with the DFSORT books and website!  The DFSORT books are freely
available on the web and on the z/OS and OS/390 CDs.   You can access the
DFSORT books from:

http://www.storage.ibm.com/software/sort/mvs/srtmpub.html

We even have the March, 2002 set of pubs out there reflecting the new features
for DFSORT R14 PTF UQ99331.   For a list of those new features, see:

http://www.storage.ibm.com/software/sort/mvs/summary_changes/srtmsocc.html#u331

For my paper "What's New in DFSORT R14", see:

http://www.storage.ibm.com/software/sort/mvs/release_14/index.html

Finally, for a list of all of the Topics on the DFSORT website (tricks, Q&A,
papers, etc), see:

http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/
```

```
Rufio wrote:

> I'M SORRY - I retract what I said - I was talking about Murach's MVS/ESA
> book - not anything associated with IBM.
> But, as Syncsort Inc. is a separate company, how can they dictate terms, to
> people, reading IBM publications?

I think people thought you were talking about Syncsort's book, not Murach's
MVS/ESA book (I know I did).  The comments about the "proprietary statement"
were referring to Syncsort's book.   Syncsort DOES dictate terms for the use of
their own books.  AFAIK, nobody said anything about Syncsort trying to dictate
terms for people reading IBM publications.  Again, the DFSORT books are freely
accessible on the Web.  You can access them all in Bookmanager and PDF format
from:

http://www.storage.ibm.com/software/sort/mvs/srtmpub.html
```

```
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

Like I said - I DO like my Murach book - I often find myself treating it as
an "official" manual, and have to remind myself that it isn't . But I am
only a moderate user of SORT (regardless whether it's a cover for DFSORT or
SYNCHSORT). I use it to sort, sum, reformat, dedup and a few other things,
and at this level, I don't find any real problems using what Murach's book
tells me, instead of Syncsort or IBM. Of course, anyone who falls into the
trap of using batch File-Aid may have more problems. I got so confused using
both batch FA & DFSORT/Syncsort - different parms etc, I gave up on batch
FA, as it only seemed like a (slow) front end for regular IBM utilities.
```

## Thread: function keys — 4 msgs

### function keys

- **From:** "AngeluS" <kevin.azijn@pandora.be>
- **Date:** 2002-04-05T08:28:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6fdr8.52464$DE4.6773@afrodite.telenet-ops.be>`

```
Hi,
I'm working in rmcobol and i would like to use the function keys in my
program, but the problem is: I don't know how to implement this in my code
Can somebody help me.

Thanx
```

#### ↳ Re: function keys

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-05T16:01:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qTjr8.8658$k92.2579175478@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<6fdr8.52464$DE4.6773@afrodite.telenet-ops.be>`

```
"AngeluS" <kevin.azijn@pandora.be> wrote in message
news:6fdr8.52464$DE4.6773@afrodite.telenet-ops.be...
> I'm working in rmcobol and i would like to use the function keys in my
> program, but the problem is: I don't know how to implement this in my code
> Can somebody help me[?]

In your RM/COBOL Language Reference Manual, check the documentation of the
ON EXCEPTION phrase of the ACCEPT Statement (Terminal I-O) in Chapter 6.
There is additional documentation in the RM/COBOL User's Guide that gives an
extensive list of "field termination codes" which identify the exact
function key that was pressed.  If you are on UNIX then you will
additionally need to use the appropriate configuration (termcap/terminfo)
for the terminal you are using; this is also documented in the RM/COBOL
User's Guide.

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
```

#### ↳ Re: function keys

- **From:** "Ronald Leenheer" <r.leenheer@wanadoo.nl>
- **Date:** 2002-04-05T23:47:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nIqr8.40$Q65.1621@castor.casema.net>`
- **References:** `<6fdr8.52464$DE4.6773@afrodite.telenet-ops.be>`

```
Like Tom wrote first modify your config file. I can give samples but have to know your runtime system
For dos it would be something like
TERM-INPUT ACTION=CONTROL-BREAK                               ETX
TERM-INPUT ACTION=BACKSPACE                                   BS
TERM-INPUT ACTION=SCREEN-TERMINATE      CODE=58               HT
TERM-INPUT                              CODE=13 EXCEPTION=NO  CR
TERM-INPUT ACTION=SCREEN-ESCAPE         CODE=27               ESC
TERM-INPUT ACTION=CONTROL-BREAK                               NUL 000
TERM-INPUT                              CODE=56               NUL 015
TERM-INPUT ACTION=ERASE-REMAINDER       CODE=57               NUL 018
TERM-INPUT                              CODE=58               NUL 019
TERM-INPUT                              CODE=59               NUL 023
TERM-INPUT                              CODE=49               NUL 025
TERM-INPUT                              CODE=41               NUL 030
TERM-INPUT                              CODE=64               NUL 031
TERM-INPUT                              CODE=61               NUL 032
TERM-INPUT ACTION=ERASE-REMAINDER       CODE=13 EXCEPTION=NO  NUL 037
TERM-INPUT                              CODE=40               NUL 046
TERM-INPUT                              CODE=55               NUL 049
TERM-INPUT ACTION=SCREEN-TERMINATE      CODE=01               NUL 059
TERM-INPUT ACTION=SCREEN-TERMINATE      CODE=02               NUL 060
TERM-INPUT ACTION=SCREEN-TERMINATE      CODE=03               NUL 061
TERM-INPUT ACTION=SCREEN-TERMINATE      CODE=04               NUL 062
TERM-INPUT ACTION=SCREEN-TERMINATE      CODE=05               NUL 063
TERM-INPUT ACTION=SCREEN-TERMINATE      CODE=06               NUL 064
TERM-INPUT ACTION=SCREEN-TERMINATE      CODE=07               NUL 065
TERM-INPUT ACTION=SCREEN-TERMINATE      CODE=08               NUL 066
TERM-INPUT ACTION=SCREEN-TERMINATE      CODE=09               NUL 067
TERM-INPUT ACTION=SCREEN-TERMINATE      CODE=10               NUL 068
TERM-INPUT ACTION=SCREEN-TERMINATE      CODE=11               NUL 079
TERM-INPUT ACTION=SCREEN-HOME           CODE=54               NUL 071
TERM-INPUT ACTION=SCREEN-TERMINATE      CODE=52               NUL 072
TERM-INPUT                              CODE=57               NUL 073
TERM-INPUT ACTION=LEFT-ARROW                                  NUL 075
TERM-INPUT ACTION=RIGHT-ARROW                                 NUL 077
TERM-INPUT ACTION=SCREEN-TERMINATE      CODE=53               NUL 080
TERM-INPUT                              CODE=59               NUL 081
TERM-INPUT ACTION=SET-RM-INSERTION                            NUL 082
TERM-INPUT ACTION=DELETE-CHARACTER                            NUL 083
TERM-INPUT ACTION=ERASE-ENTIRE                                NUL 133
TERM-INPUT ACTION=SCREEN-TERMINATE      CODE=56               NUL 134

And for Unix:
TERM-INPUT action=left-arrow                    kcub1
TERM-INPUT action=right-arrow                   kcuf1
TERM-INPUT action=backspace precedence=1        kbs
TERM-INPUT action=set-rm-insertion              kf21
TERM-INPUT action=delete-character              kf20
TERM-INPUT action=erase-entire                  kf11
TERM-INPUT action=screen-terminate      code=53 kcud1
TERM-INPUT action=screen-terminate      code=52 kcuu1
TERM-INPUT action=screen-terminate      code=01 kf1
TERM-INPUT action=screen-terminate      code=02 kf2
TERM-INPUT action=screen-terminate      code=03 kf3
TERM-INPUT action=screen-terminate      code=04 kf4
TERM-INPUT action=screen-terminate      code=05 kf5
TERM-INPUT action=screen-terminate      code=06 kf6
TERM-INPUT action=screen-terminate      code=07 kf7
TERM-INPUT action=screen-terminate      code=08 kf8
TERM-INPUT action=screen-terminate      code=09 kf9
TERM-INPUT action=screen-terminate      code=10 kf10
TERM-INPUT action=screen-terminate      code=11 kf22
TERM-INPUT action=screen-terminate      code=56 kf12
TERM-INPUT action=screen-terminate      code=57 kf23
TERM-INPUT action=screen-terminate      code=59 kf24
TERM-INPUT action=screen-terminate      code=54 khome


Sample program part:


*********************************************************************
TABLE IN WS:
       01  S-TERMKEY                   PIC 9(03)  COMP-1.
           88  KEY-TAB                 VALUE 58.
           88  KEY-RETURN              VALUE 13.
           88  KEY-AFBREEK             VALUE 27.
           88  KEY-F1                  VALUE 1.
           88  KEY-F2                  VALUE 2.
           88  KEY-F3                  VALUE 3.
           88  KEY-F4                  VALUE 4.
           88  KEY-F5                  VALUE 5.
           88  KEY-F6                  VALUE 6.
           88  KEY-F7                  VALUE 7.
           88  KEY-F8                  VALUE 8.
           88  KEY-F9                  VALUE 9.
           88  KEY-F10                 VALUE 10.
           88  KEY-END                 VALUE 11.
           88  UP-ARROW                VALUE 52.
           88  DOWN-ARROW              VALUE 53.
           88  KEY-SKIP                VALUE 55.
           88  KEY-RESET               VALUE 56.
           88  KEY-HOME                VALUE 54.
           88  PG-UP                   VALUE 57.
           88  PG-DOWN                 VALUE 59.
           88  KEY-ERASE-VELD          VALUE 213.
           88  KEY-DEL                 VALUE 214.
           88  KEY-INS                 VALUE 217.
           88  KEY-ERROR               VALUE 14 THRU 26,
                                             28 THRU 39,
                                             41 THRU 51,
                                             60 THRU 212,
                                            218 THRU 999.
****************************************************************************************
           ACCEPT SRT-VA LINE 08 POSITION 23 PROMPT TAB NO BEEP
                UPDATE ON EXCEPTION S-TERMKEY
                PERFORM LOOS.
            IF KEY-F1 AND SO ON




"AngeluS" <kevin.azijn@pandora.be> schreef in bericht news:6fdr8.52464$DE4.6773@afrodite.telenet-ops.be...
> Hi,
> I'm working in rmcobol and i would like to use the function keys in my
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: function keys

- **From:** stephen.wylie@xko.co.uk (Stephen Wylie)
- **Date:** 2002-04-11T04:59:41-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<31b7fc43.0204110359.2fa024e4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<6fdr8.52464$DE4.6773@afrodite.telenet-ops.be> <nIqr8.40$Q65.1621@castor.casema.net>`

```
Processing F-keys as interrupts: I agree the best method is totally
platform dependent, but if you are in a looping program and want F-key
interrupts you can use a loop counter to break out to an ACCEPT BEFORE
TIME statement. I think you can use BEFORE TIME 0 and just handle the
exceptions, though I haven't done this for a while. My experience is
with RMcobol.

I didn't know students studied cobol anymore, we're supposed to be
dinosaurs. I like people to punctuate and capitalise, at least to some
extent, as it makes their posts easier to read, so I am more likely to
respond.
```

---

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f81f1e27.0204050254.896bba5@posting.google.com>`

```
Is there any tool for converting wang coble data to Sql Server?
```

#### ↳ Re: Wang cobol

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-04-05T12:27:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_Kgr8.109$Sa2.31@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<f81f1e27.0204050254.896bba5@posting.google.com>`

```
See article at http://www.flexus.com/ebd2asc.html for a tutorial on
"Converting COBOL-created Data for use by non-COBOL systems.

As far as tools, I think Mr.Guy will probably jump in; he has a canned
package which can do a lot of that. If you are a Win/32 programmer, I have a
DLL which can use the information from the FD and convert records based on
that.

Also, if there is an ODBC driver for rmcobol, that would allow you to move
the data as well without playing with bits.
```

#### ↳ Re: Wang cobol

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>

```
We moved our Wang COBOL data to Oracle by writing Wang COBOL programs to
dump the data to text files, transferring the text files to the Oracle
system, and using Oracles SQL*Loader program to load the data into
Oracle. Not a direct conversion, but not too bad & it uses the tools you
already have. (Or should already have unless you got rid of your Wang
before converting the data.)

dileep kumar wrote:

> Is there any tool for converting wang coble data to Sql Server?
```

```
that guide this migration of data automatically.
So, please take a look at our website www.anubex.com, and if interested take

Geert
```

## Thread: [OT] SVC 107 Authority Determined by... Library? — 12 msgs

### [OT] SVC 107 Authority Determined by... Library?

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-04-05T09:16:43-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a8kbkb$ola$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```


All right... here's a cute one.

My client has a home-brewed utility (IBM mainframe), written in ASM, which 
when passed an appropriate set of parameters will invoke CEMT to do... 
stuff.  I have a program that extracts some data from the billing files 
and have been directed to use this utility to make sure that the online 
billing program is DISabled so that no updates occur when I do my 
extracts.  The parameters used are something like:

PRODREGN,CEMT S TRAN(BILL) DIS

... where PRODREGN is the Prod CICS region name and CEMT S TRAN(BILL) DIS 
are more-or-less self-explanatory to those familiar with such things.

Anyhow... I code up a set of parameters like

TESTREGN,CEMT S TRAN(BILL) DIS

... for testing purposes and hit... a wall; I have no auth to use the 
utility.  When I ask what the reason for this is I am told that the 
utility is an 'all or none' affair and if I have the auth to use it I have 
the auth to invoke CEMT in *any* region.

This confuses me... so I do a bit of digging and find out that the utility 
issues an SVC 107 (MODESET); system security s set up so that auth to 
issue this CALL is determined by library (or so ABEND-AID tells me in the 
dump).

My question, to Those Who Know (And Might Deign To Answer) is... eh?  This 
is a first for me (an applications-jockey); never before have I run across 
a program whose auth is determined by the library from which it is 
invoked.  That bit of novelty aside... is it the case that a program 
authorised to issue an SVC 107 has the authority to do this to any CICS 
region?

It seems to me - and my system-level knowledge is *very* limited - that 
this is what The Professionals call 'a bunch o' hooey' and that a 
configuration can be made where programmers such as I can be given auth to 
invoke the utility for Test regions and the More Trustworthy Folks (like 
the CA7 scheduler, if that is a 'folk') can have auth for Prod... but 
someone is either unable or unmotivated to figure out just how.

Meanwhile, of course, I am stuck with a jobstream that I cannot test; the 
Security folks have told me that I need to arrange with one of their own 
to get this thing run even in the Test region.  I find this... tedious, to 
say the least, and dislike going, cap in hand, to other people and saying 
'Please, Sir, may I have another run?' instead of being allowed to merely 
Do My Own Job.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] SVC 107 Authority Determined by... Library?

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-05T19:32:04+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<p8nraucsq87n60tpsqgjcg9ovnd34cntb5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<a8kbkb$ola$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Doc,

<sysprog mode>
<teacher mode>
when using MODESET (SVC 107) the issuing program must run authorized.
How is this done?  The program must be linked with

//S1 EXEC PGM=IEWL,PARM='...,AC=1,....'
//SYSLMOD DD DISP=SHR,DSN=apf.lib.pds


Where apf.lib.pds is a PDS (or PDSE) named in the APF LIST.  The APF
list is either maintained in member IEAAPFxx in SYS1.PARMLIB, or in
member PROGxx in PARMLIB


If the program has not been linked into an authorized lib, tough luck,
you will get ABEND S047

And mere applications developing mortals don't normally get write
access to APF libs.  Whoever places programs in the APF libs present
potential danger to system integrity.

You wouldn't need to come to my office with cap in hand.  But you
would need to come to my office.  It would be me who would (look at
the prog and then) compile and link it into an APF library on a
sandbox system/LPAR.  And a bottle of wine wouldn't be needed either,
but if provided, would be appreciated and shared with you after the
program performs as wanted/advertised without any adverse side effects
</teacher mode>
</sysprog mode>

On 5 Apr 2002 09:16:43 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>
>
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
>DD

                                                            
     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_        
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,     
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'   
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

        (Another bit of Wisdom from my fortune cookie jar)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] SVC 107 Authority Determined by... Library?

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-04-05T12:54:42-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a8kod2$gik$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<a8kbkb$ola$1@panix1.panix.com> <p8nraucsq87n60tpsqgjcg9ovnd34cntb5@4ax.com>`

```
In article <p8nraucsq87n60tpsqgjcg9ovnd34cntb5@4ax.com>,
Volker Bandke  <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com> wrote:
>Doc,
>
><sysprog mode>
><teacher mode>

[snip mode]

>It would be me who would (look at
>the prog and then) compile and link it into an APF library on a
>sandbox system/LPAR.

Ahhhh... let me see if I have made myself clear and now understand.  It is 
not *my* program which does the SVC 107, it is a home-grown utility 
(e.g., RUNCEMT) which is invoked by my job.  RUNCEMT lives in 
SYSTEM.PROD.UTILS and that library has the appropriate auth for modules to 
invoke CEMT in PRODREGN.

When you say '... into an APF library on a sandbox system/LPAR' what I 
understand is that there can be another library containing another copy of 
the program - say, SYSTEM.TEST.UTILS(RUNCEMT) - which has CEMT auth in 
TESTREGN but not PROD.

Is this the case?  (the local sysprogs have said 'no, auth in TEST means 
auth in PROD' but I have trouble believing that)

Your patience and precision are both appreciated and, of course, 
undeserved by me.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] SVC 107 Authority Determined by... Library?

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-06T09:05:40+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2g6tauoq32rn7ip56sld2hj7uui8tcolqb@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<a8kbkb$ola$1@panix1.panix.com> <p8nraucsq87n60tpsqgjcg9ovnd34cntb5@4ax.com> <a8kod2$gik$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Doc,

Ahh, you got me a bit confused there for a moment, there are two
different problems here with the same name:  authorization

The module RUNCEMT issues the MODESET SVC, which requires that the
module is authorized (must be in an authorized library)

If a module comes from an authorized lib, the OS allows it to do
MODESET (et al), and thus issue OS commands, for example

In comes RACF, of ACF2, or Top Secret, or whatever security package
you are using.  Here we define which users may run the RUNCEMT
program.  If DD is not in the list of authorized people, tough luck.

If, indeed, DD is a member of the elite list, he can use RUNCEMT
against every  CICS system around.

I understand the sysprog to be reluctant about this.

How can this be solved?  By writing a better RUNCEMT program.

As I understand you now, the following is the JCL to run the utility

//DDJOB    JOB (RUNCEMT),'DD'
//S1      EXEC PMGF=RUNCEMT,PARM='PRODCICS,CEMT P SHUTDOWN IMMED'
//SYSPRINT DD  SYSOUT=*

With a little bit of work one can help you rather easily, but you will
need the SYSPROG and/or security administrator for this as well:

I use RACF samples from now on,  Top secret should be very similar,
and I don't know anything about ACF2 (except that it exists)

Define a RACF facility called RUNCEMT.*   with universal access NONE

(Nobody may use RUNCEMT against any CICS)

then do 

PERMIT RUNCEMT.TESTCICS ID(DD) ACCESS(READ)

which would allow DD to use the facility RUNCEMT.TESTCICS  (but not
PRODCICS)

Then the source of RUNCEMT must be amended to contain a RACF check to
test if the user has read access to RUNCEMT.first_parm_in_JCL

voila


Please contact me offline if you are interested in a sample program

                                                            
     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_        
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,     
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'   
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)        
                                                            
      Time: nature's way of keeping everything from happeningallatonce.
      
        (Another bit of Wisdom from my fortune cookie jar)         


-----------== Posted via Newsfeeds.Com, Uncensored Usenet News ==----------
   http://www.newsfeeds.com       The Largest Usenet Servers in the World!
------== Over 100,000 Newsgroups - Ulimited downloads - 19 servers ==-----
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] SVC 107 Authority Determined by... Library?

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-04-06T05:44:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CAE8B12.F966A361@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<a8kbkb$ola$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> 
> All right... here's a cute one.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> are more-or-less self-explanatory to those familiar with such things.

Hey, Doc, there is a similar program in my shop, named something like
"XXXCOMDR".  We use it to send commands into CICS regions.  Sometimes
they are CEMT commands, and sometimes it's simply a command to execute
a named CICS transaction ID, one written by us application programmers
to open our secondary VSAM files, refresh primaries to secondaries,
close the primary files, and update some global flags in our CICS
application.

> 
> Anyhow... I code up a set of parameters like
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> the auth to invoke CEMT in *any* region.

And I had a similar problem with the XXXCOMDR program in my shop.

> 
> This confuses me... so I do a bit of digging and find out that the utility
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> region?

I was unable to locate the source code for XXXCOMDR in my shop,
although I can remember looking at it around 1995, when I had a
similar testing problem.  But I found the executable in a library only
the sysprogs can update.  So I disassembled it with the HLASM
toolkit.  The program in my shop also uses SVC 107 MODESET.

The reason I needed to test XXXCOMDR was that the operators needed to
enter the CICS transaction-ID to switch between primary and secondary
files in my CICS region, and the plan was to automate it by initiating
the command from a batch job.  This would help reduce operator errors,
and relieve them of the extra work.  The way it was explained to me at
the time did not include any discussion of APF Authorized libraries. 
Apparently, some programs can alter the system in such dangerous ways
that those programs must be APF authorized and reside in
APF-authorized libraries.  

No, the way it was explained to me in 1995 was this:  By executing the
XXXCOMDR program in a batch job, I could send ANY operator command to
the MVS Operator's Console, and it would execute with exactly the same
authority as if the "Bastard Operator From Hell" had entered it!  That
would be a very dangerous capability to give to a mere applications
jockey.

But like you, I needed to test it.  The compromise solution was they
gave me authority to execute this program under my userid for three
weeks, just for testing.  If it makes any difference to this
explanation, the security package in my shop is ACF2 instead of RACF. 
I was told that they couldn't protect the system from me entering any
console operator command I wanted, but ACF2 would have an audit trail
of anything I did.  So if I caused an unscheduled IPL, they would know
who to blame.  Since I have never worked as an MVS operator, I don't
even know any useful operator commands, although I believe the utility
in our shop submits "F" or "MODIFY" commands to CICS regions. 
Basically, any text on the control card is slammed into the MVS
console command reader, hence the COMDR portion of the program name. 
I suppose I could have shut down VTAM or performed an LLA refresh (if
I knew the command syntax).

> 
> It seems to me - and my system-level knowledge is *very* limited - that
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> someone is either unable or unmotivated to figure out just how.

It's not completely hooey.

Yeah, it's possible all right, but it would be a much more complicated
program.  The program would have to determine what permissions the
submitting userid/jobname actually has, and then analyze the submitted
operator command to see if the userid is allowed to do that particular
command.  The program would have to use whatever API is available to
talk to the security system, RACF or ACF2 or Top Secret.

As far as I know, it is rare to code your own applications program to
perform security checking.  ACF2, RACF, and such do all the security
checking for you when you log on, or when the job is submitted to
JES.  User permissions are determined by the userid or the jobname. 
You need to submit operator commands, but the assembler program has no
ability to figure out which operator commands you are allowed to
execute. 

There are some other solutions of course.  You could learn how to
writeo your own EXCI programs (are you running at least CICS 4.1 or
Transaction Server?) to allow a batch job to interact with a CICS
region.  That probably has similar security issues.  Most shops just
purchase something like MacKinney Systems "Batch CEMT" (MTPBATCH),
which allows a batch job to open and close CICS files, and submit
other CEMT commands, with security checking to see if the
userid/jobname is allowed to talk to the requested CICS region.  Then
you can have batch CEMT authority for a test CICS region without also
having it for a production CICS region. 

> 
> Meanwhile, of course, I am stuck with a jobstream that I cannot test; the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

Unless your shop has a product similar to MTPBATCH, it looks like you
will have to go "cap in hand".

Sorry about that.
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] SVC 107 Authority Determined by... Library?

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 2002-04-06T08:26:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CAEB16B.CFF4C9DB@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<a8kbkb$ola$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
The actual action of the utility you mention is undoubtably done by
issuing a console command of the form
F PRODREGN,CEMT S TRAN(BILL) DIS
in response to the PRODREGN,CEMT S TRAN(BILL) DIS parameter, as this is
the method for sending CICS commands from outside CICS.

It is quite possible that this utility was written to merely add a "F "
in front the parameters and issue the resulting command, as this would
by far be the simplest way to write it.  In that case, it can do
anything to any CICS region and do nasty things to other started tasks
as well -- much too dangerous for general consumption.  If it were smart
enough apply strict validation on its parameters, and to allow a
different class of users authority to issue commands only to TEST CICS
regions (and this could be done through checks with some special
installation-defined RACF security profiles), then it might be possible
to allow wider access to the utility.   But,... if it isn't already
coded with that flexibility in place, someone is going to have to find
time and resources to extend and throughly validate it first. 
Otherwise, it would allow you to do all sorts of nasty things, like
shutdown production CICS regions and other system started tasks, etc.

MVS integrity and security design only allows "dangerous" system
requests to be performed by authorized code.  This means the program
involved must be linked as authorized (anyone can do this), must ALSO be
loaded from an authorized library (these are carefully protected), and
must not be running in an address space containing any other executing
modules from a nonauthorized library (such modules might attempt to
corrupt the in memory copy of the authorized program code in some way). 
The ability to define a library as authorized is restricted, and the
ability to update the content of authorized libraries is also restricted
in order to prevent users from creating their own utilities that bypass
MVS security or compromise MVS integrity.  If an "approved" authorized
utility is deemed dangerous in the wrong hands, its use may be
restricted through the RACF Security Server by either disallowing READ
access to the authorized library where it resides or by disallowing
permission to execute that specific load module.

Some of the standard IBM utilities and some TSO commands run as
"authorized" programs.

docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> 
> All right... here's a cute one.
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] SVC 107 Authority Determined by... Library?

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

- **Message-ID:** `<a8ospd$7p7$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<a8kbkb$ola$1@panix1.panix.com> <3CAEB16B.CFF4C9DB@acm.org>`

```
In article <3CAEB16B.CFF4C9DB@acm.org>, Joel C. Ewing <jcewing@acm.org> wrote:

[snip]

>If it were smart
>enough apply strict validation on its parameters, and to allow a
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

And again, bingo... what I have been told three times is true.  You, Mr 
Trembley and Mr Bandke have all reported that someone will have to Do Some 
Work; this is, to say the least, unlikely.

Thanks much!

DD
```

```
Hi!

I'm building an aplication where is possible the user to make Drag and Drop
of a textbox. I've already seen the sample of Mouse Event in the PowerCobol
Examples, but instead of using icons I want to use textbox, for the Mouse
Pointer. It is possible?

Best Regards
Jm
```

```
Gianni
```

```
and when the Mouse Up event occurs it drops the string value.

Best Regards
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Drag Drop with Power Cobol V5 or V6

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-08T12:02:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HFfs8.274406$2q2.24936452@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<wilr8.177$eD.791655@newsserver.ip.pt> <bbe7925c011e5d403847b36ea17794ea.40368@mygate.mailgate.org> <G_cs8.465$ml2.2160711@newsserver.ip.pt>`

```

"PorTy" <euromercante1@clix.pt> wrote in message
news:G_cs8.465$ml2.2160711@newsserver.ip.pt...
> Thank you for the answer
>
> What I really want, if possible, is to put the string value in the mouse
> pointer.
> For example, when the Mouse Down event  occurs I select the textbox, when
I
> drag it takes in the Mouse Pointer the string value of the selected
textbox,
> and when the Mouse Up event occurs it drops the string value.
>
> Best Regards

"Drops the string value..." drop it where? You can't place text just
anywhere on a form; you have to place text into a control. Since you can't
_create_ a control on the fly, you have to use some other technique. You
could, I guess, have an invisible or off-screen text box and, at the
mouse-up event, move the receiving box to the mouse location and insert the
text.

To answer your question directly, you cannot associate text with a mouse
pointer.
```

#### ↳ Re: Drag Drop with Power Cobol V5 or V6

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-06T00:23:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zerr8.132274$VJ1.11178070@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<wilr8.177$eD.791655@newsserver.ip.pt>`

```

"PorTy" <euromercante1@clix.pt> wrote in message
news:wilr8.177$eD.791655@newsserver.ip.pt...
> Hi!
>
> I'm building an aplication where is possible the user to make Drag and
Drop
> of a textbox. I've already seen the sample of Mouse Event in the
PowerCobol
> Examples, but instead of using icons I want to use textbox, for the Mouse
> Pointer. It is possible?

Get the coordinates of the mouse during a MouseDown event. Save them. Get
the coordinates again during a MouseUp event. Compute the x and y offsets.
Apply these offsets to the coordinates of the text box.

Might work.

We routinely move whole forms off the screen and we define various things -
like list boxes - at impossible coordinates.
```

#### ↳ Re: Drag Drop with Power Cobol V5 or V6

- **From:** "Calin in TORONTO" <me@cantbebothered.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-09T17:11:12-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HNIs8.5252$rp4.1773032@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<wilr8.177$eD.791655@newsserver.ip.pt>`

```
What you want to do is use the actual text in the text box as a mouse
pointer, right?

If it is so, you will have to do some hoop jumping, i.e. you will have to
make your string into a bitmap and use it as a drag-drop mouse pointer.  You
can do that by getting the DC of the text box, create a compatible bitmap
and copy the DC into it.  Make it transparent (not sure how with Fujitsu,
with NetExpress you would use a transparent image list) and assign it as
your mouse pointer while you're dragging.

Don't want to get into technical details, but that's how I would do it.

Good luck!
Calin, TORONTO

"PorTy" <euromercante1@clix.pt> wrote in message
news:wilr8.177$eD.791655@newsserver.ip.pt...
> Hi!
>
> I'm building an aplication where is possible the user to make Drag and
Drop
> of a textbox. I've already seen the sample of Mouse Event in the
PowerCobol
> Examples, but instead of using icons I want to use textbox, for the Mouse
> Pointer. It is possible?
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
```

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2002-04-06T06:53:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers,alt.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1018075749@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02
Version: 3.01
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: PC-version of IDMS

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-08T14:44:56-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a8ss04$ofu$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<a8qhnl$8ek$1@news.hccnet.nl> <3CB1D4A5.7D12@this.mailaddress.is.invalid>`

```
The following is the "relevant" information that I received from one of my
"usually reliable sources"

"Bill:
IDMSPC is no longer supported. The only supported platforms the product was
certified to run on had been DOS.. However, it did run okay in a DOS box
under WINDOWS 3.1 and WINDOWS 95 .. However, I don't think it ever worked on
WINDOWS NT because of the WINDOWS NT Hardware Abstraction Layer.

You can advise Jack to contact CA to see if he can still purchase the
unsupported release, but I doubt he can. And even if he does get it, the
bottom line is there will be no future development nor support for it.

Hope Jack is not too disappointed"
```

```
Hi, you may want to try API calls that let you

1) Select printer
2) Set print props
3) Control Start/End Page
4) Write one line at a time, etc.

All in COBOL.

Sample codes are available at
http://www.kimsoft.com/api-cobol/api-cobol.htm - download copylib.zip
and advanced.  See PrintText.app/.cbl.

Hope this helps.
```

## Thread: Write not happening? — 12 msgs

### Write not happening?

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-10T01:52:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jVMs8.31382$4L4.1174571@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
under OS/390, Cobol 1.2.2, the following code is simply failing. 
No indication of why, it simply doesn't write to the VSAM file it is supposed to 
be writing to. 

I'm in the middle of one of those marathon coding sessions right now, so I am probably too
tired to really see what is going on. Anyone have any advice on this one? All this little segment
of the application is doing is loading some records from a sequential file. 

Thanks 
-Paul

000150              WRITE SMDR-RECORD                                        
000151                INVALID KEY                                            
000152                  DISPLAY '(1) ' WS-USER-COUNT1 ': FAILED RECORD'      
000153                  DISPLAY '(F) ' SMDRIN-RECORD                         
000154                  DISPLAY '(L) ' WS-LAST-SMDR-RECORD                   
000155                  DISPLAY ' '                                          
000156                  ADD 1 TO WS-USER-COUNT2                              
000157                NOT INVALID KEY                                        
000158                  MOVE SMDRIN-RECORD TO WS-LAST-SMDR-RECORD            
000159                  ADD 1 TO WS-USER-COUNT3                              
000160                  DISPLAY 'SMDRWRITE: ' SMDR-RECORD                    
000161                END-WRITE                                              
000162              READ SMDRIN NEXT RECORD                                  


I put display statements just before and after the write, so I am sure the code is being hit. 
I also displayed the SMDR-RECORD and it is being loaded correctly. 
TKU! -Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: Write not happening?

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>

- **References:** `<jVMs8.31382$4L4.1174571@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
I would suggest you put a file status in the select statement and
check its value after the write (and open). Invalid key does not catch
all the possible failure conditions. Let us know what happens.

===================================================================== 
  "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com> wrote in message news:jVMs8.31382$4L4.1174571@typhoon.austin.rr.com...
  under OS/390, Cobol 1.2.2, the following code is simply failing. 
  No indication of why, it simply doesn't write to the VSAM file it is supposed to 
  be writing to. 

  I'm in the middle of one of those marathon coding sessions right now, so I am probably too
  tired to really see what is going on. Anyone have any advice on this one? All this little segment
  of the application is doing is loading some records from a sequential file. 

  Thanks 
  -Paul

  000150              WRITE SMDR-RECORD                                        
  000151                INVALID KEY                                            
  000152                  DISPLAY '(1) ' WS-USER-COUNT1 ': FAILED RECORD'      
  000153                  DISPLAY '(F) ' SMDRIN-RECORD                         
  000154                  DISPLAY '(L) ' WS-LAST-SMDR-RECORD                   
  000155                  DISPLAY ' '                                          
  000156                  ADD 1 TO WS-USER-COUNT2                              
  000157                NOT INVALID KEY                                        
  000158                  MOVE SMDRIN-RECORD TO WS-LAST-SMDR-RECORD            
  000159                  ADD 1 TO WS-USER-COUNT3                              
  000160                  DISPLAY 'SMDRWRITE: ' SMDR-RECORD                    
  000161                END-WRITE                                              
  000162              READ SMDRIN NEXT RECORD                                  


  I put display statements just before and after the write, so I am sure the code is being hit. 
  I also displayed the SMDR-RECORD and it is being loaded correctly. 
  TKU! -Paul
```

- **References:** `<jVMs8.31382$4L4.1174571@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <a90795$fhf$1@suaar1aa.prod.compuserve.com> <oJNs8.31397$4L4.1187353@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
Oh great. File status 90 is one of those lovely codes defined as "No further
information". Are there any messages on the job log that might give the 
exact VSAM error? If not, you will have to go the next step and get the vsam 
return codes as well, like this: 

file status is vsam-status vsam-return.

01  vsam-return-area.
    05 vsam-status pic xx. 
    05 vsam-return. 
        10 vsam-return-code pic 9(2) comp. 
        10 vsam-function-code pic 9(1) comp. 
        10 vsam-feedback-code pic 9(3) comp.  

Then, oh joy, you have to go to the assembler vsam macro reference to interpret these 
codes. Move these values to some usage display field and display them and let us know.
I'll check in the morning. Does this file have any alternate indexes? I can't really tell
beyond that without seeing the job, the program, jcl and cluster definition.
```

```
Hi Ron,

You didn't show your select or FD stmts. The LE cobols have a runtime
option that masks the abend and leaves you with an empty file. I
misspelled the DDNAME, and it happened. You might want to check that.

Jack 


"Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net> wrote in message news:<a90fq3$qol$1@suaar1ac.prod.compuserve.com>...
> Oh great. File status 90 is one of those lovely codes defined as "No 
> further
…[96 more quoted lines elided]…
> --
```

```
Sorry I didn't reply this morning - I kind of crashed hard last night. :) 
As you suspected, there is some wierdness going on.  THe closest guess
I can get to this is that it thinks the VSAM file is an ESDS vice a KSDS
file. Huh...

Now  light begins to dawn... someone had replaced this file with a new version - 
naturally enough, they didn't tell anyone. (*sigh*) This leads me to some vauge
memory that you have to access empty VSAM files differently than you do a 
file that doesn't have any records. If that a false memory, or am I really remembering
something here? :)
```
