```
Hi,


> Seeing the Fujitsu .Net preview i think it is so hard to learn, because
> its new concepts and methods <Snip>

I've trained a few mainframe Cobol programmers in the new tool, and
for my money it would have been better just to use VB.NET or C#.  Very
few similarities.

Fujitsu also seems to have a late-to-market approach, and is not
supporting an RTM version yet.  Their support and customer sales
departments were total jerks when it came to providing release dates,
deadlines, and the support I've come to expect out of other companies
eager to get their beta .NET components into developers hands.
```

#### ↳ Re: Opinion needed of .NET and COBOL.net

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-17T11:11:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TLcv8.8425$o86.306934@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<2763b81a.0204161925.58ef9217@posting.google.com>`

```
Ugh -
  The biggest issues I have with .NET is that (1) it does not address
persistentancy (2) it is portable
only to one platform and (2) it is not as well put together as a standard
RMI, IIOP, or CORBA solution.
In fact, while .NET has some really nice parts, it also just glosses over
the warts as 'unimportant.'
That is a typical Microsoft tactic.

  Check out the MicroFocus view of Net development, it is much cleaner and
simpler.

  If you really *really* need something like .NET, you would be nest served
by learning Java,
and/or spending the $$ on a Cobol compiler like PerCOBOL that generates
Java. (This allows you
to take advantage of, use, and even create things like Enterprise Server
Beans, which are everything that
.Net proposes to be and more.)

The gotcha here is that PerCOBOL has not recognized nor provided for low
cost devleopment tools for the
developer community. They are targeting 'big shops' and are either not
financially able to offer low cost
toolsets (i.e, in PWD, Visual Age COBOL will cost you $15 for your first
copy) or are simply unwilling
to develope a strong developer community. <shrug> Their compiler is still
pretty nice. :)

-Paul


"Mike" <maross@texoma.net> wrote in message
news:2763b81a.0204161925.58ef9217@posting.google.com...
> Hi. I' am trying to evaluate my possible upgrade to Fujitsu's COBOL
> for .NET framework. I am Upgrading my pc COBOL programs to be web
…[84 more quoted lines elided]…
>     {theImg  =
document.getElementById("fruitImage").setAttribute("src","man50kg.jpg");}
> //note1**  above statement evaluates, swaps, DOC model vs NetCOBOLs
> if (funcVarible  == "man70kg.jpg")
>     {theImg  =
document.getElementById("fruitImage").setAttribute("src","man70kg.jpg");}
> alert(funcVarible);
>                              }
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> you Mike.
```

#### ↳ Re: Opinion needed of .NET and COBOL.net

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-04-17T13:57:37+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cbdbd3d_11@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<2763b81a.0204161925.58ef9217@posting.google.com>`

```
Mike,

I had some difficulty understanding your "rant" because it seems
inconsistent in places (see below).

The bottom line is that it is still early days for .Net. I am waiting for at
least another 4 months before I'll switch serious work to NetCOBOL
development (if then...there are serious limitiations in NetCOBOL which need
to be addressed before it would be acceptable to me, personally). In the
meantime, as the same effect can be achieved with components using SOAP, I
see no pressing need to switch...

Personally, I believe .NET is an important step forward because it is
allowing the use of different source languages to play together nicely on a
level playing field. This removes the emphasis on source which has been the
historical norm, and minimises the importance of the Language used for
development (which I believe is a "Good Thing" - different components SHOULD
be developed in different languages.  The language best suited to the task
at hand would be a good choice....).

I take your point that it simply represents another technical conversion for
you, and for that reason alone I would "wait a bit" if I were you.

Comments on your "rant" below...

Mike <maross@texoma.net> wrote in message
news:2763b81a.0204161925.58ef9217@posting.google.com...
> Hi. I' am trying to evaluate my possible upgrade to Fujitsu's COBOL
> for .NET framework. I am Upgrading my pc COBOL programs to be web
> enabled for a larger audience as well as make updates easier(place
> Http links for instant updates on server).

.NET  is not a requirement for the update service. Web enablement can also
be achieved by using Components and distributing them on your servers, then
accessing with HTML/JavaScript, as you mention below. (I do this. Have
embedded components loaded to web pages as Objects, then manipulate their
properties with JavaScript.)

> However since learning
> DHTML (JavaScript/CSS/html) plus server CGI (Perl/COBOL-CGI/Java), I
> find COBOL.NET or C#(however better than COBOL.NET) that it takes
> twice as much code, learning and is non transportable compared to the
> new features in the HTML/javascript-6 level browsers (ie6/ns6.2).

Don't understand this. Are you saying C# takes twice as much code as
NetCOBOL? So What? As for non-transportable, if the Objects are wrapped
properly they MUST be transportable, whether they were written in COBOL or
C#. The source code is irrelevant. If you mean "transportable across Unix
and Windows" then you will need CORBA wrapping (at the moment - again, it is
extremely likely that .NET will eventually encompass UNIX platforms also...)

> In
> fact looking at www.Cobolscript.com as a CGI that is transportable to
> Linux/windows/Sun and works with browsers for $49 make more sense than

CGI code should be transportable (across UNIX/Win32) with only minimal
rebuild, anyway. If you were seduced into multi-tasking with ISAPI then you
are locked to Win32 (at least currently).

> Fujitsu's $4200 package; however there seems to be no user
> base/threads in CobolScript and the debugger lacks Fujitsu's well
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> as this is basically VB6).

Well, not being a dedicated follower of fashion, I certainly won't be losing
PowerCOBOL anytime soon...It has served me very well indeed and I expect it
to continue to do so. And I wouldn't hold your breath for the demise of VB6
either. There is a huge Corporate base and, despite what user forums may be
saying, it is unlikely to be replaced overnight.



> My only reservations to anything with
> JavaScript are that everyone can see most of your code, which makes
> CGI.exe's calls needed.

Hmmmm.... this one baffles me. I use COBOL for CGI and JavaScript for Client
side. This is prompted purely by performance reasons and NOT because I care
if people use (or even steal) my JavaScript code. The idea of moving
functionality to CGI just so it can be "hidden" strikes me as a very BAD
idea... CGI is inherently inefficient (whether you write it in COBOL or
Perl...in fact, even if you replace it with a Java Servlet...) because it
requires an interaction between the Client and the Server (plus it is NOT
multithreaded). Therefore, what is coded in CGI SHOULD be decided on what
actually NEEDS Server back end functionality (like database access,
frinstance) and not on what "needs" to be hidden.

I use server-based templates to minimise the HTML generated from my CGI
code, and I use SSI on the server to serve up the CGI generated pages. (It
all works pretty well...)


> I've seen so many COBOL display-accept schemes
> since my first MS-2.0 COBOL compiler in1984 that using
> HTML/JavaScript-streaming-Multimedia interpreters (MS.NET's CLR and
> Java are also interpreted) is what the user wants verses second rate
> COBOL text/semi-multimedia solutions.

Sorry, you have lost me here... can you say it again slowly, please? (I
can't grasp the connection between accept-display schemes you have seen and
what the Users want...)

>>>
> Note here is COBOL.net reference to html text/scr(img)  from
…[57 more quoted lines elided]…
> THAT DOES SAME AS ABOVE **

What's the point here? That JavaScript does the same job in less code? The
code is arguably more complex (OK, it's a weak argument if you really
understand JavaScript <G> but NetCOBOL is aimed at COBOL programmers, NOT
JavaScript experts...<G>) Since when did the size of SOURCE code matter
anyway? COBOL has ALWAYS been verbose; OO COBOL is no exception. That
doesn't make it worthless; many would argue that that is one of its most
important advantages (self documentation).

> <html><head>
> <script  language="javascript">
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>     {theImg  =
document.getElementById("fruitImage").setAttribute("src","man50kg.jpg");}
> //note1**  above statement evaluates, swaps, DOC model vs NetCOBOLs
> if (funcVarible  == "man70kg.jpg")
>     {theImg  =
document.getElementById("fruitImage").setAttribute("src","man70kg.jpg");}
> alert(funcVarible);
>                              }
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> in NetCOBOL **********

Well, The DOC Object Model has established itself as extremely powerful and
part of the power of JavaScript, VBScript, and ASP comes from the underlying
power of this model.

Many people in this forum will look at both solutions above and throw up
their hands in horror... Some will argue that the COBOL solution is just as
"bad" as the JavaScript one... most of the mainframers (who are the majority
of contributors here) will simply ignore this thread...

For the remainder of us, who are desperately trying to come to grips with
emerging technologies (as they emerge), there is an argument that says maybe
OO COBOL is NOT the best solution for this kind of problem.

I am not persuaded of that yet. I think you need to look at what the
functionality requires and assess what tools to use, based on that, and all
the other factors that give any particular language its edge. The amount of
source code would not be my sole criterion. And what would go in CGI and
what would go on the Client would not be determined by paranoia <G>, but by
what is most efficient...


> Please note you must address the CLR API Class and set the properties
> prior to settings a variable. Which means you must double define all
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> not just drop COBOL?

This is a good argument. Many people will be persuaded by it. Hence the
decline of COBOL.

We can sum this up by saying that people who have mastered OO concepts and
techniques will have no difficulty with relating to and using various Models
(Like the DOC model above). As they do so, they are likely to become more
and more frustrated with the verbosity of COBOL and will use it less and
less, switching to other "pure" OO Languages like Java.

On the other hand, people with a COBOL background (and there are millions of
them), who want to get into the World of Web Enablement without the Java
learning curve MIGHT find NetCOBOL has at least some vestiges of what they
know and love... I honestly don't know whether there is a "migration route"
for COBOLlers from COBOL to NetCOBOL...there may be in theory, but in
practice, OO Concepts are absolutely fundamental to success in this field
and these have been rejected by Fortress COBOL. We can therefore say with
fair certainty that Fortress COBOL will NOT be part of the coming era and
the Fortress will be simply bypassed. (I said this first publicly over 8
years ago (predicted it would happen within 10 - 15 years) and it is now
coming true...)

COBOL people who want to be part of the future MUST expand their skill sets
(I recommend learning Java as the first step). (Think of it as taking an
escape tunnel out of the Fortress...<G>)


> IMHO this MS.NET seems to be an attempt to trap developers into
> proprietary MS-C#/MS-OS technology to offset the
> LINUX/Netscape6.2/java revenue bleed to supplement Gates bad
> investment's in Telecom/web and etc crap at the expense of their new
> 6.0 upgrade model for enterprises.

This may have elements of truth but it doesn't alter the fact that .NET will
be significant.

> Just wait to the 7.0 upgrade .NET
> model gets them.

Gets who? The Users? They would have to upgrade at some point with or
without .NET. If the product is improved no-one will reasonably object to
upgrading.

> Plus Java/JavaScript advice and sample programs can
> easily be found on the WWW.

This is one of the secrets to Java's success. It is freely available and the
User base is constantly expanding. Sun never tried to make it their mainstay
revenue generator. They just wanted everybody to use it so they could
continue to sell great hardware.  Even now, with an established User base,
ANYONE can download Java and use it, for FREE. Couple that with actively
listening to and encouraging feedback and contributions from the User base,
using modern technology and a fast turnaround (cf. the J4 COBOL Standards
committee...) and it is really no wonder that COBOL is left in the dust.
Sun's farsighted policy has paid off, in spades...

You seem to be saying that JavaScript is an alternative to .NET (I said some
of your post seems inconsistent, earlier <G>. If this is my
misunderstanding, please elucidate...). It isn't. JavaScript is a useful
HTML/XML Client side scripting language whether you use .NET or not... Java
(no real relation to JavaScript) is useful for both Client and Server side
programming (Applets and Servlets, never mind Beans...) I see no reason to
REPLACE JavaScript with .NET programming.

This may be the point where we differ; I have strong feelings about Server
side code being limited to what MUST be on the server, and Client side code
doing everything it can without server interaction. I accept that other
people may not see it that way and that means the "boundaries" are less well
defined, so "more" can be done with NetCOBOL (or whatever Server side
language is chosen). This comes down again to architecture. I use Component
technology which I believe is "future proof". You can simply wrap and deploy
the components on any server (also across UNIX and Win32), including .NET
(where they are rebuilt as Web Services), so the design decisions come down
to WHAT will be encapsulated into a Web component. This approach SHOULD
minimise the many hours of maintenance you mention below. (Currently I use
PowerCOBOL for most of this. However, OO COBOL can do the same job (Power
COBOL uses OO COBOL) but requires more detailed definition of interfaces.)

>   Sorry for the long rant, but these upgrades that claim vast
> technology shifts leads to 100s of maintenance hours for me and adds
> TCO to us and our customers along with decreased productivity.  Thank
> you Mike.

BOTTOM LINE:

1. Review your existing architecture and approach before deciding to commit
to .NET

2. Expect .NET to seriously affect the whole Client/Server world, and to
make significant inroads into the User base. It WILL be important. The trick
is to ensure that your applications are designed so as to minimise the
impact of this (or any other) "Earth shattering announcement" upon them.

The opinions expressed above are purely my own and I do not have any
affiliation with, or allegiance to, MS or any other vendor.

Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

Hi,

I worked with the Fujitsu beta for some time, so maybe I can shed a
bit of light...

<snip>, I
> find COBOL.NET or C#(however better than COBOL.NET) that it takes
> twice as much code, learning and is non transportable compared to the
> new features in the HTML/javascript-6 level browsers (ie6/ns6.2). 

Twice as much code? .NET generates code, and does a decent job at
taking care of browser compatibility.  I've had very good luck with
it.

Now, if you're asking whether I would recommend using COBOL syntax in
a web page, the answer is NO.  I would, however, encourage it in
middleware or back-end components.

> Fujitsu NET also gives you free
> VB.net, which by the threads of VB6 users looks like it is better to
> just learn C# and give the  <snip>
This boils down to using what you're most comfortable with. C# can do
a few things VB can't, but for the average user who does not have a
C++ background, this is trivial.


>> and if you study java/C#.net why
>> not just drop COBOL?
If you have an entrenched COBOL code base that (1) contains complex
business logic, as many healthcare or financial companies have
developed, or (2) is easily modularized, then it pays to stick it into
components, then call it from C# or VB.NET.

> IMHO this MS.NET seems to be an attempt to trap developers into
> proprietary MS-C#/MS-OS technology to offset the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> easily be found on the WWW.
Umm.. .NET does a lot more than a simple upgrade, and there are
TREMENDOUS .NEt resources, free code and components online, rivaling
what you'll find in the JavaScript free market.  This is one instance
where it doesn't pay to generalize. Msoft did it's homework this time.
 Since most enterprises DON'T use Netscape and Linux on the desktop,
.NET is a perfectly viable solution.

>   Sorry for the long rant, but these upgrades that claim vast
> technology shifts leads to 100s of maintenance hours for me and adds
> TCO to us and our customers along with decreased productivity.  

It's a part of life.  I contend that if you take a longer-term
perspective and put the initial time in learning, your development and
maintenance times in a .NET application will be dramatically smaller
than with other platforms, with a similar level of expertise.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinion needed of .NET and COBOL.net

- **From:** usenetrob@hotmail.com (Rob)
- **Date:** 2002-04-18T06:16:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3826a0a6.0204180516.1bf2b4bc@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2763b81a.0204161925.58ef9217@posting.google.com> <3826a0a6.0204171857.43f09039@posting.google.com> <a9lhb702u3q@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
> 
> This is a bit tangential, but would you elaborate on the things that C#.net
> can do that VB.net can't.  I've been working my way through C#, but realize
> that I prefer the syntactic style of VB.  What do I lose if I jump to
> VB.net?

As I understand it, two of the biggest things are that you can't
overload operators in VB.NET (this is where you can use +,-,=, etc.
with your own complex datatypes and classes, where not otherwise
defined.  For example, if you wanted to set one car object equal to
another, you could actually set up a situation where the "=" is used.)

Secondly, you can't run unmanaged C++ code in C#.

Other than that, if you prefer VB, go for it. I work back and forth
between the two.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinion needed of .NET and COBOL.net

- **From:** usenetrob@hotmail.com (Rob)
- **Date:** 2002-04-18T06:34:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3826a0a6.0204180534.29484f96@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2763b81a.0204161925.58ef9217@posting.google.com> <3826a0a6.0204171857.43f09039@posting.google.com> <2763b81a.0204172328.1fd26ce1@posting.google.com>`

```
> <snip> into .NET.  However I found learning JavaScript DOM (Document Object
> Model not DOC (I started that)) so easy, refreshing and graphical that
> I believe any COBOL programmer not learning JavaScript for a jump to
> Java must see their death coming before it will be required. <snip>

It's better to start with Java, since it's more strongly typed, and
not that much more difficult.  Plus, you can get decent debuggers and
don't have to learn it in terms of a user interface (the browser). 
You can create command-line applications at first,then once familiar
with classes and objects, move into applets, servlets, etc.

> Linux with KDE 3.0, Apache 2.2, and Java Web Services due out this
> week, along with   Lindows WINE project will cause a vast decrease in
> MS revenue the next 5 years unless .NET traps them.

How do you figure?  Most desktops in the world are Windows based, and
the .NET framework is available for most Windows versions.  Most
business workstations are Windows-based, if for no other reason than
to run Office.  Sure, you can get an Office "clone" for Linux, but
this doesn't do everything that Word or Excel can do.

> Micro$ofts pricing of 6.0 is having an adverse affect on corporations
> views and many are looking for other solutions as are web programmers.

You should clarify your meaning, because the survey you list below
does not support that blanket assessment (see below).

> This article (with graphics) tells a lot.
> http://www.sunbelt-software.com/survey_02mar.cfm IBM is also
> contributing to the move to Linux and Java with their push. 

Consider the survey (and read the book "How to Lie with Statistics").
As quoted from the article itself:
"Disclaimer: This is a self-selecting Web-based survey undertaken to
identify corporate user trends, behavior and buying patterns. The
findings are not absolute and no one can predict with any certainty
how any of us will act in the future. "

You're dealing with a very small sample-- corporations who were
willing to respond to surveys, whose possible motives may include
pushing Microsoft to lower costs. Plus, there are many more
corporations whose confidentiality policies prohibit them from filling
out surveys.  We were given no indication as to how Sunbelt went about
choosing respondents.  Finally, we don't have any budget figures to
back up corporate assessments that the Microsoft Licensing is
unaffordable.  Not exactly conclusive.  Corporations can't just
"switch" to Linux overnight.  What they CAN do is delay purchases,
which, thankfully, the article mentions, but in my opinion is more
likely.

>  Actually Rob I also find C#/asp.net a much improvement over asp. But
> Fujitsu's .NET is seems to have developed into a lower level compiler
> verses their successful high level PowerCOBOL.

Again, Fujitsu's .NET isn't even shipping, and I haven't been too
impressed.

On another note, people should also consider that Cobol Code is
DEFINITELY more verbose than JavaScript.  C# is closer in "tight code"
than JavaScript in any case. Frankly, I still use a lot of JavaScript
for complex client validation and other knicknacks (like throwing
alert messages to a web page after a server-side validation.  Talk
about neat..)
```

> for .NET will be no more transportable than they have with their Win32

>  http://www.mandrakesoft.com

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

Spoken like a true geek. You need to get your mind around two concepts:

1. You are confusing the tool with the ultimate goal. Java, COBOL, Windows,
etc. are NOT the goal, they are a means to a completely different end.

2. Nobody wants Linux or Windows; what they want is the result that comes
from using the tool. Nobody who bought a 1/4" drill bit ever wanted the damn
 drill bit. What they wanted was a 1/4" HOLE! It doesn't matter if a drill
bit is sharper, lasts longer, costs less, and comes it bright colors, if it
uses an ancient Hittite measuring system that is incompatible or marginally
compatible with their 1/4" bolt.

## Thread: Reentrant programs in Cobol — 6 msgs

### Reentrant programs in Cobol

- **From:** menonshyam@yahoo.com (shyam menon)
- **Date:** 2002-04-16T20:43:48-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10986fae.0204161943.1a2c630f@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,
  I am unable to understand some topics regarding reentrancy in Cobol
program.

1. A reentrant program is made by using RENT compiler option.
   So this program can be used by multiple users.But this will require
a driver like IMS or CICS to handle the MVS tasks right?

2. Is a separate copy of Working Storage alloted to each user who uses
the program or is it common for all?
Can any changes be made to the data in the working storage variables
and if so how will it impact the multiple users who are executing the
program?

3.There is a lot of stuff about the program not being able to modify
itself?
 What is the exact meaning of this?

4. Is there any restriction on changing the constant data declared in
the WS for such programs?

Thanks in advance,
Shyam.
```

#### ↳ Re: Reentrant programs in Cobol

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-16T23:13:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a9isp4$qju$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<10986fae.0204161943.1a2c630f@posting.google.com>`

```
The bottom-line is that if you use an IBM compiler from VS COBOL II on (eg.
IBM COBOL for this-and-that) *and* compile with the RENT compiler option
*and* link-edit with the RENT link-edit attribute (which will be your
default if you don't override things), then you need do NOTHING special in
your program to insure that it is "truly" re-entrant.  If you run the
program in any MVS (OS/390 or z/OS) environment that supports reentrant
programs, then everything should work "fine".

HOWEVER, there was a restriction in VS COBOL II (and early versions of IBM
COBOL for this-and-that) on "multiple" copies of SEPARATE run-units under
the same "task".  See

http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3mg00/1.2.4

and look at the topics
  "Support to |run in  multiple threads"
and
  "Support for multiple tasks"

NOTE: For the "new" multi-thread support, you need to use PL/I, Assembler,
or USS services to "start" the simultaneous threads.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Reentrant programs in Cobol

- **From:** menonshyam@yahoo.com (shyam menon)
- **Date:** 2002-04-18T20:54:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10986fae.0204181954.71dd7146@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<10986fae.0204161943.1a2c630f@posting.google.com> <a9isp4$qju$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

  I am not sure i understand what you said about the reentrant
programs.From what i gathered the program has to be run in an
environment that supports reentrant programs.So does it have to be run
under CICS  or IMS or can it be run simply on MVS.But i dont think
multithreading is supported in cobol on os/390.We are using VS Cobol 2
and not Enterprise cobol.
  Also is there any compulsion to initialize the working storage
variables beforehand by using value clause in the WS or move
statements in the procedure division because that is one is the
changes we are doing to the code as instructed to make it reentrant.
  And i also did not understand the phrase that the program cannot
modify itself.Are they talking to some run time changes made in the
code by the program itself?
 Please throw some light on these topics,
Thanking You in advance,

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reentrant programs in Cobol

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-04-19T04:43:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c1e75c$c933b0a0$3f93f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<10986fae.0204161943.1a2c630f@posting.google.com> <a9isp4$qju$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <10986fae.0204181954.71dd7146@posting.google.com>`

```
Rentrant and mutithreading are related but somewhat different topics. A
reentrant Cobol program gives each user their own copy of working storage
without you having to worry about the details of how it does it.  The
compiler could generate code to do it, or the runtime environment could do
it, or they could both work together to do it.  Basically your original
working storage is kept somewhere and never modified.  For each user,
storage is dynamically acquired and intialized from the original copy. MVS
supplies the GETMAIN and STORAGE macros to support this and they can be
used by subsystems such as CICS or IMS or whatever to support reentrant
programs. Each user interacts with your program but modifies only their
copy of working storage.  Also your program cannot modify its code with
nasty things like ALTER GO TO. If you were coding in assembler you would
have to worry about all these issues, but COBOL is a higher level language
and all you need is the proper compiler and link edit options and you get a
reentrant program. No muss no fuss.

The lastest IBM compiler does support some forms of mulitasking for
interacting with JAVA. Some versions on the PROGRAM-ID clause let you add
"AS INITIAL" but that is used more often for serially reueable programs. 
You might also read up on the LOCAL-STORAGE SECTION.  I beleive the
programmers guide has an example of a recursive program that uses local
storage.  All of these are somewaht related but different.

Some older compilers used with CICS required extra coding to support
reentrancy. IIRC something like "SERVICE RELOAD".

shyam menon <menonshyam@yahoo.com> wrote in article
<10986fae.0204181954.71dd7146@posting.google.com>...
> Hi,
>   I am not sure i understand what you said about the reentrant
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…

news:<a9isp4$qju$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>...

(eg.
> > IBM COBOL for this-and-that) *and* compile with the RENT compiler
option
> > *and* link-edit with the RENT link-edit attribute (which will be your
> > default if you don't override things), then you need do NOTHING special
in
> > your program to insure that it is "truly" re-entrant.  If you run the
> > program in any MVS (OS/390 or z/OS) environment that supports reentrant
> > programs, then everything should work "fine".
> > 
> > HOWEVER, there was a restriction in VS COBOL II (and early versions of
IBM
> > COBOL for this-and-that) on "multiple" copies of SEPARATE run-units
under
> > the same "task".  See
> > 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > NOTE: For the "new" multi-thread support, you need to use PL/I,
Assembler,
> > or USS services to "start" the simultaneous threads.
> > 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

require
> > > a driver like IMS or CICS to handle the MVS tasks right?

uses

…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reentrant programs in Cobol

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-04-19T05:02:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c1e75f$61d14aa0$3f93f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<10986fae.0204161943.1a2c630f@posting.google.com> <a9isp4$qju$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <10986fae.0204181954.71dd7146@posting.google.com>`

```
Here is a thread from bit.listserv.ibm-main that discusses this subject
(retrieved from google).  Tom Ross is IBM's Cobol expert.

All messages from thread	

Message 1 in thread	
From: Tom Ross (tomross@VNET.IBM.COM) Subject: Fw: help running OS390 cobol
code from java  Newsgroups: bit.listserv.ibm-main	
View this article only	
Date: 2001-10-26 15:20:19 PST  	
<hightek@home.com> wrote in message
news:<PT0B7.314104$j65.83369192@news4.rdc1.on.home.com>...
> Hi there...I am new to this newsgroup, but thought I would post a problem
> that has recently come up at a work site.  The problem appears to be
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> and COBOL programs), and the problems with multi-threading, or making
them
> thread-safe (?), but I don't fully understand the problem.  I know the
Java
> world running under OS390 is very multithreaded....what happens to this
when
> running these COBOL modules?  I am a little unsure of the resource
> requirements of these programs and how they are actually executed (ie;
under
> the USS side, via HTTP and/or WebSphere servers, or MVS?).

I know this is an older post, but I have been very busy working on our new
COBOL compiler which supports multi-threading!  Thread-safe means that
multiple copies of a program can run in the same run unit (or enclave) and
share a single run-time library.  Things like control blocks and storage
must be allocated on a per invocation basis, rather than per program.

Anyway, current COBOL cannot run in more than one thread today, so we
diagnose
this at run time, thus the 'COBOL found in multiple threads' message.

The good news is that soon you will be able to run COBOL programs in
multiple threads.  WebSphere and Java are multi-thread environments, and
COBOL needs to be able to play those games too, so that's what we have
been working on!  Coming soon to an IBM stand near you...

Cheers,
TomR              >> COBOL is the Language of the Future! <<

----------------------------------------------------------------------
For IBM-MAIN subscribe / signoff / archive access instructions,
send email to listserv@bama.ua.edu with the message: GET IBM-MAIN INFO
Search the archives at http://bama.ua.edu/archives/ibm-main.html

Message 2 in thread	
From: Charles Hottel (chottel@cpcug.org) Subject: Re: Fw: help running
OS390 cobol code from java  Newsgroups: bit.listserv.ibm-main	
View this article only	
Date: 2001-10-26 20:05:23 PST  	
Can anyone explain to me how "thread-safe" differs from "reentrant"?

My understanding of reentrant is one copy of the code and constants with
separate dynamically acquired storage and registers for each invocation,
which I have always thought of as a "thread".  Thanks.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reentrant programs in Cobol

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-19T16:33:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a9q2et$470$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<10986fae.0204161943.1a2c630f@posting.google.com> <a9isp4$qju$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <10986fae.0204181954.71dd7146@posting.google.com>`

```
I think one of your problems is that you don't understand the difference
between the *COBOL code* not modifying itself versus the *generated OBJECT
code* not modifying itself.  From VS COBOL II on (even though the VS COBOL
II compiler - your compiler - is no longer supported by IBM), the RENT
compiler option makes the COMPILER create (no matter WHAT your source code
does) ASSEMBLER code (more accurately object code) that meets all the
requirements of the RENT link-edit option.

As far as the "need" to use IMS or CICS - or something else, I guess, I
don't know "where you are coming from".  Why do you care about re-entrant
code?
 - Do you have an environment  (such as CICS) that requires this for your
program?
 - Do you have a "user requirement" that makes you THINK you need to know
about this?
 - or is this just an "education" process that you want to understand about?

    ***

Bottom-Line: If you don't "know" about reentrant code, then the chances are
you don't NEED to know about it.  If you have a requirement to produce
re-entrant code, then let the compiler do it for you - and you don't need to
modify your COBOL source code at all.
```

## Thread: Indexed Files in OUTPUT with appends... — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Indexed Files in OUTPUT with appends...

- **From:** "Nicod���me" <maitrenicodeme@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-17T11:41:01+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a9jg5d$una$1@s1.read.news.oleane.net>`

```
Hello,

First, I beg your pardon if my english is bad, I'm french...

A friend have a program in COBOL. He want to obtain an indexed file in
output, but in append... So he have use the command EXTENDS, but the
compiler make an error...
So, if someone could tell me how to use indexed file in output with append,
He will be nice :) !

Thanks,

Nicod�me.
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed Files in OUTPUT with appends...

- **From:** "Gianni Spano" <softline2000@tin.it>
- **Date:** 2002-04-17T10:17:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<65b0287c26bef7ec2a6bfd88036ed356.40368@mygate.mailgate.org>`
- **References:** `<a9jg5d$una$1@s1.read.news.oleane.net>`

```
Hi

You need to define a primary key and, if you want, add some alternate 
keys. 
Here is a little example of coding:

FILE-CONTROL.
    SELECT   DATAFILE   ASSIGN TO "DATA.INX"
                        ORGANIZATION INDEXED
                        ACCESS DYNAMIC
                        RECORD KEY KEY1
                        ALTERNATE RECORD KEY KEY2
                        DUPLICATES
                        FILE STATUS  IS FILE-STATUS.
...
...
DATA DIVISION.
FILE SECTION.

FD   DATAFILE
     LABEL RECORD STANDARD.

01   FD-REC.
     02  KEY1.
         05  CUSTOMER-KEY          PIC X(6).
     02  KEY2.
         05  CUSTOMER-NAME         PIC X(40).
...
...

Hope in this help
     
Gianni

#### ↳ Re: Indexed Files in OUTPUT with appends...

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-04-17T11:46:16+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<cgdv8.22313$bn2.2858@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`

```
Nicod�me <maitrenicodeme@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:a9jg5d$una$1@s1.read.news.oleane.net...
> So, if someone could tell me how to use indexed file in output with
append,
> He will be nice :) !

To write to an indexed file you just open the file I-O, set the key (either
a relative key or key value) and do a WRITE. If the record does not exist in
the file, it will be added. If the record does exist, you will get an
INVALID KEY condition or the appropriate file status if you do not specify
an INVALID KEY clause in the WRITE.

OPEN APPEND only applies to sequential I-O and does not apply to files with
ORGANIZATION INDEXED or ORGANIZATION RELATIVE.  (This might be the cause of
the compile-time error).

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed Files in OUTPUT with appends...

- **From:** "Alberto Magalh���es" <aamagalhaes@oninet.pt>
- **Date:** 2002-04-17T23:48:12+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a9ku2q$dfm$1@news.oninet.pt>`
- **References:** `<a9jg5d$una$1@s1.read.news.oleane.net>`

```
Hi, Nicod�me, the OPEN EXTEND is for sequential files.
For you to make a append to an INDEX FILE, you only have to open it in
INPUT-OUTPUT, and write it, look at your keys, to see how records will be
'arrange it'.
AAM

## Thread: Experiences in Migrating IBM COBOL to Language Environment? — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Experiences in Migrating IBM COBOL to Language Environment?

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2002-04-17T10:48:40-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CBDB578.CA448FF3@attglobal.net>`

```
Finally, folks are starting to recognize the need to use Language
Environment as the run time support for their "old COBOL" programs.
While it is still unclear (at least to me) exactly when programs
compiled with OS/VS COBOL and / or VS COBOL II, and linked with the old
run time libraries will stop running, it seems that recent announcement
for CICS and DB2 are finally getting companies' attention.

Programs compiled with the old compilers are supported in execution by
IBM if Language Environment is the run time in use.

Literally thousands of load modules that exist and still execute need to
be migrated to LE support.  The basic method is to run the Program
Binder (Linkage Editor) to replace library routines from the old run
times with equivalent library routines from the LE run time.  We are
ready to do this, from a mechanical standpoint.

What we wonder about is what will happen when we try to execute those
new modules in place of the old ones.  What has been your experience?
Has (almost) everything run first try?  Did you have to go one program
at a time?  What kinds of problems did you encounter?  Do you have
advice for those who are heading down this path?

Thanks,
Colin Campbell
```

#### ↳ Re: Experiences in Migrating IBM COBOL to Language Environment?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-17T18:03:42-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a9kv0g$rn6$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3CBDB578.CA448FF3@attglobal.net>`

```
Colin,
  Have you looked at:
    "2.1.2 Taking an inventory of your applications"
at
    http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/IGY3MG00/2.1.2

and particularly
    "2.1.2.3 Assigning complexity ratings"
at
    http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/IGY3MG00/2.1.2.3

The bottom-Line is that USUALLY, the "difficulty" depends on
  - what run-time library you are moving from
  - how many "undocumented extension" your programs rely on
  - interactions with other languages and products

I certainly STRONGLY recommend *full* system (and load) tests to insure
expected "results," performance, and (production) debugging situations.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Experiences in Migrating IBM COBOL to Language Environment?

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2002-04-18T13:53:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CBF3230.34E04F87@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<3CBDB578.CA448FF3@attglobal.net> <a9kv0g$rn6$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill,
I've done a migration at my "home shop", but it was a pretty well behaved
world, where we had already migrated to VS COBOL II run time, compiled both
OS/VS COBOL and VS COBOL II with the RES option for many years, and used a
Standards CHecker program integrated into the compiler to enforce our rules.
This shop is about 99% LE supported, and the remainng batch job steps are
being knocked down, although slowly.

Now, I'm visiting strange new worlds, or maybe it's parallel universes.
Another large enterprise for whom my company does applications support is
looking at their COBOL situation, across the entire enterprise.  Some LPARs
are CICS / IMS-DC, and DB2.  Others (I'm told) are more like my world, with
IMS-TM & IMS-DC plus DB2.  I know very little about the CICS universe, but
this shop has had two major problems trying to move up from OS/VS COBOL with
their two of their applications.  Naturally, the programmers and the customers
are pretty gun shy right now.

I've read the Migration Guide cover to cover (and your book, too, plus one
that Prince Software sent me), but I'll certainly go back for a refresher
course.

If you know of anyone I might contact person-to-person, I'd like to try that
route, too.
Thanks,
Colin

"William M. Klein" wrote:

> Colin,
>   Have you looked at:
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

#### ↳ Re: Experiences in Migrating IBM COBOL to Language Environment?

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2002-04-18T02:09:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020417220958.26890.00004800@mb-cj.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3CBDB578.CA448FF3@attglobal.net>`

```
>From: Colin Campbell cmcampb@attglobal.net 
>Date: 4/17/02 1:48 PM Eastern Daylight Time

> What has been your experience?
>Has (almost) everything run first try?  Did you have to go one program
>at a time?  What kinds of problems did you encounter?  Do you have
>advice for those who are heading down this path?
>

We ran into 2 main problems.  The first with CICS programs because the maps
were not cleared by the program before display and we had garbage in them. 
There is a LE setting to handle this but I don't remember it off hand (we did
the LE conversion as part of our Y2K chaos).

The 2nd was U4035 errors (I remember 4035 - it could be 4038 - or even both).
Anyway what we found was if you define a file in a SELECT and do not have it in
your JCL you get one of the 2 errors above.  The other error is when the length
of the file in the DCB does not equal the length of the 01 level in the
program.  The SELECT error was prevelent with output files defined in programs
but not in JCL, it's the open that triggers is - you don't have to write
anything to it.

As part of this whole process we also tried to get everything AMODE=31 but not
everything cooperated.  We still have a few programs at 24.  One thing that I
did find out when working on getting some of these uncooperative messes changed
was that if you have the DD as REPORT the compile isn't happy unless you have
it as UT-S-REPORT.  Leave off the UT-S and it won't compile.  This could be
caused more by the newer version of Cobol we are using than LE, but if you are
changing compilers you should make note.  Which reminds me of one other thing -
again more to version of compiler than LE.  BMS maps used to assemble with the
attribute byte receiving a suffix of 'F' as well as 'A' (I think one was a
redefine of the other).  Programmers past used to redefine their maps using the
'F' suffix for the attribute byte.  A lot of the maps use the abreviation of
SEL for the selection field on the map and SELF is not allowed by our Cobol
compiler.  Changing to SELA works just fine.

Other than above it wasn't too painful.  Took about a week after the whole
thing was in production to get the rest of the bugs out that weren't obvious at
the time of conversion.

Eileen
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Experiences in Migrating IBM COBOL to Language Environment?

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2002-04-18T13:41:09-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CBF2F64.5DF74FF3@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<3CBDB578.CA448FF3@attglobal.net> <20020417220958.26890.00004800@mb-cj.aol.com>`

```
Thanks for your update.  At least one of the shops I have to work in is a CICS
shop, and I have almost no experience in that environment, so this will help.  I
think that's the STROAGE option, and it can be set just to apply to the CICS
regions, if I recall correctly.

With regard to your problem with SEL concatenated to F (SELF), you can specify an
option WORD with a value of NOOO (no object oriented) to make COBOL ignore the
reserved words for Object-Oriented COBOL, including SELF, SUPER, METHOD, etc.

There is also a WORD(CICS) that will ignore reserved words that are not supposed to
be used with CICS programs, like FD, SD, SORT, etc.

You can't use both of these special table together, though.  It is possible to make
custom reserved word tables for your installation, and if your systems programmer
did so, you could both keep out the invalid CICS words and the OO words.

Best of luck!
Colin

YukonMama wrote:

> >From: Colin Campbell cmcampb@attglobal.net
> >Date: 4/17/02 1:48 PM Eastern Daylight Time
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> Eileen
```

Having actually used CAPSIL myself, I can confirm that it is a life
assurance policy management system.  It was written in CICS and COBOL
74, originally using VSAM files, but subsequently a converted version
using DB2 tables like VSAM files was developed.  I can't fully
remember what the initials stand for but I remember that it was
developed by CAPSCO back in the 1980s, or maybe even earlier, CAPSCO's
and CAPSIL's initials were in part derived from the name Canadian
Pacific.  CAPSCO was a Canadian company.  I believe CAPSIL and perhaps
CAPSCO were taken over by PAXUS and that the company CONTINUUM may
have owned it at one time.  I can't say for sure who owns it at
present, but think that it may be PAXUS.  I think Ingenium is a
company specialising in web-based systems and with a product of that
name that has been used by Woolwich Life to provide a more up to date
front end.

Regards, Robert

> enlightened us:

<a id="t-6b5798e976"></a>
## Thread: HELP: Maximum size of PIC X fields ? — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### HELP: Maximum size of PIC X fields ?

- **From:** mbruyninckx@hotmail.com (Michael Bruyninckx)
- **Date:** 2002-04-18T07:35:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b66b3515.0204180635.664e54e4@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello,

We are trying to get something done for a schoolproject... the idea is
to read some text in a variable (typically PIC X variable), and then
do some counting on that string.

If I define the variable like this:

01 MyVariable PIC X(1600)

And if I then do an accept of this MyVariable and write it back to the
screen it seems that it only accepted a patition of the text I typed,
for example if I would have typed :
"hello, world, how are you doing, this is my nice little nifty program
that actually doesn't work, but it should work, because where is the
logic in programming else ?"

then it would only display the first 64 characters being : 

"hello, world, how are you doing, this is my nice little nifty p".


Has anyone got a idea why this is ?

PS: the machine we run this on is a typical Intel P4 running WinXP,
the cobol compiler is from CA-Realia.

Michael & Sindy
```

#### ↳ Re: HELP: Maximum size of PIC X fields ?

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-04-18T16:13:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a9mr1l$58o$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b66b3515.0204180635.664e54e4@posting.google.com>`

```

On 18-Apr-2002, mbruyninckx@hotmail.com (Michael Bruyninckx) wrote:

> If I define the variable like this:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> "hello, world, how are you doing, this is my nice little nifty p".

I'm really surprised your compiler compiled without giving you a warning. 
Always read your compiler messages.
But you appear to have inferred your answer yourself.  There IS a maximum
size of pic x fields.
I'm not sure how your compiler does accepts, but if you want a really long
text variable, use an array/table of characters.
```

```
Pic x(1600) is a perfectly acceptable picture clause.
I suspect something with either the accept or display
command. It works as expected using my micro-focus
compiler. Could you post the entire program code so we
can see what you are doing?


"Michael Bruyninckx" <mbruyninckx@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:b66b3515.0204180635.664e54e4@posting.google.com...

#### ↳ Re: HELP: Maximum size of PIC X fields ?

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-04-19T02:58:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c1e74e$05b68e80$98c2f943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<b66b3515.0204180635.664e54e4@posting.google.com>`

```
I believe this is a limitation of ACCEPT on your compiler. IIRC the IBM
ACCEPT has a limit of 114 characters and this is probably different for
different compilers.

If your max is 64 characters then define an array or table containing 25
occurrances of PIC X(64)  (as 25*64=1600 and this is probably not a
coincidence!) then code a loop that accepts one occurance at a time until
the table is full.  Then process it.

<a id="t-a33852cd7f"></a>
## Thread: COBOL (lack of) optimization question — 8 msgs

### COBOL (lack of) optimization question

- **From:** Robert Ngan <rngan@csc.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-18T11:08:00-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.1728ab5cd07aa011989681@news.csc.com>`

```
Sorry about the wide post, required for the listing below.

I'm an assembler program (you COBOL programmers are the dinosaurs, we on the other hand have not yet decided whether it is safe to 
crawl out of the oceans yet!), I seldom write any COBOL so to verify that my logic is correctly coded, I use the LIST option and 
check out the generated assembler code. I was amazed when the following code sequence:

           05  WA-ACTIVITY-ID            PIC  9(8) COMP.
               88  WA-SET-ADDR-ACTIVITY  VALUE 4 6 29.  
       LINKAGE SECTION.
           :
           IF (WA-ACTIVITY-ID = 4 OR 6 OR 29)
              EVALUATE WA-SAVE-PARM-POS
           :

generated:

000719  IF                                                                                                       
    000506  5870 204B               L    7,75(0,2)               WA-ACTIVITY-ID
    00050A  1F66                    SLR  6,6
    00050C  9067 D0D8               STM  6,7,216(13)             TS2=0
    000510  D507 D0D8 A094          CLC  216(8,13),148(10)       TS2=0                             PGMLIT AT +128
    000516  4780 B23A               BC   8,570(0,11)             GN=14(000542)
    00051A  5870 204B               L    7,75(0,2)               WA-ACTIVITY-ID
    00051E  1F66                    SLR  6,6
    000520  9067 D0D8               STM  6,7,216(13)             TS2=0
    000524  D507 D0D8 A084          CLC  216(8,13),132(10)       TS2=0                             PGMLIT AT +112
    00052A  4780 B23A               BC   8,570(0,11)             GN=14(000542)
    00052E  5870 204B               L    7,75(0,2)               WA-ACTIVITY-ID
    000532  1F66                    SLR  6,6
    000534  9067 D0D8               STM  6,7,216(13)             TS2=0
    000538  D507 D0D8 A07C          CLC  216(8,13),124(10)       TS2=0                             PGMLIT AT +104
    00053E  4770 B256               BC   7,598(0,11)             GN=13(00055E)
    000542                 GN=14    EQU  *

My questions are:

(1) Why is the field being expanded to a doubleword value?
(2) Why didn't the optimizer eliminate the 2nd and 3rd L/SLR/STM instruction sequences?

If we zero register 6 (i.e. the high order 1/2 of the doublword), and the high order 1/2 of the literal is zero, the first 4 bytes 
of the compare are redundant!

When WA-ACTIVIYT-ID is signed, the compiler generates:

000719  IF
    0004F8  5860 204B               L    6,75(0,2)               WA-ACTIVITY-ID
    0004FC  8E60 0020               SRDA 6,32(0)
    000500  9067 D0D8               STM  6,7,216(13)             TS2=0
    000504  D507 D0D8 A094          CLC  216(8,13),148(10)       TS2=0                             PGMLIT AT +128
    00050A  4780 B232               BC   8,562(0,11)             GN=14(00053A)
    00050E  5860 204B               L    6,75(0,2)               WA-ACTIVITY-ID
    000512  8E60 0020               SRDA 6,32(0)
    000516  9067 D0D8               STM  6,7,216(13)             TS2=0
    00051A  D507 D0D8 A084          CLC  216(8,13),132(10)       TS2=0                             PGMLIT AT +112
    000520  4780 B232               BC   8,562(0,11)             GN=14(00053A)
    000524  5860 204B               L    6,75(0,2)               WA-ACTIVITY-ID
    000528  8E60 0020               SRDA 6,32(0)
    00052C  9067 D0D8               STM  6,7,216(13)             TS2=0
    000530  D507 D0D8 A07C          CLC  216(8,13),124(10)       TS2=0                             PGMLIT AT +104
    000536  4770 B24E               BC   7,590(0,11)             GN=13(000556)
    00053A                 GN=14    EQU  *
000720  EVALUATE

On suggestion from a COBOL programmer, I added the 88 level and used:

           IF WA-SET-ADDR-ACTIVITY

This made no difference. This is COBOL for OS/390 and VM 2.2.0, with OPT(STD) and TRUNC(BIN) (required since our system runs under 
CICS and IMS, and may use DB2).

Using TRUNC(STD), it generates:

000719  IF                                                                     
    0004A4  5850 204B               L    5,75(0,2)               WA-ACTIVITY-ID
    0004A8  4950 A026               CH   5,38(0,10)              PGMLIT AT +18 
    0004AC  4780 B1D4               BC   8,468(0,11)             GN=14(0004C0) 
    0004B0  4950 A022               CH   5,34(0,10)              PGMLIT AT +14 
    0004B4  4780 B1D4               BC   8,468(0,11)             GN=14(0004C0) 
    0004B8  4950 A020               CH   5,32(0,10)              PGMLIT AT +12 
    0004BC  4770 B1EA               BC   7,490(0,11)             GN=13(0004D6) 
    0004C0                 GN=14    EQU  *

which is closer to what I would expect.

So what does OPT actually do?
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL (lack of) optimization question

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2002-04-18T14:06:21-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CBF354D.60056245@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1728ab5cd07aa011989681@news.csc.com>`

```
Robert,
I'm Colin Campbell, also at CSC (TMG, Long Beach, CA).

That's what you get for looking at generated code!  Questions!

I would refer you to the Performance Tuning manuals for COBOL, available at IBM's COBOL Web Site:
http://www-4.ibm.com/software/ad/cobol/

I think you definitely want to use TRUNC(OPT) nearly all the time.

Also, are you sure you specified the OPTIMIZE option?  And that the TEST option was not set (it overrides OPTIMIZE)?  It is hard to
believe that the generated code is so poor, but maybe that's why IBM recommends using OPT as a performance option.

Contact me thru company e-mail if you feel the need, but watch out for the Colin Campbell in Australia!
=====
Robert Ngan wrote:

> Sorry about the wide post, required for the listing below.
>
…[86 more quoted lines elided]…
> CSC Financial Services Group
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL (lack of) optimization question

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2002-04-18T14:44:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0204181344.17439122@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1728ab5cd07aa011989681@news.csc.com>`

```
Hi Robert,

Haven't used BAL in ages, but I'll give it a try. Number 1, IBM has
admitted performance problems with TRUNC(BIN) and reccommends that it
be avoided, if possible.

Don't know why you get a dblword with 9(8); should be a word, could be
the TRUNC option. BTW, do you need 8 digits for that field? If you can
keep it below 5 the compiler is supposed to allocate 2 bytes.
 
The rules I always (almost) follow are:

Signed  numeric pics, e.g. S9(004)
Signed literals, e.g. +0005, -0029
Leading zeros in lits, where reasonable, to match length w/pic. See
above.

They're supposed to have positive performance implications, but some
of them may have been anticipated in later compiler releases.

Regards, Jack.

- **Message-ID:** `<%KJv8.41218$XV5.3461901@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1728ab5cd07aa011989681@news.csc.com>`

```

"Robert Ngan" <rngan@csc.com> wrote in message
news:MPG.1728ab5cd07aa011989681@news.csc.com...
> Sorry about the wide post, required for the listing below.
>
> I'm an assembler program (you COBOL programmers are the dinosaurs, we on
the other hand have not yet decided whether it is safe to
> crawl out of the oceans yet!), I seldom write any COBOL so to verify that
my logic is correctly coded,

<irrelevant part snipped>


>
> So what does OPT actually do?

You've written some COBOL code, so you are now a COBOL programmer.
Congratulations.

```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL (lack of) optimization question

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-04-19T03:38:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c1e753$afb71e40$98c2f943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1728ab5cd07aa011989681@news.csc.com>`

```
First of all you said you were checking your logic for correctness. It
seems strange to have a pic 9(8) field when the three values you are
checking for are two digits or less.  Also if the program is correct and
runs quickly enough then you do not have a performance problem.

According to the IBM COBOL Language reference: 1-4 digits in PIC generates
2 bytes, 5-9 digits in PIC generates 4 bytes, and 10-18 in PIC generates 8
bytes.  I believe the compiler "knows" that WA-ACTIVITY-ID is a fullword
because it uses a Load  instruction to put it register 7.  So why is it
creating a double word item for comparision? The only reason I can think of
is that the compiler does "not know" the maximum size numeric literal that
you may want to test at some later point in your code.  What if you decide
to code "IF WA-ACTIVITY-CODE IS LESS THAN 9999999999"? That is ten 9's
which is hex '2540BE3FF' which is larger than a fullword.  I think the
short answer is you cannot expect the compiler to beat human coding in all
cases, as it is sometimes generating code to handle the general case.

So why does it generate the extra  L/SLR/STM instructions?  I can only
guess that it generates code for the second and third tests without
"remembering" what it did for the first test.  Only the compiler writer can
really answer your questions, and as many compiler options "interact" even
he/she would need more information.  From my reading of the manual and from
your tests the TRUNC option you select seems to be a key item.

#### ↳ Re: COBOL (lack of) optimization question

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-04-19T05:07:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c1e760$1a6e56c0$3f93f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1728ab5cd07aa011989681@news.csc.com>`

```
Maybe the compiler is smarter than we think. In most programs 5% of the
code causes most of the overhead.  If your test is in the other 95% maybe
the compiler figures it is not worth optimizing.  Try PERFORMing your test
several million times and see if it gets more attention from the compiler.

Robert Ngan <rngan@csc.com> wrote in article
<MPG.1728ab5cd07aa011989681@news.csc.com>...
> Sorry about the wide post, required for the listing below.
>

the other hand have not yet decided whether it is safe to

code sequence:
> 
>            05  WA-ACTIVITY-ID            PIC  9(8) COMP.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

>     000506  5870 204B               L    7,75(0,2)

>     00050A  1F66                    SLR  6,6
>     00050C  9067 D0D8               STM  6,7,216(13)             TS2=0
>     000510  D507 D0D8 A094          CLC  216(8,13),148(10)       TS2=0   
                         PGMLIT AT +128

high order 1/2 of the literal is zero, the first 4 bytes

#### ↳ Re: COBOL (lack of) optimization question

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-04-19T07:09:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CBFC2BF.A051A89A@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1728ab5cd07aa011989681@news.csc.com>`

```
Robert Ngan wrote:
> 
> Sorry about the wide post, required for the listing below.
> (snip)

You might try recompiling with OPT(FULL), which should give you more
optimization that OPT(STD).  You should also try using TRUNC(OPT) for
optimized truncation processing.  I've used TRUNC(OPT) in CICS
programs and CICS programs with DB2 without any problems.  TRUNC(BIN)
is well-known to generate a lot of less-than-efficient code.  
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL (lack of) optimization question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-19T16:24:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a9q1v8$sv9$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1728ab5cd07aa011989681@news.csc.com>`

```
A) IBM has (slightly) modified its recommendation concering TRUNC(BIN) and
"other" languages/products.  Please read the "recommendation" at:
  http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3pg00/2.4.52

B) As also indicated in that same section, *IF* you are using the latest
compiler, you can use TRUNC(OPT) for your entire program and use COMP-5 for
any *specific* data-items that might (realistically) have values outside the
picture clause.

  ***

IBM is quite clear that even with their latest compiler changes, using
TRUNC(BIN) is *always* the poorest performer (for programs that reference a
lot of binary items).

Have you read the section in the programming guide on performance options
at:
  http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3pg00/7.1.5.1

and/or the section on "7.1 Tuning your program"

at:
 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3pg00/7.1

Bottom-Line:
  Don't use BAD coding or features (such as TRUNC(BIN)) and expect the OPT
compiler option to do as much as "good coding" will.
```

---

- **From:** Larry Lindstrom <larryl@aracnet.com>

```
Hi Experts:

   I've been cutting C and C++ for many years, mostly 
industrial control and web apps on Unix systems.  Now 
a client wants my skills, but this technical project 
has been built with "LM Cobol"(?) and runs under DOS.  

   I need to get up to speed on Cobol as fast as I 
can.  I'd like to have some experience when I talk 
to some technical managers next week.  

   I've been to Deja, is the Jim Ingles book the one 
to get?  What is the title, version number and ISBN?  

   I have no Cobol compiler.  Is there any free, or 
inexpensive, environments I can get?  Windows, DOS 
running under Windows, Linux or Solaris (intel) would 
be fine.  It probably wouldn't need to be a production 
quality package.  

   I guess Worx Press packages a Cobol in one of their 
books.  

                                                Thanks
                                                Larry
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Recommend Cobol Book, and Compiler, for C++ Programmer Please

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-19T12:15:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mTTv8.14561$R33.627925@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3CBF3EAB.A9856B04@aracnet.com> <XqIv8.19214$L1.1511432@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <TWSv8.166$t65.21@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <3CC009D3.9B2ECA4C@aracnet.com>`

```
Yep - that is the way to do it. :)

I would have advised you to download the Fujitsu compiler, which is an
outdated version but is an unrestricted Windows based compiler that is
perfect to learn on. (Well, it is if you know and remember that the SCREEN
Section is compiler specific and not found in every compiler, and ignore
their
wierd editor and IDE. <grin>)

Or pick up Thank Hubble's book (COBOL in 24 HOURS I think) or Mo Bundings
COBOL in 21 days book at your local Barnes and Noble. This is much less
expensive
than $400 and provides pretty much the same stuff.

After you get a little feel for the language, you (or your client) can
decide what Compiler
is most appropriate for the task(s) at hand and the development environment
available.
For example, if you are deploying on Windows and using just VSAM (indexed)
files,
then it is possible that even the free Fujitsu compiler might be
appropriate. If you are
on an AS/400, then only the AS/400 Language Environment compiler is an
appropriate
choice. (And everything you learned about screens is out the Windows --
welcome
to the world of workstation files. :)

The important thing is to learn what the language really provides - which is
a powerful lot
for business style processing. It isn't appropriate to write say, HTTPD
Servers in COBOL,
but believe it or not, people have done it. (It's a fact, the Wang VS Web
Server was originally
written in COBOL. I think the current source is BASIC though, since the
author did not
have COBOL-85 available,with nice things like POINTER variables and ADDRESS
OF...)

It is reasonable and practical, and often accomplished that a basic
accounting system can be
written from a standing start in two to four weeks though.

Basically, the best advice that I or anyone else can give you is to ask
questions, and keep asking them
until you understand the answers. And good luck. :)

-Paul

Larry Lindstrom wrote:

Well if you really want to get into a sophisticated debugger - then you want either
Fujitsu or Micro Focus. Others will have to give you the details, but I'm not sure if
there is a debugger with the CD that comes with Thane's 'TYCI24H' - there may well be.
Certainly Fujitsu has a debugger with POWER COBOL.

I mustn't be partisan - but the debugger (Animator) with Net Express is superb. If you
can swing it that you can get a copy of Net Express University Edition (about $100) then
that would be another possible route to go - basically you have to qualify as a
'student', (depending on the broad definition of that word ?). The University Edition
contains ALMOST what is in the current V 3.1. of Net Express, excluding things like Java
classes which were added to the latter. (Yes it's got OO - you ex-C++ man <G>. Same goes
for Fujitsu).

You should anticipate that as the University Edition is geared for students it CAN'T be
used for production systems - but that doesn't detract from its usefulness as a VERY
sophisticated learning tool.

In referring to sites for examples I should also have mentioned microfocus.com;
similarly, I haven't checked, but it's a fair bet AcuCorp also has examples for
AcuCOBOL.

I gather the Pro COBOL from M/F is quite good, and will certainly follow the style of
other M/F compilers/documentation. Funnily enough, somebody from Australia told me a
week ago that M/F had stopped distributing Pro COBOL.

Jimmy, Calgary AB

> "James J. Gavan" wrote:
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>                                                Larry
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Recommend Cobol Book, and Compiler, for C++ Programmer Please

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-19T23:51:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<x42w8.14959$R33.708536@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3CBF3EAB.A9856B04@aracnet.com> <XqIv8.19214$L1.1511432@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <TWSv8.166$t65.21@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <3CC009D3.9B2ECA4C@aracnet.com> <mTTv8.14561$R33.627925@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC045C9.2F9964EF@aracnet.com> <3CC0473D.87C4D7A1@shaw.ca> <3CC08A31.C448ECBE@aracnet.com>`

```
MMM- well, Micro Focus is a great compiler, but I do not know what is in the 'Personal Cobol' set. 
I do know however that the MicroFocus NetExpress 'University' package is really good. Nice debugger,
nice compiler, limits are very appropriate for a student package. Not a current version, but still very
very nice.  Especially the debugger - though more on that below.

On the other hand, I would probably send the $100 Personal Cobol back and simply download
the Fujitsu compiler. Just take 10 minutes to learn the command line utilities instead of the 
IDE. 

One thing, though in a production environment a debugger is pretty much absolutely essential,
in a learning situation, you will almost never need to use a debugger. Heck, I doubt most COBOL
programmers use debuggers anyway, at least on the Mainframe. Workstation COBOL is definitely
a different world!

As for R/M COBOL under DOS - mmm - I would look seriously at AcuCOBOL. They are a bit of a 
nuisance with runtime charges, but their compiler is fast, supported, and has switches that enable very
HIGH compatibility with R/M Cobol. You can also develop on any platform, targeting deployment on 
any other platform.  Given that developing under DOS is a painful and time wasting experience these
days, that would be a heck of a option to me. 

-Paul


"Larry Lindstrom" <larryl@aracnet.com> wrote in message news:3CC08A31.C448ECBE@aracnet.com...

…[26 more quoted lines elided]…

Paul,

Back track a bit :-

1. The 'possible' employer uses DOS RM/COBOL. Unless part of their
attraction to the candidate is his knowledge of C++ and perhaps *maybe*
they might just be interested into getting into OO, using him ..... ???
On the other hand, if he goes in their proclaiming "Compiler X is the
best thing since sliced bread, and you should switch to it", he is going
to be as popular as a pork pie in a synagogue <G>

2. Secondly, and not totally related to 1 above, he wants to get to know
COBOL - getting the best for the cheapest.

3. Disagree with you about debugging. Learning or production, a
sophisticated debugger  is a great tool to have. (Don't believe me - try
using RM/COBOL V2 (ANSI 74) without a debugger. Agreed 20 years old, but
what a pain. In sheer frustration I had to add display statements to
emulate a debugger - and then of course, comment them out (an  * in
Column 7) when it worked. Give me a break. Nuthin' as sophisticated as a
conditional compilation back then). Memory has lapsed, but I think I was
even dependent upon a Mickey Mouse text Editor called Edlin, one line at
a time !

PS: Your message about the 1,001 different languages/tools you use every
day, really cheered me up. I don't ENVY you one bit <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB

Paul Raulerson wrote:

> MMM- well, Micro Focus is a great compiler, but I do not know what is
> in the 'Personal Cobol' set.I do know however that the MicroFocus
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
> >                                                Larry
```

- **References:** `<3CBF3EAB.A9856B04@aracnet.com> <XqIv8.19214$L1.1511432@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <TWSv8.166$t65.21@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <3CC009D3.9B2ECA4C@aracnet.com> <mTTv8.14561$R33.627925@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC045C9.2F9964EF@aracnet.com> <3CC0473D.87C4D7A1@shaw.ca> <3CC08A31.C448ECBE@aracnet.com> <x42w8.14959$R33.708536@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC0B438.A3E08B50@shaw.ca>`

```
Good points Jimmy - 

Usually with clients who want C programmers to write COBOL, they are just looking for two things:
(1) This is the solution you need to make it work and
(2) This is lowest cost I can find to make it work that way. 

You can make a lot of money dealing with one up situations and customers who think they know a lot 
more than they do, but it is not very satisfying. On the other paw, if you go in with (1) and (2), all the 
onus is on you to deliver - in a timely and appropriate manner. ;) 

Re: debuggers - they are available on the mainframe, but they are not all that common. Deskchecking 
is still very important. <grin> Since a lot of what I do winds up on the mainframe one way or another, 
I tend to discount debuggers to a large degree. When you have a really good debugger, like MF or 
Wang's - then they are a perfectly wonderful joy to use. But I learned not to depend on having them around. 
;) 

  "James J. Gavan" <jjgava@shaw.ca> wrote in message news:3CC0B438.A3E08B50@shaw.ca...
  Paul, 
  Back track a bit :- 

  1. The 'possible' employer uses DOS RM/COBOL. Unless part of their attraction to the candidate is his knowledge of C++ and perhaps *maybe*  they might just be interested into getting into OO, using him ..... ???  On the other hand, if he goes in their proclaiming "Compiler X is the best thing since sliced bread, and you should switch to it", he is going to be as popular as a pork pie in a synagogue <G>

  2. Secondly, and not totally related to 1 above, he wants to get to know COBOL - getting the best for the cheapest. 

  3. Disagree with you about debugging. Learning or production, a sophisticated debugger  is a great tool to have. (Don't believe me - try using RM/COBOL V2 (ANSI 74) without a debugger. Agreed 20 years old, but what a pain. In sheer frustration I had to add display statements to emulate a debugger - and then of course, comment them out (an  * in Column 7) when it worked. Give me a break. Nuthin' as sophisticated as a conditional compilation back then). Memory has lapsed, but I think I was even dependent upon a Mickey Mouse text Editor called Edlin, one line at a time ! 

  PS: Your message about the 1,001 different languages/tools you use every day, really cheered me up. I don't ENVY you one bit <G> 

  Jimmy, Calgary AB 

  Paul Raulerson wrote: 

    MMM- well, Micro Focus is a great compiler, but I do not know what is in the 'Personal Cobol' set.I do know however that the MicroFocus NetExpress 'University' package is really good. Nice debugger,nice compiler, limits are very appropriate for a student package. Not a current version, but still veryvery nice.  Especially the debugger - though more on that below. On the other hand, I would probably send the $100 Personal Cobol back and simply downloadthe Fujitsu compiler. Just take 10 minutes to learn the command line utilities instead of theIDE. One thing, though in a production environment a debugger is pretty much absolutely essential,in a learning situation, you will almost never need to use a debugger. Heck, I doubt most COBOLprogrammers use debuggers anyway, at least on the Mainframe. Workstation COBOL is definitelya different world! As for R/M COBOL under DOS - mmm - I would look seriously at AcuCOBOL. They are a bit of anuisance with runtime charges, but their compiler is fast, supported, and has switches that enable veryHIGH compatibility with R/M Cobol. You can also develop on any platform, targeting deployment onany other platform.  Given that developing under DOS is a painful and time wasting experience thesedays, that would be a heck of a option to me. -Paul  "Larry Lindstrom" <larryl@aracnet.com> wrote in message news:3CC08A31.C448ECBE@aracnet.com...> "James J. Gavan" wrote:

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgava@shaw.ca>

- **Message-ID:** `<3CC1D44E.62BF1C2B@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3CBF3EAB.A9856B04@aracnet.com> <XqIv8.19214$L1.1511432@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <TWSv8.166$t65.21@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <3CC009D3.9B2ECA4C@aracnet.com> <mTTv8.14561$R33.627925@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC045C9.2F9964EF@aracnet.com> <3CC0473D.87C4D7A1@shaw.ca> <3CC08A31.C448ECBE@aracnet.com> <x42w8.14959$R33.708536@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC0B438.A3E08B50@shaw.ca> <g14w8.15213$R33.731268@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC1BA5E.C65F32C8@aracnet.com>`

```


Larry Lindstrom wrote:

> > Good points Jimmy -
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Larry,

One thing one of us should have mentioned to you earlier is the 'differences' in COBOL. The COBOL
standards were, from memory, ANSI 68, 74, 85, (and 97 which introduced intrinsic functions) and
currently they are moving (I HOPE !) to COBOL 2002, officially due this December.

Generally speaking, I think you will find that most of the PC/Unix compilers are up to ANSI 85;
some may not have the 'Intrinsics' of '97. Fujitsu and Micro Focus have already introduced MANY
features to be included in COBOL 2002, specifically the topic of OO. They each have their
separate approaches to handling GUIs - not and probably never will be, part of the COBOL
standard. Others may also have added features, but not OO. (If you want to take Japanese
citizenship you can always code in Hitachi in Tokyo - it isn't marketed in the West so we just
don't know what is going on in the Inscrutable Far East <G>).

So you can get a better handle, go to coboreport.com and take a look at Bill Klein's COBOL FAQ.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Recommend Cobol Book, and Compiler, for C++ Programmer Please

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-22T08:05:28-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aa18rp$1vj9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3CBF3EAB.A9856B04@aracnet.com> <XqIv8.19214$L1.1511432@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <TWSv8.166$t65.21@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <3CC009D3.9B2ECA4C@aracnet.com> <mTTv8.14561$R33.627925@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC045C9.2F9964EF@aracnet.com> <3CC0473D.87C4D7A1@shaw.ca> <3CC08A31.C448ECBE@aracnet.com> <x42w8.14959$R33.708536@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC0B438.A3E08B50@shaw.ca> <g14w8.15213$R33.731268@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC1BA5E.C65F32C8@aracnet.com> <3CC1D44E.62BF1C2B@shaw.ca>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgava@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3CC1D44E.62BF1C2B@shaw.ca...
<<One thing one of us should have mentioned to you earlier is the
'differences' in COBOL. The COBOL
standards were, from memory, ANSI 68, 74, 85, (and 97 which introduced
intrinsic functions) >>

The Intrinsic Functions Amendment was completed in 1989 and approved by ANSI
on September 13  of that year.  The Corrections Amendment was completed in
1993 and approved by ANSI on January 31, 1994.  Both were amendments to the
'85 standard and are considered part of it.

<<(If you want to take Japanese citizenship you can always code in Hitachi
in Tokyo - it isn't marketed in the West so we just don't know what is going
on in the Inscrutable Far East <G>).>>

Mostly true.  However, it's my understanding that the Unisys "HMP IX"
(ex-Univac-2200) series "OOCOBOL" compiler was developed in cooperation with
Hitachi and may represent a "western" version of their COBOL compiler, at
least for users of that system.  From what I can tell, this compiler
conforms to the Committee Draft of the 2002 standard from several years ago
(about CD 1.4, perhaps?).

   -Chuck Stevens
```
