###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Recommend Cobol Book, and Compiler, for C++ Programmer Please

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-22T10:59:44-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aa1j2g$28cm$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3CBF3EAB.A9856B04@aracnet.com> <XqIv8.19214$L1.1511432@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <TWSv8.166$t65.21@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <3CC009D3.9B2ECA4C@aracnet.com> <mTTv8.14561$R33.627925@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC045C9.2F9964EF@aracnet.com> <3CC0473D.87C4D7A1@shaw.ca> <3CC08A31.C448ECBE@aracnet.com> <x42w8.14959$R33.708536@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC0B438.A3E08B50@shaw.ca> <g14w8.15213$R33.731268@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC1BA5E.C65F32C8@aracnet.com> <3CC1D44E.62BF1C2B@shaw.ca> <aa18rp$1vj9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3CC43E66.D6F9E2AB@shaw.ca>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3CC43E66.D6F9E2AB@shaw.ca...

<<I recall about three years ago (?) there was an announcement you would be
using Hitachi technology. I don't recognize machine numbers, so I guess the
"HMP IX" is a mainframe/mini ?>>

I would put the IX definitely in the "mainframe/enterprise server" camp;
it's the architectural descendant of the Univac 1108/1110/2200 series,
indisputably the flagships of the Sperry-Univac line.

<<Is the OO COBOL still actively in use or has it bitten the dust ? >>

So far as I know it is still actively marketed and in use.  A brief overview
can be found on the Unisys web site, more specifically at
        http://www.unisys.com/sw/app_dev/oocobol.asp  .

I don't know how widely it has been accepted.  I know almost nothing about
it except for the fact that it was developed in cooperation with Hitachi.

<<Based on Hitachi did it include their 'carbon copy' version of
Smalltalk's collections/dictionaries ?>>

I have little exposure to 2200-related technical issues, as I work on the
"NX" and "MCP/AS" product line (the descendants of the Burroughs Large
Systems).  The last time I worked on a machine of this architecture was in
about 1970, a Univac 1108.  Thus, I'm not equipped to answer your question
adequately.  You might check through the Unisys website for more details.
Or try contacting your local marketing folks; they'd be far better equipped
to answer your specific technical feature questions than I.

<<I know you can't give away company 'top secrets', but is Univac, (whoops,
you can see where my mind is at - that should be Unisys), seriously thinking
about OO - just a quick nod of the head would create some enthusiasm <G>>>

The OOCOBOL compiler has been in the price book for some time (announced
1998), and the development of this compiler was clearly precipitated by
market demand for it.  I would say that Unisys has demonstrably thought
seriously enough about OO to have invested in the development and marketing
of this compiler and the associated support software over a period of
several years!

       -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Recommend Cobol Book, and Compiler, for C++ Programmer Please

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-20T21:04:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yJkw8.23068$o86.920718@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3CBF3EAB.A9856B04@aracnet.com> <XqIv8.19214$L1.1511432@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <TWSv8.166$t65.21@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <3CC009D3.9B2ECA4C@aracnet.com> <mTTv8.14561$R33.627925@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC045C9.2F9964EF@aracnet.com> <3CC0473D.87C4D7A1@shaw.ca> <3CC08A31.C448ECBE@aracnet.com> <x42w8.14959$R33.708536@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC0B438.A3E08B50@shaw.ca> <g14w8.15213$R33.731268@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC1BA5E.C65F32C8@aracnet.com>`

```
Yep. You can probably do all the industrial control in COBOL-85 if you want
to.  Probably easier to
write those modules in C/C++ and link edit them to the COBOL program though.
Depends upon what
you are actually doing with 'industrial control.' If it amounts to
reading/writing a serial port, do it from COBOL.
If it something on the order if processing interrupts from a 1832 device or
something, then obviously, C is
a much better choice. Inbetweens are a tossup. :)

-Paul

- **From:** floydj@netins.net (Floyd Johnson)

```
Whether to use C++ or not may depend on the customers environment.
Generally, C++ would be a better choice for the application (or what we
know of it); but if the contract is for maintenance of an existing
control system written in COBOL, then a switch to C++ would need to be
negotiated with the customer - I certainly would not make a change like
that unilaterally.

Floyd Johnson

   No, the project I was thinking about is written in COBOL, and 
selecting the language for implementing it is not my 
responsibility.  They have other projects, so the COBOL job isn't 
certain.  

   I want COBOL so I can learn to develop in that language.  

   I've been thinking about all the time I wasted shoehorning my 
C code into DOS compatible 64K chunks.  Am I going to have to 
worry about that in RM COBOL for DOS?  

                                                          Thanks
                                                          Larry
```

Floyd Johnson wrote:

> Whether to use C++ or not may depend on the customers environment.
> Generally, C++ would be a better choice for the application (or what we
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> that unilaterally.

And before switching to C++, you should consider Ada 95.   You would
have a language with all the power of C++ with the addition of an
Information Systems Annex that supports all data types and formatting
capabilities you are accustomed to in COBOL.   Not only that but Ada
has a direct capability for interfacing with existing libraries of COBOL
code so you could make the transition in an orderly and systematic
manner.

For more information you could look at:   http://www.adaic.org

Richard Riehle
```

Richard Riehle wrote:

>
> And before switching to C++, you should consider Ada 95.   You would
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Richard Riehle

Last reference I recall to the good lady was an old movie abut the poet
Byron.
So who uses Ada ? Ok so you are going to reply, "Well I DO !" (perhaps <G>).

We've got Susan lurking there in Seattle - Madam, is the following statement
correct from the site reference Richard gave us :-

"Ada is designed for embedded systems, safety-critical software, and large
 projects that require portability and maintainability. For example, over 99
 percent of the aviation software in the Boeing 777 is in Ada" - and what
about the rest of the Boeing models ?

Accepting that the above is accurate, shouldn't the phrase, "safety-critical
software, and large projects that require portability and maintainability",
apply to ALL computer languages. I wouldn't mind betting Smalltalkers would
make the same claim, citing it is used in commercial environments like
banking. Some of the former Xerox PARC crowd are truly PO'd with the
marketing success of Java.

As somebody wrote to me about a week ago, C++ and Java are the 'sexy'
languages - and basically that's what the kids are learning.  Apart from the
Gartner figure ( 3 million programming in COBOL - let's give or take half a
miill), I wonder how the other languages break down in usage.

It's about 25 years ago. A large COBOL file needed tuning, in a real hurry,
by adding one additional field, so read in x chars and write out x chars +
4.  The IT Manager went ballistic when he found out - the new jerk employed
as Assistant DBA volunteered to write it. Just to show how clever he was, he
wrote it in FORTRAN for a COBOL-only shop ! (As it was, it did turn out to be
a 'once only' - but if it hadn't ????)

Jimmy, Calgary AB

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Recommend Cobol Book, and Compiler, for C++ Programmer Please

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Richard Riehle <richard@adaworks.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-25T18:20:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CC8AB52.1962A824@adaworks.com>`
- **References:** `<3CBF3EAB.A9856B04@aracnet.com> <TWSv8.166$t65.21@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <3CC009D3.9B2ECA4C@aracnet.com> <mTTv8.14561$R33.627925@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC045C9.2F9964EF@aracnet.com> <3CC0473D.87C4D7A1@shaw.ca> <3CC08A31.C448ECBE@aracnet.com> <x42w8.14959$R33.708536@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC0B438.A3E08B50@shaw.ca> <g14w8.15213$R33.731268@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC1BA5E.C65F32C8@aracnet.com> <yJkw8.23068$o86.920718@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <963e602a.0204221002.7dc04f3c@posting.google.com> <3CC4C351.D3834630@adaworks.com> <3CC4CF9F.186BCB0D@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:

> Richard Riehle wrote:
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> about the rest of the Boeing models ?

Ada is being used for updating existing flight software systems,
at least those of which I have direct knowledge.

> Accepting that the above is accurate, shouldn't the phrase, "safety-critical
> software, and large projects that require portability and maintainability",
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> marketing success of Java.

1) Sun expressly states that Java is not intended for safety-critical software.

2) Smalltalk is not suited for safety-critical software.  This is unfortunate
    because I personally like the language.

3) Java's success is largely due to marketing hype, as you have noted.
    However, Java also addresses some of the error-prone features of
    C++, just as C# attempts to address some of the error-prone features
    of Java.

Now to the question of Ada and safety-critical software, since you asked.

Ada, formerly a language focused on military environments, is now used
for commercial software such as avionics in which high reliability is a
key factor.   What makes Ada the language of choice?  The reasons are
multiple, but we will give only a few here.

1) Ada, the language, is designed to maximize the  amount of error detection
    in design, syntax, and semantics as early as possible in the development
    process.    This is more of an engineering issue than a programming concern.

2) The compilers for Ada, those used in safety-critical software, are required to

    pass a set of validation tests before they can be used in production.   The
    validation suite consists of a large number of programs that exercise the
    compiler to determine whether it conforms to the ISO language standard.

3) Ada is the only language with an Annex specifically targeted to the issues
    of software safety.   Annex H of the Ada Language Reference Manual
    defines specialized pragmas and rules that provide  restrictions, code
    review capabilities for ensuring a higher-level of integrity of the final
    product.

4) Ada programs are compiled to a specialized Run-time Environment that
    sits on top of the operating system or bare-board platform.  This RTE
    includes a set of specialized features for run-time checking of the
    program.   It is augmented by an error management capability at the
    source code level.

                NOTE:   The RTE is specific to each processor and processor
                              variant.   The FAA requires that the RTE be
certified
                              according their rules.   Once the RTE is FAA
certified,
                              it becomes a commercial, off-the-shelf product
(COTS)
                              that needs no additional changes.   Ada programs
written
                              for that RTE are then subjected to rigorous testing
and
                              inspection as well, but the compiler will have
detected
                              a great many errors that might have slipped by a
less
                              demanding language compiler than Ada


> As somebody wrote to me about a week ago, C++ and Java are the 'sexy'
> languages - and basically that's what the kids are learning.  Apart from the
> Gartner figure ( 3 million programming in COBOL - let's give or take half a
> miill), I wonder how the other languages break down in usage.

COBOL and Visual Basic seem to have the lion's share of the business systems
market.   I continue to like COBOL, especially any  compiler that conforms to
the full ANSI-85 or later standard.   I see Ada as a good alternative, when one
wants an alternative, because the Ada's Annex B allows direct interfacing with
COBOL,  Annex F supports all the COBOL data types and editing clauses,
and much of the rest of the language is easy for a COBOL programmer to
learn.   It is certainly a better choice than C++ under any circumstances.

> It's about 25 years ago. A large COBOL file needed tuning, in a real hurry,
> by adding one additional field, so read in x chars and write out x chars +
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> a 'once only' - but if it hadn't ????)

I too have seen some pretty dumb things done by people who underestimated
the power and virtues of COBOL.  It is nice to see the grand old language
continue to evolve into the current century.


Richard Riehle
```

LOL! Yes, UNIX on the PC was a wonderful advancement, even if SCO did seem
to cost a fortune back then.

RE: Memory under DOS - all the compilers that run under DOS I am aware of
use a DOS Extender, and
thus are somewhat freed from the 64K tyranny. Do check and see if it is
really DOS, or if it just a DOS
window running under say, Windows NT. There is a big difference between
them, with the version being
much better.

You really need to talk to them and get some solid specifications about what
they are running, and what they
want it to do. It would serve to answer a lot of your questions - operating
without the facts is no fun! :)

I would suggest you also take a look through the COBOL FAQ if you have not
seen it yet. Our own Mr. Klein
does a great job of putting a whole lot of answers into that document. It
also includes the address to download
the Fuji compiler. :)

-Paul

```
> "Larry Lindstrom" <larryl@aracnet.com> wrote 

> >    I've processed DOS interrupts in C.  Along with wrestling with
> > the 64 K limit.  When Unix came to the PC 10 years ago, I was very
> > happy.

"10 years ago" ?.  Microsoft Xenix (an AT&T licenced Unix later sold to
SCO) was out for 8086 machines in 1979, that's 23 years ago. Various *ix
were available on several types of PC, including Venix on IBM-PC (pre
XT).

The 64 K limit was CP/M and 8080/Z80.  MS-DOS had a 640Kb limit (though
it did have 64Kb segments).
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CC6D579.80FB77AB@aracnet.com>`
- **References:** `<3CBF3EAB.A9856B04@aracnet.com> <XqIv8.19214$L1.1511432@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <TWSv8.166$t65.21@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <3CC009D3.9B2ECA4C@aracnet.com> <mTTv8.14561$R33.627925@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC045C9.2F9964EF@aracnet.com> <3CC0473D.87C4D7A1@shaw.ca> <3CC08A31.C448ECBE@aracnet.com> <x42w8.14959$R33.708536@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC0B438.A3E08B50@shaw.ca> <g14w8.15213$R33.731268@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC1BA5E.C65F32C8@aracnet.com> <yJkw8.23068$o86.920718@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC4893A.6EAB8B34@aracnet.com> <lg1x8.920$g72.74827@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC6C890.78E3D7CF@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> > "Larry Lindstrom" <larryl@aracnet.com> wrote
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> it did have 64Kb segments).

   Yes, Microsoft did have a Unix called Xenix, but it had to 
run on Intel processors with no hardware memory management, 
in "Real" memory mode.  Because of this, I always considered 
Xenix more of a Unix emulator.  

   Unixware was a port or AT&T SVR4, which had been developed 
to run on processors with demand paged memory management, it 
was a joy to use.  System resources were now protected from 
any mistakes I might make in my code.  

   My brand new 486/66, with an unheard of 32 Megs of memory, 
a spacious 1 1/2 Gig drive, high performance VL bus ATI video 
card with its own processor and VL bus SCSI controller, ran 
Unixware as if it were borne for that OS.  My clients who were 
committed to SCO, developing on text screens, were impressed.  

   This liberated me from adding code to a project, then 
spending a day to move things around so the executables would 
fit under the 64 K segment limit.  I don't remember all the 
issues, I do remember repeated trial and error.  I had no tools 
to tell me exactly how far over the segment limit I was, so I 
didn't know if I was getting close until things started to 
work.  The 640 K limit wasn't a problem for the projects I was 
developing.  

   Anyway, it's nice not to have to worry about that.  

                                                        Thanks
                                                        Larry
```

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>

- **References:** `<3CBF3EAB.A9856B04@aracnet.com> <XqIv8.19214$L1.1511432@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <TWSv8.166$t65.21@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <3CC009D3.9B2ECA4C@aracnet.com> <mTTv8.14561$R33.627925@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC045C9.2F9964EF@aracnet.com> <3CC0473D.87C4D7A1@shaw.ca> <3CC08A31.C448ECBE@aracnet.com> <x42w8.14959$R33.708536@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC0B438.A3E08B50@shaw.ca> <g14w8.15213$R33.731268@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC1BA5E.C65F32C8@aracnet.com> <yJkw8.23068$o86.920718@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC4893A.6EAB8B34@aracnet.com> <lg1x8.920$g72.74827@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC6C890.78E3D7CF@Azonic.co.nz> <3CC6D579.80FB77AB@aracnet.com>`

```
Larry Lindstrom wrote:
> 
>    Yes, Microsoft did have a Unix called Xenix, but it had to
> run on Intel processors with no hardware memory management,
> in "Real" memory mode.  Because of this, I always considered
> Xenix more of a Unix emulator.

And what type of 'hardware memory management' do you imagine that PDP
7/9/11s and Interdata 8/32, or even 68020 had ?  Xenix was a real AT&T
edition 7 Unix.  In 1979 it was as much Unix as was available.

Xenix 286 and Unix 286 was available nearly 20 years ago.

It seems that it wasn't 'Unix for the PC' so much as '486 for the PC'
that enlightened you.


>    This liberated me from adding code to a project, then
> spending a day to move things around so the executables would
> fit under the 64 K segment limit. 

I don't know why you had a problem with this.  Most compilers in the 80s
supported 'Large' model, or even 'Huge' model.  Since the AT many have
supported extended memory. I even experimented  with Zortech's EMS
extenders on an 8088 which gave access to megabytes without the
programmer having to be concerned with segments.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Recommend Cobol Book, and Compiler, for C++ Programmer Please

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-04-24T23:01:37+00:00

Tom at Liant,

Way beyond my memory recall but about 20 years ago I was using RM/COBOL for
Radio Shack (Tandy) Model 16s with Xenix - multi user. Hence for years my
direcory structure, (even in PC DOS) used to read :-

    \usr\applicaton\programs etc....,

thinking that Xenix (or rather Unix) would be the up and coming thing.

RM/COBOL - did it have a memory limitation or did it take advantage of the
full Xenix memory, I just don't remember. I might also add, I've completely
forgotten how I programmed for multi users <G>. Guess I must have gotten
into record locking, if that was a feature of RM/COBOL back then.

Jimmy, Calgary AB

Microfocus' compiler can often be purchased as a textbook/compiler kit for 
less than the cost of the compiler.  I know that this is true for MF 
NetExpress University Edition - I expect that it would be true for other 
MF products.  Check out Barnes and Noble or Amazon.com for available 
texts.  I wish I could give you specifics, but our campus uses Fujitsu V6 
on campus - and students have access to Fujitsu V3 in Thane's book.

Floyd Johnson

: HIGH compatibility with R/M Cobol. You can also develop on any platform, =
: targeting deployment on=20
: any other platform.  Given that developing under DOS is a painful and =
: time wasting experience these
: days, that would be a heck of a option to me.=20

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Recommend Cobol Book, and Compiler, for C++ Programmer Please

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-04-25T00:46:51-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B8ED027B00A54D6F0DB3DC60@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<3CBF3EAB.A9856B04@aracnet.com> <XqIv8.19214$L1.1511432@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <TWSv8.166$t65.21@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <3CC009D3.9B2ECA4C@aracnet.com> <mTTv8.14561$R33.627925@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC045C9.2F9964EF@aracnet.com> <3CC0473D.87C4D7A1@shaw.ca> <3CC08A31.C448ECBE@aracnet.com> <x42w8.14959$R33.708536@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
On Fri, 19 Apr 2002 19:51:57 -0400, Paul Raulerson wrote
> Heck, I doubt most COBOL
> programmers use debuggers anyway, at least on the Mainframe.

I can only speak for a few environments ... about a year ago I taught a 
series of "COBOL85 for COBOL74 Programmers" classes for a large client. 
Though the class was largely language-oriented and implementation- 
independent, an important topic to them was the changes in the debugger 
for their environment. The debugger is COBOL85 TADS under Unisys MCP 
systems. We spent one morning (about 1/5 of the class) on that, and the 
programmers in the class were perhaps at their most attentive for that 
session -- even though it was Friday morning.

I have little experience with other modern debuggers. Perhaps this 
indicates that the Unisys TADS product is that much better than other 
mainframe debuggers. I'm not in a position to say.

On Fri, 19 Apr 2002 22:05:00 -0400, Paul Raulerson wrote
> Re: debuggers - they are available on the mainframe, but they are not all 
> that common. Deskchecking  is still very important.

Programmers are always going to prefer using debuggers over desk 
checking when the debugger is fun to use. Though debuggers are very 
useful, I don't find this imbalance to be good, in any environment. 
Dijkstra's famous comment applies: testing can prove the presence of 
bugs, but not the absence.

For this same client, we also converted (74->85) and renovated about 
half a million lines of COBOL. By renovated, I mean that we didn't just 
get it to work with the COBOL85 compiler, we also introduced COBOL85 
features where they would improve readability (including universal use 
of scope terminators), and cleaned up a lot of badly written code (some 
of it over 25 years old), especially with respect to GO TO usage. Of 
course we automated the conversion and renovation everywhere we could, 
but that still left a lot of lines with manual changes.

But we didn't have the opportunity to test the code -- the client did 
that. So ironically, we didn't get to use the good mainframe debugger.

We got a LOT of exercise on our desk checking skills. I don't have the 
final count on the number of errors found in code after we returned it 
to the client, but I could count them on my fingers. Would have liked 
zero of course, but under the circumstances we were pretty happy with 
an error rate around 1 in 50,000 lines.

Edward Reid
```

```
Actually, the best debugger I know of ran/runs on under Wang VS. You can
break into it anytime,
view/change memory, breakpoints, restart, etc. All the normal stuff, but it
really does meet the
criteria  you mentioned below - it is FUN. :)

TADS is actually very cool too, I like it a lot., but have not had the
opportunity to really use it.
I had a possible MCP contract come up a few months ago, and tried to get
Loonisys to give me
a price on the little hosted development system for ClearPath, but
despite ->15<- calls, I never
reached the right person. Absolutely amazing. I do have a micro-A series
board here still, but I
don't have any legal way to run it. CANDE isn't ISPF, but it is a good
editor in itself. Did they
ever get the debugger integrated into it? My last professional use of it was
in 1985, working on
one of the front end processors. (We were really defense systems, but
Treddyfin needed
some help... :)

-Paul

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Recommend Cobol Book, and Compiler, for C++ Programmer Please

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-04-25T13:52:05-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B8EDBA8500D089980DB3DC60@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<3CBF3EAB.A9856B04@aracnet.com> <XqIv8.19214$L1.1511432@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <TWSv8.166$t65.21@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <3CC009D3.9B2ECA4C@aracnet.com> <mTTv8.14561$R33.627925@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC045C9.2F9964EF@aracnet.com> <3CC0473D.87C4D7A1@shaw.ca> <3CC08A31.C448ECBE@aracnet.com> <x42w8.14959$R33.708536@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <01HW.B8ED027B00A54D6F0DB3DC60@news-east.usenetserver.com> <VnTx8.12759$g72.467350@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
On Thu, 25 Apr 2002 9:19:49 -0400, Paul Raulerson wrote
> I had a possible MCP contract come up a few months ago, and tried to 
> get Loonisys to give me a price on the little hosted development 
> system for ClearPath, but despite ->15<- calls, I never reached the 
> right person. Absolutely amazing. 

Yeah, you'll hear lots of long-time Unisys customers talk about this 
phenomenon. Mikey Blumenthal made a big deal about combining the 
strengths and duplications of Burroughs and Sperry, but what got 
combined most effectively was their Sales Prevention Departments (SPD), 
which seem to have been equally effective in both companies. The 
amazing thing is that the systems have been good enough to survive 
despite the SPD.

> I do have a micro-A series board here still, but I don't have any 
> legal way to run it. 

The current equivalent of the MicroA is the LX100 -- a complete MCP 
system several times faster than a MicroA, running in a stock laptop 
(albeit a $5000 laptop plus I think a $5000 software license). It's 
only for development and demonstration purposes, no production use, and 
there's no support. A wonderful boon to small developers. Runs circles 
around an LX5120, the smallest production system.

But the SPD got their fingers in here too:  you can't upgrade the 
software once it's been installed, and you can't get support even on a 
pay-per-call basis. (These might change after a long meeting at UNITE 
last fall though.)

> CANDE isn't ISPF, but it is a good editor in itself. Did they ever 
> get the debugger integrated into it? 

Not into CANDE. CANDE is the low end editor, although a lot of people 
who don't like change still use it. Because it's basically a line mode 
editor (despite the page mode kludge), Unisys aren't trying to update 
it much. CANDE has been far surpassed by two other editors, 
SYSTEM/EDITOR (aka U ED) and Programmer's Workbench (aka NX/Edit).

SYSTEM/EDITOR was developed at one of the software plants around 1980. 
It was first for internal use and later released to the field. It's a 
true full screen editor, always showing a current image of the work 
file on the screen. It handles patch mode, integrated cross-reference 
files, macros, up to 256 lines and columns, dump reading, object code 
reading, and numerous other features. It's the editor that the software 
plants have used almost exclusively for over 20 years, and I'm sure you 
know what happens to software when the users are also the developers: 
it gets extended when there's a need and fixed when it breaks, with 
little or no red tape. TADS is fully integrated into EDITOR; you can 
display variable values, monitor and trap on variables, walk, and so on 
from within EDITOR. The big problem with EDITOR was always that it 
wasn't bundled and the SPD never figured out how to market it.

NX/Edit (renamed Programmer's Workbench, or PW, in the newspeak) is a 
Windows-based product which edits mainframe files. It maintains all the 
conventions of MCP file usage and program development while giving the 
user a Windows environment. It can run either in integrated mode (with 
the files on the mainframe and a server providing the access), or 
completely standalone with all the files on the PC and no connection to 
the mainframe, or any combination. It has most of the important 
features of EDITOR, including cross-reference integration -- and the 
cross-reference files can be downloaded to the PC for standalone 
operation. TADS is also integrated with PW. It's still a new product 
with a few raw edges, the biggest one being no macros or scripting, but 
most of these are being addressed. So far it's bundled, thus bypassing 
the SPD. This combined with the now-familiar Windows UI gives it a good 
chance of much greater acceptance than EDITOR has ever had.

> My last professional use of it was in 1985, working on one of the 
> front end processors. (We were really defense systems, but Treddyfin 
> needed some help... :) 

They always worked closely anyway ... I worked for PRC on the Phase IV 
project, and people from our offices were constantly going up to Paoli 
for consultations ... I never asked exactly who they were visiting.

Edward Reid
```

```

"Edward Reid" <edward@paleo.org> wrote in message
news:01HW.B8EDBA8500D089980DB3DC60@news-east.usenetserver.com...

Thanks for the cool information, I really miss Loonisys in a lot of ways.
(*sigh*)

Development Corp. branch of things.)

>

>> programmers use debuggers anyway, at least on the Mainframe.

I don't know what mainframe shop you were at but where I'm at people swear by

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Recommend Cobol Book, and Compiler, for C++ Programmer Please

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2002-04-27T16:46:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CCAD5E5.7A3A6F92@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<01HW.B8ED027B00A54D6F0DB3DC60@news-east.usenetserver.com> <20020425232401.28309.00005198@mb-ms.aol.com>`

```
YukonMama wrote:
> 
> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> when I get a chance.

I'll vouch for Xpediter. It offers all the features of another debugger
that a previous poster mentioned, plus it supports other environments,
e.g., DB2, and presents the same interface to the programmer, ISPF style
menus, with which virtually everyone working on IBM mainframes is
familiar. Relying heavily on desk checking utilizes your most expensive
resource (people) in its least productive mode. Iron is very cheap when
compared to computer staff.
```

### Server Express

- **From:** rosario.carbone@ocnuk.com (Rosario)
- **Date:** 2002-04-19T00:25:21-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38555a5a.0204182325.281d9351@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello everyone.

First my English is trash sorry for errors.

I am a delphi and vb developer, but I have many years experience in
COBOL Workbench by Micro Focus, I also had experience (not for long)
with Net Express by Micro Focus but I have not any experience of
Server Express. Server Express should be equivalent of Net Express for
a UNIX machine.
Well, the problem is I have to give to Server Express the right
directives to precompile a COBOL source that use SQL embedded, the
rdbms is ORACLE ver. 8.1.7.

I do not know which version of PRO*COBOL (procob) is supported by this
version of ORACLE, for COBOL Workbench (DOS) I simply pass a cobol
source to the procob with the right directives and then I
compiles/linked the output. In Net Express, if I remember well I
simply changed the properties of the project and I gave the project
the directives, if I remember well the procob for oracle was native of
Net Express (but I don't remember how I could give information about
the version of procob to use, I do not remember to be honest).

Could someone give me information about Server Express? What I mean
is, if I have Server Express and Personal Oracle installed, what I
have to do for precompile and compile a cobol source under Server
Express.

Remember that I have no manuals and either no tools (Server Express
and Oracle) at moment and last but not least I have no much experience
of UNIX.

Please help, just few informations.

Thanks in advance.

Rosario Carbone

P.S. Write me on my personal e-mail rosario.carbone@ocn.uk
```

What he said, was, there is no artificial limit on the number of control
beaks in any COBOL program; the only limit is that of the programmer's
ability to code them.

## Thread: Max value possible in COMP field ?? — 19 this month / 26 total

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Max value possible in COMP field ??

- **From:** sandythesatan@rediffmail.com (sandy)
- **Date:** 2002-04-19T15:33:46-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d88238ab.0204191433.27443f4a@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Folks,
Am a new born baby in the world of COBOL ;-) 

Wanted to know if I can store a 5 digit numeric value in a PIC 9(4) COMP field ??

Thanks in advance guyz
```

#### ↳ Re: Max value possible in COMP field ??

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-19T17:50:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a9q6vk$bjv$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<d88238ab.0204191433.27443f4a@posting.google.com>`

```
Depends on compiler, compiler options, and several other things.

Many (not all) compilers have a "compiler option" to allow "as big" a value
in a COMP field as will fit into the allocated storage.

Look for an option similar to TRUNC.
```

#### ↳ Re: Max value possible in COMP field ??

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2002-04-20T00:55:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-pJv42NrL20m8@thishost>`
- **References:** `<d88238ab.0204191433.27443f4a@posting.google.com>`

```
On Fri, 19 Apr 2002 22:33:46 UTC, sandythesatan@rediffmail.com (sandy)
wrote:

> Hi Folks,
> Am a new born baby in the world of COBOL ;-) 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks in advance guyz

It depends on the compiler, but remember that a pic 9(4) comp field is
usually a 16 bit integer and you can only store +-32667 in a signed 16
bit integer or +65535 in an unsigned 16 bit integer. You will be 
limited by the data format even if the compiler allows you store five 
digits.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Max value possible in COMP field ??

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-04-20T12:17:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<I%cw8.2432$uV.893@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<d88238ab.0204191433.27443f4a@posting.google.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-pJv42NrL20m8@thishost>`

```
Who is telling this lady or gentleman (sandythesatan is either ambiguous or
androgynous) that storing a 5-digit number in a PIC 9(4) USAGE COMP field
"can be done" by playing around with the value and perhaps the TRUNC()
option of the compiler?

This is the worst advice you could possibly give; should that advice be
heeded, I hope your next job is debugging this code when some value changes
in the application and it mysteriously stops 'working'.

The correct answer is, "No. You must use a PICTURE clause containing at
least as many 9's as there are digits in the data."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Max value possible in COMP field ??

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-21T17:24:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dBCw8.120770$GS6.12187781@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<FRhw8.123985$K5.10565071@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <20020420152159.27470.00003797@mb-mr.aol.com>`

```

"S Comstock" <scomstock@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20020420152159.27470.00003797@mb-mr.aol.com...
> JerryMouse writes ...
>
> [snip]
>
> >> Wanted to know if I can store a 5 digit numeric value in a PIC 9(4)
COMP
> >field ??
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Whoa, there! Why not use comp? Small size with fast machine instructions,
on a
> mainframe, anyway. Lots of places where BINARY numbers are the best
choice.
>
Assuming you meant "Why not" for experienced programmers on a mainframe, the
reasons are:

1. Exaggeration for the purpose of emphasis to the original poster.
2. Communication difficulties when transferring data ("How do I convert
EBCDIC COMP to ASCII?"
3. Introduction of very subtle bugs (MOVE 32679 TO MYCOUNT)
4. While the efficiency of COMP is indeed much greater, using COMP fields to
compute the sales tax on 10,000 invoices saves maybe this much (holding
thumb and forefinger about 1mm apart).

Mostly, however, I was jumping up and down for the novice's benefit.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Max value possible in COMP field ??

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-20T21:53:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rrlw8.23150$o86.928895@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<d88238ab.0204191433.27443f4a@posting.google.com> <FRhw8.123985$K5.10565071@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Huh? Why not? COMP is LOTS faster, at least on a mainframe, and far more
space efficient.
MMMM -  Okay - even a quick and dirty test shows that COMP is far more
efficient than
not using it. Here are the results- the 3 minute to write program that
generated it included below.
I ran this 10 times each with comparable results. (And yes, it is a very
SLOW mainframe. :)
The results are of course, only accurate to within a second or so, but the
efficiency difference is
quite apparent, even with these rough measurements.


(1 Mainframe)

IEF285I   VOL SER NOS= USER02.
IEF373I STEP/TEST1   /START 2002082.1635
IEF374I STEP/TEST1   /STOP  2002082.1636 CPU    0MIN 57.28SEC SRB    0MIN
00.02S
IEF375I  JOB/PRAULT1 /START 2002082.1635
IEF376I  JOB/PRAULT1 /STOP  2002082.1636 CPU    0MIN 57.28SEC SRB    0MIN
00.02S
START TIME        : 16:35:09
END TIME          : 16:36:04    <<-- 55 seconds
START TIME BINARY : 16:36:04
END TIME BINARY   : 16:36:13    <<--  9 seconds
******************************** BOTTOM OF DATA
********************************

(2  Windows 2000 PC with IBM VA COBOL Version 3.)

test1
START TIME        : 16:37:58
END TIME          : 16:38:07    <<-- 9 seconds
START TIME BINARY : 16:38:07
END TIME BINARY   : 16:38:08    <<-- 1 second

PRESS ANY KEY TO CONTINUE.


----

And here is the code that ran.

      ******************************************
      * quick and dirty test COMP vs DISPLAY
      *   Mainframe and PC
      ******************************************
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. TEST1.

       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

       01 WS-ACCUM                       PIC S9(8) .
       01 WS-ACCUM-P                     PIC S9(8) COMP.

       01 WS-COUNTER                     PIC S9(8).
       01 WS-COUNTER-P                   PIC S9(8) COMP.

       01 WS-ADDVALUE                    PIC S9(4).
       01 WS-ADDVALUE-P                  PIC S9(4) COMP.

       01 WS-TIME.
          05 T-HOURS                     PIC 9(2).
          05 T-MINUTES                   PIC 9(2).
          05 T-SECONDS                   PIC 9(2).

       01 WS-DISPLAY-TIME.
          05 T-HOURS                     PIC 9(2).
          05 FILLER                      PIC X(1) VALUE ':'.
          05 T-MINUTES                   PIC 9(2).
          05 FILLER                      PIC X(1) VALUE ':'.
          05 T-SECONDS                   PIC 9(2).


       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       START-PROGRAM.
           MOVE ZEROS TO WS-COUNTER, WS-COUNTER-P,
                         WS-ACCUM, WS-ACCUM-P

           MOVE 1     TO WS-ADDVALUE, WS-ADDVALUE-P

           ACCEPT WS-TIME FROM TIME
           MOVE CORRESPONDING WS-TIME TO WS-DISPLAY-TIME
           DISPLAY 'START TIME        : ' WS-DISPLAY-TIME

           PERFORM UNTIL WS-ACCUM > 9999999
             ADD WS-ADDVALUE TO WS-ACCUM
             END-PERFORM

           ACCEPT WS-TIME FROM TIME
           MOVE CORRESPONDING WS-TIME TO WS-DISPLAY-TIME
           DISPLAY 'END TIME          : ' WS-DISPLAY-TIME

           ACCEPT WS-TIME FROM TIME
           MOVE CORRESPONDING WS-TIME TO WS-DISPLAY-TIME
           DISPLAY 'START TIME BINARY : ' WS-DISPLAY-TIME

           PERFORM UNTIL WS-ACCUM-P > 9999999
             ADD WS-ADDVALUE-P TO WS-ACCUM-P
             END-PERFORM

           ACCEPT WS-TIME FROM TIME
           MOVE CORRESPONDING WS-TIME TO WS-DISPLAY-TIME
           DISPLAY 'END TIME BINARY   : ' WS-DISPLAY-TIME

           GOBACK.



"JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com> wrote in message
news:FRhw8.123985$K5.10565071@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com...
>
> "sandy" <sandythesatan@rediffmail.com> wrote in message
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Max value possible in COMP field ??

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-04-24T09:51:22-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B8EC309A0073FED70DB3DC60@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<d88238ab.0204191433.27443f4a@posting.google.com> <FRhw8.123985$K5.10565071@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <rrlw8.23150$o86.928895@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
On Sat, 20 Apr 2002 17:53:27 -0400, Paul Raulerson wrote
> Huh? Why not? COMP is LOTS faster, at least on a mainframe, and far more
> space efficient.

Maybe on your mainframe, but definitely not on all. The representation 
of COMP is totally implementor-dependent.

On Unisys MCP systems, COMP is stored as packed decimal. This is for 
historical reasons -- compatibility with the old Burroughs B2500. But 
MCP systems don't do decimal arithmetic (the B2500 did), so such fields 
must be converted to and from binary for most uses.

On these systems, the most efficient USAGEs are BINARY EXTENDED and 
REAL -- neither of them ANSI COBOL of course. Just BINARY is better 
than COMP but obeys the truncation rules of ANSI COBOL, which renders 
it less efficient, since of course extra code is required for the 
truncation.

Edward Reid

Glossary:

MCP systems are also known as ClearPath NX/LX, A-Series, Burroughs 
Large Systems, and Burroughs 6xxx/7xxx. This line began in about 1970 
and is very much alive today.

The Burroughs B2500 was part of a family also known as V-Series and 
Burroughs Meduim Systems, and Burroughs 2xxx/3xxx/4xxx. This line began 
in about 1965 and was discontinued about ten years ago. Naturally 
Burroughs/Unisys had a vested interest in making conversion to the 
A-Series easy for their existing customers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Max value possible in COMP field ??

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-25T01:08:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XFIx8.9118$d17.387384@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<d88238ab.0204191433.27443f4a@posting.google.com> <FRhw8.123985$K5.10565071@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <rrlw8.23150$o86.928895@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <01HW.B8EC309A0073FED70DB3DC60@news-east.usenetserver.com>`

```
Then your compiler is wrong. (Burroughs machines really want to do Pascal
anyway... :)

[COMP]UTATIONAL does NOT mean packed decimal, it means BINARY. In fact,
COMPUTATIONAL is synonymous with BINARY.  If the MCP systems are storing
COMP items in some non-computational form, I would seriously take it up with
the
compiler writers.

IBM compilers have COMP-1 (Floating point), COMP-2 (Long Floating Point),
COMP-3(Internal
Decimal), and COMP-5 (Native Binary). COMP-4 is again the equivalent of
BINARY.
Those are certainly extensions to the spec, but they are fully in compliance
with COMP.


-Paul

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Max value possible in COMP field ??

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-24T22:09:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aa7s0s$nmq$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<d88238ab.0204191433.27443f4a@posting.google.com> <FRhw8.123985$K5.10565071@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <rrlw8.23150$o86.928895@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <01HW.B8EC309A0073FED70DB3DC60@news-east.usenetserver.com> <XFIx8.9118$d17.387384@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
Paul - simply put
  YOU ARE WRONG

Usage COMP is (always has been - always will be) TOTALLY implementor
defined.  Even in the IBM environment, it is equivalent to Packed-Decimal
for COBOL/400.

COMP is part of the ANSI/ISO Standard, but what it means is up to the
implementor and COMP-? (where "?" can be 1, 2, 3, X, or whatever) is totally
an extension (and any vendor may do with it as they see fit).

My guess is that you have never worked on (or with) anything but IBM
mainframes where COMP is equivalent to BINARY (which was introduced in the
'85 Standard) and where it meant this "same thing" in older compilers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Max value possible in COMP field ??

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com>

- **References:** `<d88238ab.0204191433.27443f4a@posting.google.com> <FRhw8.123985$K5.10565071@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <rrlw8.23150$o86.928895@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <01HW.B8EC309A0073FED70DB3DC60@news-east.usenetserver.com> <XFIx8.9118$d17.387384@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <aa7s0s$nmq$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

Uh William, you are definitely an authority I respect, so don't take this
wrong, but
go look on page 170 of the ILE COBOL/400 Reference.  You are right that in
ILE COBOL/400, COMPUTATIONAL is PACKED-DECIMAL, but that is
because COMP and COMPUTATIONAL are almost always assigned to the
most efficient of all the USAGES for a particular platform.  (As usual on a
400,
COMP-4 is BINARY in the IBM World.)

While you are right I was talking about IBM Mainframes, I thought I was
being clear I was talking about IBM Mainframes (and incidentally, AIX
and Windows versions of the IBM Compiler as well.)

What I was reacting to is that by convention, though not by decree,
compilers
assign the most efficient usage to COMP. Which ties into where the whole
discussion
started - and I still contend - it is not a bad thing to use COMP. Rather it
is a much
more efficient way to do computations and that -> should <- apply to any
compiler.

Obviously, we found out Unisys has decided to not follow that standard, for
whatever
twisted reason. :)

And while I do have some experience with ancient compilers, including COBOL
compilers,
I must admit, I tend to not look back fondly on older and more limited
compilers and systems.
I like to take as much advantage of new features as possible.  I'll match
assembler coding
skills with ya on a CP901 if you want though... ;) :) :) :) :) :) :) :) :)
:) :) :) :) :) :) :) : :) :) : :)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Max value possible in COMP field ??

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-04-25T14:25:57-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B8EDC27500D266FC0DB3DC60@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<d88238ab.0204191433.27443f4a@posting.google.com> <FRhw8.123985$K5.10565071@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <rrlw8.23150$o86.928895@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <01HW.B8EC309A0073FED70DB3DC60@news-east.usenetserver.com> <XFIx8.9118$d17.387384@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <aa7s0s$nmq$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <MSKx8.9486$d17.417864@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
On Wed, 24 Apr 2002 23:38:20 -0400, Paul Raulerson wrote
> Uh William, you are definitely an authority I respect, so don't take 
> this wrong, but go look on page 170 of the ILE COBOL/400 Reference. 

Paul,

Try page VI-47 of the ANSI85 standard, section 5.14.4, paragraph (4):

: The USAGE IS COMPUTATIONAL clause specifies that a radix and format
: specified by the implementor is used to represent a numeric item [etc]

It says nothing about an efficient representation.

> What I was reacting to is that by convention, though not by decree, 
> compilers assign the most efficient usage to COMP. 

What X3J4 was reacting to, in introducing USAGE BINARY into the ANSI 
standard, was the fact that this convention was too often not followed, 
for valid reasons, and thus many implementations needed another choice.

> Which ties into where the whole discussion started - and I still 
> contend - it is not a bad thing to use COMP. Rather it is a much 
> more efficient way to do computations and that -> should <- apply to 
> any compiler. 

Actually, the only representation I know of that's truly BAD for 
computation is numeric edited. (Numeric de-editing can be useful under 
limited circumstances, but it should have required explicit syntax to 
prevent programmers from using it accidentally.)

Serious enough computation in a COBOL program is rare. When you do it, 
you can afford to pick the USAGE from the standard that's best on the 
present target computer. At least the program will run, and not too 
badly, on another standard-conforming implementation, and generally 
you'll have only a little bit of fixup to bring it up to best 
performance.

The point again, though, is that in general (platform-independent), you 
can't assume that COMP is the most efficient.

> Obviously, we found out Unisys has decided to not follow that 
> standard, for whatever twisted reason. :) 

Which standard? The Unisys/MCP COBOL85 implementation conforms 
completely to the ANSI85 standard on this point.

Edward Reid
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Max value possible in COMP field ??

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-26T00:56:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9B1y8.11764$Y03.655438@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<d88238ab.0204191433.27443f4a@posting.google.com> <FRhw8.123985$K5.10565071@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <rrlw8.23150$o86.928895@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <01HW.B8EC309A0073FED70DB3DC60@news-east.usenetserver.com> <XFIx8.9118$d17.387384@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <aa7s0s$nmq$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <MSKx8.9486$d17.417864@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <01HW.B8EDC27500D266FC0DB3DC60@news-east.usenetserver.com>`

```
It isn't a part of the ANSI standard buy any means Edward, and I never
purported it to be so. But it is a
general rule of convention that seems to be widely applied:

IBM Cobol (all platforms)
Wang COBOL
2200 COBOL
MicroFocus COBOL (all platforms)
AcuCOBOL

Are all the ones I can immediately test, but essentially, I can not think of
any good reason for COMP to not be assigned to
the most efficient computational representation.

-Paul

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Max value possible in COMP field ??

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-25T20:10:57-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aaa9ks$2op$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<d88238ab.0204191433.27443f4a@posting.google.com> <FRhw8.123985$K5.10565071@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <rrlw8.23150$o86.928895@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <01HW.B8EC309A0073FED70DB3DC60@news-east.usenetserver.com> <XFIx8.9118$d17.387384@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <aa7s0s$nmq$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <MSKx8.9486$d17.417864@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <01HW.B8EDC27500D266FC0DB3DC60@news-east.usenetserver.com> <9B1y8.11764$Y03.655438@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
Micro Focus USAGE comp is changed (or chageable) by the COMP-5 directive.
Also, as MF has always documented COMP-X (and/or COMP-5) may well be MORE
efficient that COMP.

IBM COBOL all platforms (definitely depending on big/little-endian issues)
on Intel platforms. See page 33 of
SC27-0812-01 which says (in part),

"The numeric data you use in your program will have one of the following
formats
available with COBOL:
    ...
 - Binary (USAGE BINARY)
 - Native binary (USAGE COMP-5)
    ...

COMP and COMP-4 are synonymous with BINARY, and COMP-3 is synonymous with
PACKED-DECIMAL."


The bottom-line is that
  A) USAGE COMP is not portable as to how it stores data
  B) USAGE COMP is not portable as far as efficiency
  C) do NOT code USAGE COMP and assume that its "implications" are portable
across compilers or operating systems.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Max value possible in COMP field ??

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-26T09:22:16-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aaburo$29eg$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d88238ab.0204191433.27443f4a@posting.google.com> <FRhw8.123985$K5.10565071@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <rrlw8.23150$o86.928895@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <01HW.B8EC309A0073FED70DB3DC60@news-east.usenetserver.com> <XFIx8.9118$d17.387384@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <aa7s0s$nmq$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <MSKx8.9486$d17.417864@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <01HW.B8EDC27500D266FC0DB3DC60@news-east.usenetserver.com> <9B1y8.11764$Y03.655438@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <01HW.B8EE474900F18B140DB3DC60@news-east.usenetserver.com> <x3ay8.13205$d17.601390@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
Paul Raulerson opined:

<<Oh, I picked that up, I just don't think it is a good reason. A better way
to handle that (solely in my opinion, which isn't too popular on this
subject at the moment... :) would be a compiler parameter to compile with
compatibility to the predecessor system.>>

Unisys had (and actually still has) exactly such a convention in both
COBOL74 and COBOL85 in the form of its "BINARYCOMP" Compiler Control Option.
When this compiler option is TRUE, the compiler treats all COMPUTATIONAL
data items as if they had been declared BINARY for efficiency; when it is
FALSE, it treats them as declared for compatibility with the existing files.

(Note also that "compiler parameters" such as this, while allowed in the
2002 draft (where they are called "directives"), are not provided for in the
prior standards to start with. )

And it's not just *predecessor* systems; in fact, the origins of this had to
do with compatibility among products from the same company being offered at
the same time in different performance ranges.

BINARYCOMP was provided not so much for efficiency as it was to provide
assistance in migration of COBOL(68) programs to the more recent dialects.
In MCP/AS (ex-Burroughs B6700) COBOL(68), COMPUTATIONAL (and
COMPUTATIONAL-1) were indeed word-oriented integers; COMPUTATIONAL-2 was
packed-decimal.  This was contrary to, and incompatible with, the rest of
the Burroughs product line at the time.

And it's not just *predecessor* systems as Ed points out.   When the 1974
standard came out, Burroughs management insisted on a single approach to the
language and its extensions across the entire product line, and a Burroughs
Corporate Standard for ANSI-74 COBOL was developed.  This corporate standard
mandated the use of packed-decimal format for COMPUTATIONAL to assist in
migration and portability, from the smallest B1710 through the Medium System
line to the largest B7800, with a minimum of changes to the source program
anywhere in the process.

Note that of these systems only the Burroughs Large System at the time had
an architecture in which anything approaching a base-2 representation of
numerics was appropriate for COBOL in the first place.

Moreover, while manipulating a BINARY item in MCP/AS COBOL74 or COBOL85
today is undeniably faster in terms of processing time than manipulating a
COMPUTATIONAL one, for any item other than one declared with exactly 11
digits or exactly 23 digits, COMPUTATIONAL is more efficient in terms of
storage.  And I would characterize the performance difference as substantial
but not dramatic; the MCP/AS architects have gone to great lengths to
improve the performance of decimal-to-binary and binary-to-decimal
conversion operators since the days of the B6700 (where users and compiler
developers alike tried to avoid ICVD and SCRS/SCRF like the plague).    So,
yes, doing arithmetic on BINARY items (particularly 77-level BINARY items in
COBOL74) is faster, but it's not clear that that performance difference
automatically, categorically and unconditionally outweighs the advantages of
portability and compatibility for each and every user.

Given that in the '74 standard the only USAGEs are COMPUTATIONAL and
DISPLAY, I believe the Burroughs decision to make both of them portable
across several very different product offerings was a wise one.  The point
is moot for the '85 standard, because a COBOL85 compiler was never offerred
for the other products in the ex-Burroughs line, and compatibility with and
portability from MCP/AS COBOL74 to MCP/AS COBOL85 is far more important than
what Unisys chooses to call the USAGE for which arithmetic cn be said to
operate the fastest.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

---

<a id="t-65ac3c05fc"></a>
## Thread: Mainframe Software Packaging. — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Mainframe Software Packaging.

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-20T02:17:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wc4w8.15343$R33.733039@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
    I'm looking for some advice on an application 

and its packaging for the Mainframe. I'm just about to 

release my Asset Management and Recurring Billing 

packages into the OS/390 market, and to tell the truth, 

packaging is really throwing me a curve. :)  

 

  On the mainframe, these are COBOL and a few assembler 

programs, currently ported to take advantage of LE and TX Server.

I have a few issues there:

 

  I want to release a version that runs VSAM to avoid the cost of 

  DB/2, and wonder if anyone has advice about how to deliver 

  the empty VSAM files. I currently plan to just deliver a IDCAMS

  job and custom COBOL program to create, load, index, and test

  the files. This would also allow an installation to easily customize

  the VSAM catalog it uses, the sizes and other tunable parameters,

  and the names. However, I want to come up with a good workable 

  default. It takes a good deal of savvy to create the dozens of VSAM

  files and indexes necessary to run the application, and some of the

  potential customers might not have the savvy or the resources to 

  expend on a lot of customization. The IDCAMS job takes a HLQ

  as a parameter, and creates its own VSAM catalog. It also uses

  my preference for a naming schema for the files, indexes, and paths.

  Is this a reasonable approach?



  The CICS resources are all in their own group with a basic job to load

   them. It still has to be modified on site to adapt the HLQ for the 

   VSAM files.  This seems like the least possible trouble for a end user. 



   Of course, I know the system inside and out, but from a dead standing

   start, I can install the Asset Management app in about 30 mins. That 

   is without the internal setup and customization that the application 

   requires internally. (i.e. Setting up asset tag ranges and all sorts of 

   "application only" stuff.)   



A definite issue is that TX Server with LE seems to present a few 

differences from CICS/ESA. BMS maps that work perfectly well 

under CICS in a non LE environment give me junk data 

problems under TX Server in a LE region. This means that

I am doing things for LE that make it not a good idea to run under

ESA.  (?) 

 

My current thought is to 'package' the application for 

TX Server and LE. I know this limits the immediate

usefulness of the software to some people - I'm willing 

to be convinced that multi-packaging is necessary. :) 

 

Secondly, similar to what I do with workstation and 

OS/400 versions of the software, I want to offer a 

range of licensing options; from a very low cost 

binary only distribution to a version with source and 

redistribution rights. By low cost, I mean perhaps as 

low as $2500 for a single application with maintenance 

of about $1000/year. (That's the projected cost for the

Asset Management application. I use it as a bit of a

loss leader; it solves a need for some organizations 

and it gets my software in the door very fast. That's

the pricing for the AS/400 version  - I would like to 

maintain that price in the OS/390 world if at all possible.) 



I'm kind of wondering if it even makes sense to have a 

non-source distribution in the OS/390 world, and is at the 

core of this message. Would most systems folks feel comfortable

installing a binary only package? I'm more concerned with meeting



I have one client on the AS/400  who regularly customizes 

the software and puts it up as an ASP application for 

their clients. Works great for me, as I made the licensing 

cheaper for them if they gave me the right to include software fixes

they make into the general distribution. :) 

 

 Third, I would like to tap into a potential but pretty well 

non-existent Mainframe Market - small business. 

 

Short of spending a million dollars I do not have to host an 

ASP version of the software under OS/390, I think there is a 

definite S/390 market for the business willing to invest say 

$70K to $125K in their hardware and software investment. 



At that level, it will have to involve Flex, UMX, or (better yet) 

low cost MultiPrise systems. I am not at all happy with the Flex 

people. They are focused on defending their small piece 

of turf instead of growing new markets. They do an alright 

deal through some resellers for PWD development systems, 

but they are patronizing towards the idea of entering the small

business market.  I don't know enough about UMX to make any 

reasonable choices there. That leaves the MP market, which 

is 'high end' for the small business in the $1 - $10m /year market. 

Still, it is a potentially very profitable area, and opens lots of 

avenues for support. Obviously, in that market, support and 

the recurring revenue stream it generates is the primary

goal.

 

The MP is a very possible scenario, with some creative leasing

from IBM. (I love IBM, even if they can be aggravating to deal with.)  

 

Lastly, I've been tentatively exploring Linux on the S/390 platform. 

I must say, I am not convinced there is any vast advantage to it 

at this time. A z800 is impressive and nicely priced, but what the 

heck does it do to make the great price difference between it and 

an RS/6000? (Excuse me, pSeries machine.)  RS/6000's in useable 

configurations, with OS software, start in at about $2700. 

 

I do not think that Linux on an S/390 can be targeted towards a 

small business at this time, other than perhaps running under something 

like Hercules. And except for clean upward scalability, why take the 

performance hit to do that? COBOL development tools from IBM are 

not there yet either. 

 

Still, I have the nagging feeling that I am missing something here. 

Opinions welcome!

 

Sorry for the long post, but I am looking for opinions and advice 

here, even if it is something I don't really want to hear. :) 

 

Thanks 

-Paul
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe Software Packaging.

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-21T02:04:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<m6pw8.24075$o86.953634@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<wc4w8.15343$R33.733039@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC1FC71.6040401@pdq.net>`

```
Unreasonable expense and demands on my time. :)

Seriously, the business plan calls for not being onsite as much as possible,
to
keep the cost of the software low. The income is basically recurring revenue
for support. And even *that* should be an order of magnitude less expensive
than it currently is.

It also is something we think is wrong with the mainframe software
industry -
why should software be so complicated that you need to have a specialist go
onsite to install  and configure it?

After all, most Sysadmins are more than capable of managing their own
systems,
and no software anywhere should be so complex that it cannot be installed in
at
most, a couple or three hours.

We have a different philosophy as you can see. Whether or not it turns out
to
be nicely profitable yet remains to be seen, but we have faith. :)


-Paul


"Richard Fuller" <rfuller1@pdq.net> wrote in message
news:3CC1FC71.6040401@pdq.net...

> of course.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe Software Packaging.

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2002-04-21T20:10:09+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<210420022010090265%josephk@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<wc4w8.15343$R33.733039@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC1FC71.6040401@pdq.net> <m6pw8.24075$o86.953634@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
In article <m6pw8.24075$o86.953634@typhoon.austin.rr.com>, Paul
Raulerson <praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com> wrote:

> It also is something we think is wrong with the mainframe software
> industry -
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Most Unix sys admins have a fixed file heirarchy to manage.

Such a thing DOES NOT EXIST on the mainframe.

You had better make ALL your file (dataset) names options on any
install jobs.

The most frustrating thing I finf as a sysprog for mainframes is PC
types not understanding that the programs are differentiated from the
data that they operate on.

In a typical mainframe environment the same programs may operate on
production data and a number of testing environment data sets. This is
possible because the programs only ever refer to DDNAMES or CICS files.

It is the responsibility of the SITE to determine when and what those
ddnames refer to.

To create a package for a mainframe that disobeys this concept is just
asking for trouble. Unlike Unix and Windows, Mainframe packages Must
differentiate between the programs and the data. They MUST NOT be
installed in such a way to force the user to accept what the supplier
wanted, but must be capable of reflecting what the user wants. The user
in this situation is NOT the company owner, but the system programmer
that is hired to run the show.

The closer you are to standard MVS operating principals you are, the
better your product will be received.

1. Ship the Product as a SMP/E package.
There is NO EXCUSE for not doing this.

2. Provide sample JOBS to create the Dataset's that the package will
need for operation.
Clearly label those datasets that are optional. Do not worry about the
dataset names as it will be a forgone conclusion that any naming
standard you come up with will offend someone. Furthermore, the nature
of the mainframe is such that any names you come up with will be
renamed by the local standards.

3. DO NOT require a unique catalog or catalogs for your product.
Catalogs are system management things and have nothing what so ever to
do with applications.

4. Ship sample jobs to run your application. Better would by a JCL
procedure that the system programmer can copy and modify to local
standards.

5. DO NOT Name your program libraries SYS1.anything. These are by
convention reserved for the operating system and as a default usually
have the highest level of security access applied to them. Security is
a customer issue, you have no rights to force this upon them.

6. DO NOT create PLT programs for CICS systems to INSTALL CICS
resources. DO create DFHCSDUP jobs and statements to define the CICS
resources. DO NOT create documentation that describes the CICS
resources to be defined and then provide no JCL and samples to define
them.

7. Ship the manuals as BookManager files, so that the standard library
reader can read them. Optionally, supply PDF's.

Hope this helps. :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe Software Packaging.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-21T19:53:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pNEw8.20926$R33.1015039@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<wc4w8.15343$R33.733039@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CC1FC71.6040401@pdq.net> <m6pw8.24075$o86.953634@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <210420022010090265%josephk@iinet.net.au>`

```

"Joseph Katnic" <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote in message
news:210420022010090265%josephk@iinet.net.au...
> In article <m6pw8.24075$o86.953634@typhoon.austin.rr.com>, Paul
> Raulerson <praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It doesn't actually have to exist under UNIX either. We have seen a few
alien file systems we wish had never landed... (*sigh*)

> You had better make ALL your file (dataset) names options on any
> install jobs.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes, we find the same thing. By the same token though, I think there is some
value in suggesting at least a standard file naming schema for the less
technically adept. (Actually, I tend to fall into that region my self. A
system
programmer I am not, though with a little help from my friends, I manage to
get done what I need to get done. Okay, make that a LOT of help... :)



> In a typical mainframe environment the same programs may operate on
> production data and a number of testing environment data sets. This is
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Most definately, we do this in every environment, AS/400, UNIX, and
even in Windows where possible. (With Windows it isn't always possible,
but we try... )


> To create a package for a mainframe that disobeys this concept is just
> asking for trouble. Unlike Unix and Windows, Mainframe packages Must
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

MM- to some degree, I must disagree here. One thing our research has found
is that the very best shops do not have much, if any, of that separation
between
the sys progs and managment. Indeed, in those shops, management actually
makes the descisions, or at least approves them. We have seen really poor
efficiency caused by egos in other shops - those egos being either on the
sys progs side, management's side, or worse yet, on both sides.

At the bottom line, the people who authorize the money to be spent are the
people we have to satisfy, and that does mean not antagonizing their sys
progs.
It is a hard job to make *everybody* happy, but somebody has to do it!! :)
:) :)

Also, a relatively competent sys prog would have no problem changing any
dataset names they want, or setting up multiple copies of the software for
test/production etc. We have NOT built any of that knowledge into the
software
itself at this time. (In fact, that is one of the 'bones of contention' we
deal with
internally. Some of us think we should, some os us think we should not.
Mostly
we have all stated our point of view and just moved ahead. If we get it
wrong, we
will come back and do it over again - and do it more correct the
second/third/fourth,
or however many times it takes to get it right.)



> The closer you are to standard MVS operating principals you are, the
> better your product will be received.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Do itty bitty shops use SMP/E much? Is there any reason to NOT use SMP/E for
them?


> 2. Provide sample JOBS to create the Dataset's that the package will
> need for operation.
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>

<grin> Well, we internally decided on PDF as the documenation ship vehicle.
There was some - ah - disagreement about that as well. The reason PDF's got
selected is the master-of-the-documenation uses a Macintosh, and really
likes
PDF's. Also he convinced us that most people are going to view the docs on a
PC anyway so....


> Hope this helps. :)

Very much indeed! Thank you -Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: Mainframe Software Packaging.

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-21T08:18:09+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5ol4cuopdjor3cmp3lvr6pmik892ntb9dv@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<wc4w8.15343$R33.733039@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
On Sat, 20 Apr 2002 02:17:00 GMT, "Paul Raulerson"
<praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com> wrote:
> The IDCAMS job takes a HLQ as a parameter, and creates its own VSAM catalog. 

This may or may not be a good thing.  Make it variable in such a way
that the customer can decide to place the files into an already
existing catalog

>  my preference for a naming schema for the files, indexes, and paths.

Very probably not a good thing.  customers already have a decided on
name space, and are reluctant to change.  Also, they may have rules in
place (like ACS routines for SMS) which makes changing the rules ...
difficult


>  The CICS resources are all in their own group with a basic job to load
>   them. It still has to be modified on site to adapt the HLQ for the 
>   VSAM files.  This seems like the least possible trouble for a end user. 

There is an even less intrusive approach:  EXEC CICS CREATE resource,
which can define CICS resources on the fly.  You would have to provide
a PLT program to do this once on CICS startup.  Other than that, the
approach looks fine to me.  The job that builds the IDCAMS commands
can as well build the DFHCSDUP commands to populate the CSD


>A definite issue is that TX Server with LE seems to present a few 
>differences from CICS/ESA. BMS maps that work perfectly well 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>ESA.  (?) 

Hm, did you do MOVE LOW-VALUSES to mymapO before doing anything else
with the map?  If you don't  do this for CICS/ESA and above, you can
expect random data showing up in your map, and worse (PROG 705, ABEND,
whatever)

>
> 
I am not going to comment on the other issues,  I am a very happy
Flex-ES customer here, and an MP3K would be way out of my range,
monetarywise.....
                                                            
     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_        
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,

      Doing gets it done.

Thanks Volker, good advice as usual! :)

"Volker Bandke" <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com> wrote in message
news:5ol4cuopdjor3cmp3lvr6pmik892ntb9dv@4ax.com...
> On Sat, 20 Apr 2002 02:17:00 GMT, "Paul Raulerson"
> <praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com> wrote:
> > The IDCAMS job takes a HLQ as a parameter, and creates its own VSAM
catalog.
>
> This may or may not be a good thing.  Make it variable in such a way
> that the customer can decide to place the files into an already
> existing catalog
>

That could be easily controlled by changing the HLQ onsite I think. In fact,
we are kind of confused
by the intricate possibilities of this issue. For example, one potential
client is a small publishing shop
running on a P/390. They don't have the onsite skillset to do deep analysis,
so we want to have a
setup there that is simple and very easy.

Another possible client is so far out at the other end of the range they
might as well be in a different
galaxy. *Everything* for this customer is going to be changed, if for no
other reason than their current
system progs' ego won't allow anything she has not changed on her system.
They may change system
progs soon and flip to the end of the spectrum evidenced by our potential
P/390 client.

Definately making it a variable thing is good, though I wonder if we should
should setup some kinf of
user freindly configuration program? That would probably be a lot of work,
but if it is worth doing, it
might save a lot of grief later. (?)


> >  my preference for a naming schema for the files, indexes, and paths.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

While anyone with any experience at all can easily change all the DD names
and the IDCAMS
job to set them up, finding the correct balance between simplicity and
flexibilty is a very hard
job. At least it is for me. :)

For the sake of support simplicity, we would like to be able to "strongly
recommend' that all the working
datasets retain the same HLQ and the same pattern we use for indexes and
paths. No compelling technical
reason to do so, but with clients at the lower end of the scale, this will
save us hours of support issues. (Did the
IDCAMS job fail for some reason? How to find out?  Go to ISPF 3.4, enter
HLQ, press enter, and read
off the list of files you see to us please... etc. etc.)


>
> >  The CICS resources are all in their own group with a basic job to load
> >   them. It still has to be modified on site to adapt the HLQ for the
> >   VSAM files.  This seems like the least possible trouble for a end
user.
>
> There is an even less intrusive approach:  EXEC CICS CREATE resource,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
 This sounds like a great idea for most of the potential clients. Why did
none of
us think of it before? Thanks Volker, I owe you (root) beer, your
preference.

>
> >A definite issue is that TX Server with LE seems to present a few
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I think we did, but it is a great idea to go and triple check. Some of the
screens are still changing,
do that is not a difficult task to assign and accomplish.  We actually see
PROG755's (bad data)
from PCOMM, so this is probably a lurking culprit. Funny thing though, the
other emulators we test
with seem to work okay. Just PCOMM putting up a bit of a fuss...

> >
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Oh heck, I can't afford a personal MP3K myself (yet! :)  I really was
talking about clients
who are willing to spend around six digits of capital.

did not intend to slam Flex, and rereading that it looks like I did.
We have not had great success in communicating with some of your
resellers our needs and intentions, and even less success getting
quotes back from them that are appropriate to use in a quote to a
potential customer. Part of this is just that we have not dug out all the
facts regarding how you do business yet, and where, if anywhere,
our business plan and your operation can and should work together.
We are, however, at the point where we can talk a bit more openly
about our business plan and strategies, so perhaps my opinion will
soon change.

I'll restate publicly that your personal contacts with us have been
appropriate and useful.

 Thanks
-Paul

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Updated some old (bad) web links

frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).

"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".

where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-04-22T16:29:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CC439DB.9EC9EAF1@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<aa0s7t$r9q$05$1@news.t-online.com>`

```


Alfred Langer wrote:

> I make my first steps with Cobol. I want 1 line after the
> heading before the detail starts. How can I do this. My code is for
> example:
>
> 01 TPYE IS CONTROL HEADING DEPT LINE NUMBER PLUS 2.

I don't use Report Writer but go to the University of Limerick site -
there is one example using R/W which might help.

    http://www.csis.ul.ie/COBOL/examples/

You just *might* find some other examples if you go to the sites of
compiler vendors, AcuCOBOL, Fujitsu, Liant(Ryan McFarland) and Micro
Focus, etc.. Go to cobolreport.com, and check out Bill Klein's COBOL FAQ
which lists all the vendors.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```
