```
On Tue, 23 Apr 2002 11:36:57 -0400, Howard Brazee wrote
> I can't repeat the language I would use - children might be reading this
> message.

When you're lying awake with a dismal headache
And repose is taboo'd by anxiety,
I conceive you may use any language you choose
To indulge in without impropriety.
  -- W S Gilbert

If anyone cares, I'd use Fortran90. Good language, good compilers 
available at reasonable prices, don't know about IDEs.

Edward Reid
```

#### ↳ Re: What language?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-04-23T19:22:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CC5B3FB.9F2EF25F@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<fqm9cug7ith14ta32hs8gdu6s38qdod6vl@4ax.com>`

```


Nospam@neverspam.com wrote:

> This question might upset some of you; if so, I'm sorry but it has to
> be asked.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Firstly serves you right for getting involved with CA-Relia. I had a copy
before I latched on to Micro Focus VISOC, predecessor to Net Express. My
reaction to Realia was, this was just another Computer Associates tool,
amongst their many.
I got the impression they didn't have a commitment to COBOL in the sense
that Fujitsu or Micro Focus have. I dropped the product real quick. (It's
yonks ago, but I still get invites to attend their annual gabfests in
Florida or elsewhere - and I see bugger all mentioned about COBOL in their
glossies).

Your reasoning, "can't  afford to upgrade". That kinda shuts out COBOL.
Unfortunately there are no cheapies. Is it that you really feel a need to
switch to one of the sexy languages. At the PC level it narrows down to
VB, C++ or Java. Not familiar, but I think there is a price for VB. We
know that Java starts out cheap (freebie), but for serious production work
you need add-on tools like an IDE (Integrated Development Environment).
Can't recall whether CA-Realia had one, so if it doesn't, my comment might
leave you a bit perplexed - but believe me IDEs are a great boon in the PC
world. Then of course, I suspect whichever of the above three you might
choose, at some stage you are going to want some support libraries,
provided by third parties - here's where additional dollars come in.

(Quick reminder, should you switch to another COBOL,  you can still use
Screen IO).

To be honest, if I was starting from scratch on a PC, I'd go for Java with
C++ as a second. But here's a problem, you've indicated you aren't too
thrilled about the OO jazz - and these are both OO languages. You'll have
to bite the bullet on OO if you are going to make a switch.

I think you are right on the button about the less than business like
approach of explaining these 'other' languages. They just don't seem to be
co-ordinated in the same fashion as COBOL texts. Here are some texts
designed to help the COBOL programmer switch from 'christianity to heresy'
:-

- "Moving from COBOL to C" - Mo Budlong - SAMS Publishing - ISBN:
0-672-30327-2

- "Java for the COBOL Programmer" - Doke & Hardgrave - Cambridge U. Press
- ISBN : 0-521-65892-6 (has a CD)

- "Java for COBOL Programmers " - John C. Byrne -  Charles River Media -
ISBN: 1-886801-84-3 (has a CD). More comprehensive than the previous book.

- "Visual Basic for COBOL Programmers" - Greg Perry - QUE books - ISBN :
0-7897-0268-1 (has a diskette)

And as regards what the others don't cover, I have, "Pitfalls of OO
Development" with chapter headings which cover "Conceptual, Political,
Management, Analysis and Design, Environment, Language and Tool,
Implementation, Class and Object, Coding, Quality Assurance, Reuse" - all
followed by the word 'Pitfalls'. Read this guy's book and kinda looks like
everything is honky dory about files/DBs - they get no mention, not even
in the index !!!

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What language?

- **From:** Nospam@neverspam.com
- **Date:** 2002-04-24T01:46:24-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<flhccuc6jopfibsgk9ibt02mb44n1hbpil@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<fqm9cug7ith14ta32hs8gdu6s38qdod6vl@4ax.com> <3CC5B3FB.9F2EF25F@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

>Nospam@neverspam.com wrote:

>>  I have old 16 bit versions of the Realia compiler and ScreenIO but I
>> can't afford to upgrade so essentially I'm going to have to change
>> languages. The question is: to what? And how to make the process as
>> easy as possible.

>Firstly serves you right for getting involved with CA-Relia. I had a copy
>before I latched on to Micro Focus VISOC, predecessor to Net Express. My
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>glossies).

Before they were acquired by CA, they were a dynamic COBOL-committed
company. Back in the early eighties when the company I worked for was
looking for a COBOL compiler Realia were head and shoulders over the
competition, particularly:

- they simply used IBM's language manual and as we all know if IBM
says so, it's so.

- they produced 370 assembler code which although not useful for me,
impressed the hell out of the big iron systems programmers. It may
have even convinced some of them that the PC was not just a toy.

- there was no run-time fee.

- the compliler ran like a bat out of hell in comparison to such
monstrosities as RM COBOL.

- the people from Realia wore suits and white shirts at each of our
meetings. Laugh all you like but this was impressive to a conservative
company. One of the DB mfg's (I think Oracle) didn't get our business
because they dressed casually for their meeting. 

>Your reasoning, "can't  afford to upgrade". That kinda shuts out COBOL.
>Unfortunately there are no cheapies.

Unfortunately you're right.

> Is it that you really feel a need to
>switch to one of the sexy languages.

Hmmm. I don't feel a need in the sense that I'm going to get some
satisfaction out of changing. I'm being pushed to change:

- my indexed files in the application are nearing the magic 64 meg
limit at which I believe the world ends. Splitting these files up
would entail a lot of work

- I have reached and surpassed the limit of Data Division size. Each
time I expand a table those items at the end of the data division just
magically disappear. No warning from the compiler and no abend at
execution time. The data just falls into the bit bucket. This results
in a scramble to cut data items elsewhere and I'm running out of
surplus items.

- I could profit from an expanded screen (beyond 25 * 80).

- I can't run the compiler in a DOS box under NT. I have to boot up
DOS 6.1 and run full screen. Do you know what 25 * 80 looks like on a
19 inch monitor, not to mention the ugliness of having to go through
the compile and then boot NT to run the application. Oops, bug. Shut
down NT, reboot DOS...well, you get the idea.

- I would like to use a mouse.

> At the PC level it narrows down to
>VB, C++ or Java. Not familiar, but I think there is a price for VB.

Not if I have a working copy in my hot little hands, which I do.

> We
>know that Java starts out cheap (freebie), but for serious production work
>you need add-on tools like an IDE (Integrated Development Environment).
>Can't recall whether CA-Realia had one,

Not my version but I believe later ones did and do.

> so if it doesn't, my comment might
>leave you a bit perplexed - but believe me IDEs are a great boon in the PC
>world. Then of course, I suspect whichever of the above three you might
>choose, at some stage you are going to want some support libraries,
>provided by third parties - here's where additional dollars come in.

Hmmm, maybe.

>(Quick reminder, should you switch to another COBOL,  you can still use
>Screen IO).

I'm don't think that 16 bit ScreenIO will work with a 32 bit compiler.

>To be honest, if I was starting from scratch on a PC, I'd go for Java with
>C++ as a second. But here's a problem, you've indicated you aren't too
>thrilled about the OO jazz - and these are both OO languages. You'll have
>to bite the bullet on OO if you are going to make a switch.

>I think you are right on the button about the less than business like
>approach of explaining these 'other' languages. They just don't seem to be
>co-ordinated in the same fashion as COBOL texts. Here are some texts
>designed to help the COBOL programmer switch from 'christianity to heresy'

>- "Moving from COBOL to C" - Mo Budlong - SAMS Publishing - ISBN:
>0-672-30327-2

>- "Java for the COBOL Programmer" - Doke & Hardgrave - Cambridge U. Press
>- ISBN : 0-521-65892-6 (has a CD)
>
>- "Java for COBOL Programmers " - John C. Byrne -  Charles River Media -
>ISBN: 1-886801-84-3 (has a CD). More comprehensive than the previous book.

>- "Visual Basic for COBOL Programmers" - Greg Perry - QUE books - ISBN :
>0-7897-0268-1 (has a diskette)

Sounds great but unfortunately out of print and no used copies
available (I just checked on the last one).
```

```
<Nospam@neverspam.com>

> - my indexed files in the application are nearing the magic 64 meg
> limit at which I believe the world ends. Splitting these files up

I think Fujitsu recently upped their 1 Gig file limit size.

>
> - I have reached and surpassed the limit of Data Division size. Each

Again, for Fujitsu:
Maximum data item length: 2,147,483,647 bytes
Max char length of elementary item: 2G
Max len alphnumeric edited item: 2G
Max len of numeric edited item: 160
Max value of subscript: 2G
Max repeat count of occurs: 2G
Max number of keys for table element: 2G
Max depth of implicit pointer qualification: 7
For indexed files:
Max rec length: 32760
Max data names in RECORD KEY clause: 254
Max ALTERNATE RECORD KEYs: 254
Max file capacity: 1,073,741,824 bytes

I'm sure other, modern, compilers have similarly generous limitations.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What language - Realia?

- **From:** <mkozelka@remove.ameritech.net>
- **Date:** 2002-04-24T14:20:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kazx8.8918$d7.2830721@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<fqm9cug7ith14ta32hs8gdu6s38qdod6vl@4ax.com> <3CC5B3FB.9F2EF25F@shaw.ca> <flhccuc6jopfibsgk9ibt02mb44n1hbpil@4ax.com>`

```
Hi,

    Are you saying that you cannot compile & link DOS-based Realia in a
window on NT4?  We have been using Realia 4.2 (non-GUI, 16 bit) to compile &
link on NT 4.0 for years.  RealDbug also runs.  No need to reboot.

    And if you need to use the "modern" 32-bit version of COBOL from
Fujitsu, you can also compile & link it at the command prompt - no GUI. I
call this "reactionary COBOL".  I'm hanging on, tooth & nail, to the C
prompt.
```

- **Date:** 2002-04-24T22:28:53-04:00

- **References:** `<fqm9cug7ith14ta32hs8gdu6s38qdod6vl@4ax.com> <3CC5B3FB.9F2EF25F@shaw.ca> <flhccuc6jopfibsgk9ibt02mb44n1hbpil@4ax.com> <kazx8.8918$d7.2830721@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
<mkozelka@remove.ameritech.net> wrote:

>    Are you saying that you cannot compile & link DOS-based Realia in a
>window on NT4?  We have been using Realia 4.2 (non-GUI, 16 bit) to compile &
>link on NT 4.0 for years.  RealDbug also runs.  No need to reboot.

I think the problem is in the bind step. I use a batch file but
executing Realbind and just incidentally Realif and Realcopy on their
own return me immediately to the NT desktop.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What language - Realia?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** <mkozelka@remove.ameritech.net>
- **Date:** 2002-04-25T19:57:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DcZx8.9102$d7.2931670@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<fqm9cug7ith14ta32hs8gdu6s38qdod6vl@4ax.com> <3CC5B3FB.9F2EF25F@shaw.ca> <flhccuc6jopfibsgk9ibt02mb44n1hbpil@4ax.com> <kazx8.8918$d7.2830721@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <l0qecu8a431u717jvqeq7gv21j1vhabukd@4ax.com>`

```
    I just tested it again on NT 4.0, using Realia vers 5.xxx.  And Realcopy
works too.

    This is the bat file I use for Realia 5.x, using a replaceable parameter
for the source file:
>
    forcedos c:\real2\cobol %1,%1,%1
    pause
    forcedos c:\real2\optlink %1,%1,%1,
c:\real2\lib\realcob,c:\real2\lib\realexe.def
    pause
    forcedos c:\real2\realbind /E %1.EXE
>

    I put the pauses in to look for errors (never have any).

    The key is the "forcedos" command. i.e.: C:\>forcedos realcopy
data.txt[t] data.prn[u]
```

#### ↳ Re: What language?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-24T23:15:53-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aa7vu0$72q$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<fqm9cug7ith14ta32hs8gdu6s38qdod6vl@4ax.com>`

```
Lots of notes have gone back and forth on this thread.  Some of them
"silly" - some wrong - some "name calling" - but let me try and understand
what "nospam" is looking for:

1) A compiler (product) that runs on PC's (don't worry about Unix, Linux,
mainframe, whatever) and generates code for this environment

2) The entire "development" product cost should be minimal (my guess is
certainly less than $150 - and probably he/she wants less than $35).

3) The product should (is required to?) produce code for 32-bit Windows
(including Windows NT, Windows XP, Windows 2000) but also (???) for 16-bit
Windows

4) The software needs to support "screen" I/O (I think GUI is preferred, but
I have lost track of that too).

5) The software should require MINIMAL training and conversion from an
existing RM/COBOL source base

6) The software should be "optimized" to run well, support complex (indexed)
file systems, and large/complex data structures.

  ***

If this really is (are) the requirements, I think I have some ideas - but I
do want to verify first that these are the requirements.

nospam,
  Please correct whatever errors I may have made (if any)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What language?

- **From:** Nospam@neverspam.com
- **Date:** 2002-04-25T01:17:52-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eg1fcukj4gkqduvlhbego8pc1iql0g6l1o@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<fqm9cug7ith14ta32hs8gdu6s38qdod6vl@4ax.com> <aa7vu0$72q$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>Lots of notes have gone back and forth on this thread.  Some of them
>"silly" - some wrong - some "name calling" - but let me try and understand
>what "nospam" is looking for:

>1) A compiler (product) that runs on PC's (don't worry about Unix, Linux,
>mainframe, whatever) and generates code for this environment

OK

>2) The entire "development" product cost should be minimal (my guess is
>certainly less than $150 - and probably he/she wants less than $35).

It's a he and yes, those numbers would be acceptable, even the upper
one and for a really appropriate product (which I'm afraid I haven't
been able to identify yet) we could talk about double that.

>3) The product should (is required to?) produce code for 32-bit Windows
>(including Windows NT, Windows XP, Windows 2000) but also (???) for 16-bit
>Windows

Just NT presuming an upgrade to any later Windows version will not
obsolete the product. There is no reason for it to run under 16-bit
Windows. 

>4) The software needs to support "screen" I/O (I think GUI is preferred, but
>I have lost track of that too).

Well ScreenIO (Norcom's product) in a 16 bit format is what I
currently use for screen processing. This is of course character
based. I want to move to 32 bit and GUI but there's no reason (not
that I have anything against it except the cost structure) to insist
on ScreenIO. 

>5) The software should require MINIMAL training and conversion from an
>existing RM/COBOL source base

No conversion required. I will rewrite the application. As to
training...hmmm... I suppose I expect to learn as I go but it would be
nice to have some similarity to what I already know.

>6) The software should be "optimized" to run well, support complex (indexed)
>file systems, and large/complex data structures.

It should run as fast as my current application. Speed is not my
current problem and although I complain about Fujitsu's lack of
optimization, it appears that this only slows down batch processing
and is only noticeable over 100,000 records. While I exceed this
number (by multiples) I only do so a couple of times a year and I
could live with a (reasonable) speed constraint.

If you think an indexed file is complex, OK, I'll agree with that
part.

I don't know what large/complex data structures are. I'm tempted to
say it should support anything COBOL (say Fujitsu's) will support but
that might prejudice the direction of thought.

If you look at my reply to Raulerson dated 2/24/02 at 11.09 pm, you'll
see at the end a description of part of the application. I don't
believe I'm the only person in the world with a look up problem or an
end-of- or beginning-of-file problem or a display problem so I'd be
perfectly happy to consider a product that is not a language itself
but rather a solution at a much higher level. Analogy: I could build
my own word processor but it's a lot cheaper and easier to buy
WPerfect or even Word <g>.

Thanks for your consideration.
```

```
Nospam@neverspam.com wrote:

> Or looking at the postings on this group over the last couple of days
> I see a reference to Ada. Free compiler! There's one of the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> blah blah" etc. Depressing.

For an easy introduction to Ada, you may download my little booklet,
Ada Distilled, for free from http://www.adaic.org.    I include fully
coded programs, annotation of the each code example, and even
annotate the standard libraries included in the book.   For a really
simple
approach to programning Microsoft Windows in Ada, download a
free set of libraries called JEWL using the http://www.adapower.com
website.   From either site you can download a couple of free compilers.
I recommend the GNAT compiler as a starting place.   If you are going
to program Linux,  you can get a good set of libraries for that as well.

Richard Riehle
```

#### ↳ Re: What language?

- **From:** robert_s_quinn@yahoo.com (Robert Quinn)
- **Date:** 2002-04-30T21:51:50-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<db500a4f.0204302051.1356d601@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<fqm9cug7ith14ta32hs8gdu6s38qdod6vl@4ax.com>`

```
> Or looking at the postings on this group over the last couple of days
> I see a reference to Ada. Free compiler!

Since you mention Ada I will just say that it certainly is NOT dead. 
It is a great language for many people's purposes, from low-level to
systems.

Whether or not it is best for your purposes, I don't know.  If you
drop by comp.lang.ada with any questions (an active group) you could
probably throw out any technical/practical questions you have.

The language Ada is one of those love/hate topics that you see a lot
of people taking passionate stands for and against, like the writings
of Ayn Rand.  I would just say investigate and think with your own
mind about it.

Regards,
Robert Quinn
```

## Thread: some of questions for NetExpress and Pro*COBOL — 6 msgs

### some of questions for NetExpress and Pro*COBOL

- **From:** rosario.carbone@ocnuk.com (Rosario)
- **Date:** 2002-04-23T01:06:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38555a5a.0204230006.2eaf8c5@posting.google.com>`

```
First of all I want to tell I have not any tools at moment but I will
have next days,
I also do not have manuals of both Oracle and NetExpress. I need to
know before I
go toa client and decide how long can take the configuration.

Suppose I want access to a relational db oracle by embedding SQL
statements
(from a Pro*COBOL program). You know NetExpress includes 
three SQL preprocessors: OpenESQL, COBSQL and another one that is for
DB2.
Suppose I choice COBSQL for connect to Oracle.
You know that COBSQL is an integrated preprocessor designed to work
WITH COBOL
precompilers supplied by relational database vendors. It is intended
for use WITH:

Oracle Pro*COBOL Version 1.8  or Oracle Pro*COBOL Version 8.04 

Now the first question is Oracle Pro*COBOL Version 1.8  or Oracle
Pro*COBOL Version 8.04
are embedded in the preprocessor COBSQL or not? It works WITH or
WITHOUT the precompiler
separately installed? If the precompiler is included into the
preprocessor so I don't need install the precompiler on my machine to
compile a source,
I do not need ORACLE at all on my machine to compile? And how can I
know which version of
precompiler is used? If the precompiler is not included into the
preprocessor it means I
need in order to compile a source (in NetExpress with sql embedded) to
tell to NetExpress
somewhere into the directives (?)
where the precompiler is, which is the version etc etc, in which way?

Reading on MicroFocus website it is not clear at all, but when they
talk about the
COBSQL directives they say:

COBSQLTYPE (CSQLT) Specifies which precompiler to use (ORACLE, SYBASE
or INFORMIX-NEW);
  for example, COBSQLTYPE=ORACLE

Maybe COBSQLTYPE=ORACLE specifies also which version of precompiler
you want to use,
but I really do not know.

Any help will be appreciate.

Rosario Carbone
```

#### ↳ Re: some of questions for NetExpress and Pro*COBOL

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-04-23T17:38:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CC59BAA.39ACFF7C@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<38555a5a.0204230006.2eaf8c5@posting.google.com>`

```


Rosario wrote:

> First of all I want to tell I have not any tools at moment but I will
> have next days,
> I also do not have manuals of both Oracle and NetExpress. I need to
> know before I
> go toa client and decide how long can take the configuration. <snip>

I can't specifically answer your questions, because I don't use Oracle. As
you say, you don't have the Net Express manuals. Actually there aren't any
printed manuals, but when you get Net Express, in addition to standard
help,  it does have a fairly extensive on line  book for SQL and DBs.
Hopefully this will be detailed enough to answer most of your questions.
It covers all the major DBs.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: some of questions for NetExpress and Pro*COBOL

- **From:** dan.passarella@microfocus.com (Dan Passarella)
- **Date:** 2002-04-23T13:02:30-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f4831e2c.0204231202.30b4772b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<38555a5a.0204230006.2eaf8c5@posting.google.com>`

```
rosario.carbone@ocnuk.com (Rosario) wrote in message news:<38555a5a.0204230006.2eaf8c5@posting.google.com>...
> First of all I want to tell I have not any tools at moment but I will
> have next days,
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> where the precompiler is, which is the version etc etc, in which way?

COBSQL is used to preprocess the output of DBMS vendors precompilers.
So you would need to install Pro*COBOL from Oracle before using
COBSQL. COBSQL does not contain the Oracle precompilers but it will
invoke them for you depending on the directives set. If you use the
following directive CSQLT=ORA COBSQL will try to invoke the 1.8
precompiler if you use CSQLT=ORA8 COBSQL will try to invoke the 8.4
precompiler from Oracle. When you get your NetExpress product go to
the Online documentation, Database Access, Chapter 11 COBSQL. There
are Oracle sections there to answer you questions in more detail.

I recommend setting up Oracle first, make sure you can precompile
their sample programs using just ProCOBOL. Also make sure you can
connect to an Oracle database if you are planning on animating you
programs, using SQLPlus or another tool. In NexExpress you are just
going to add the program to a project, select COBSQL as your
preprocessor and add any COBSQL directives and Oracle directives you
need. The Online doc gives good examples of sample sets of directives
needed when using COBSQL + Pro*COBOL.


> 
> Reading on MicroFocus website it is not clear at all, but when they
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Rosario Carbone
```

```

http://support.merant.com/websupport/supportnet/microfocus/docs/books/nx31books/dbpubb03.htm
```

```
   LINKAGE SECTION. 
   01 PARAM1     PIC X(48). 
   01 PARAM2     PIC X(48). 

   PROCEDURE DIVISION USING PARAM1, PARAM2.

   on AS400 each PARAM is being given as 48 characters. 

   Fixed length parameters. 

   I donot want to do it like that. 
   I want to have a single string. Length is immaterieal. 
   Variable length.
```

- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aa74sg$5p0$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<f0bf00c.0204241105.4467dc29@posting.google.com>`

```
    LINKAGE SECTION.
    01 PARAM1.
         05 Each-Byte1 occurs 1 to 48 times     PIC X.
    01 PARAM2.
        05 Each-Byte2 occurs 1 to 48 times     PIC X.

    PROCEDURE DIVISION USING PARAM1, PARAM2.

If you KNOW the length when you PASS the parameter, then add an Occurs
Depending On phrase.
```

```
wrote:

>Hi !

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

do 
linkage...
01 param-size pic 9(4).
01 param-all pic x(5000).

procedure using param-size param-all.

aaa.
move param-all(1:param-size) to working-storage-variable.

then only work with this working-storage-variable.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Variable length Params in Linkage section

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-04-25T10:17:17+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CC7C99D.5CCAFB15@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<f0bf00c.0204241105.4467dc29@posting.google.com> <i56ecu034qis327uu4mpidsm3crvvlv1lq@4ax.com>`

```
Frederico Fonseca wrote:

> do
> linkage...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> the size of the string (param-all).

Only because you have designed it that way.  It can be done without a
specific size being passed as long as there is some mechanism for
stopping the subroutine accessing data outside the actual data area of
the caller.

For example a null (x"00") could be used as a string terminator to
indicate not to access beyond that.  As long as the string parameter is
read-only then there should be no problem being passed various data
lengths.

However if the routine is to be written to be the subroutine then (as in
all languages) you had beeter be certain that the caller has created an
actual data area large enough to take whatever the subroutine will
write.

eg routine:
       LINKAGE SECTION.
       01  Param                PIC X(5000).
       PROCEDURE DIVISION USING Param.
           UNSTRING Param
               DELIMITED BY x"00"
               INTO WS-Area
           ...

caller:
       01  Small-string         PIC X(10) VALUE "Small" & x"00".
           CALL "routine" USING Small-String

This should be no problem (YMMV depending on hardware checking).

If, however, the routine does:

        MOVE "X"       TO Param

Then the program will overwrite the 10 byte Small-String (with 'X' and 9
spaces) and then overwite the next 4990 bytes in the calling program
with spaces causing severe disruption.
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable length Params in Linkage section

- **From:** Doug Scott <dwscott@ieee.org>
- **Date:** 2002-04-24T22:11:13+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VA.000001cf.08b88155@ieee.org>`
- **References:** `<f0bf00c.0204241105.4467dc29@posting.google.com>`

```
Mary,

> Fixed length parameters. 
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>    And I will parse the parameter(s) in my Code.

Don't be silly. This costs CPU time. The point about developing in Cobol 
is that it runs extremely fast. If you spend your time decoding messages 
rather than acting upon them, you'll begin to achieve MS execution times, 
and that's no good for your users.

Sigh. But if you insist, you could define a single string, length 96, 
redefined as 96 bytes (PIC X(96)) and do your own thing.

---

Doug

dwscott@ieee.org
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Variable length Params in Linkage section

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-04-25T09:53:53-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B8ED82B100C36C130DB3DC60@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<f0bf00c.0204241105.4467dc29@posting.google.com> <VA.000001cf.08b88155@ieee.org>`

```
On Wed, 24 Apr 2002 17:11:13 -0400, Doug Scott wrote
> Don't be silly. This costs CPU time. The point about developing in Cobol 
> is that it runs extremely fast.

It's possible to develop COBOL programs that run extremely fast, but 
the language is certainly *not* very helpful in this respect. There are 
all kinds of constructs in COBOL that allow you to waste a lot of CPU 
time, and you won't have any idea what's happening if you have no 
background in assembler (on at least some system). I've seen many quite 
competent COBOL programmers who can't figure out where the CPU time is 
going if their lives depend on it.

What saves them -- and why I call them quite competent nonetheless -- 
are two things. One is that the applications they are developing don't 
often lead them to do severely CPU-expensive things and don't generally 
require much algorithmic analysis. The other is that CPUs have become 
so powerful that large organizations find it easier to throw a lot of 
CPU at the problem than to find programmers who can optimize for CPU 
time.

> If you spend your time decoding messages 
> rather than acting upon them, you'll begin to achieve MS execution times, 
> and that's no good for your users.

If the code executes a thousand times per second, then by all means use 
code which optimizes the CPU time.

If the code executes once a day, then by all means use code which 
optimizes the programmer's time.

Somewhere in between lies the break-even point. Most of the time, 
nowadays, the answer is to optimize programmer time, design programs 
well to avoid inefficiencies that are hard to fix, and then consider 
CPU time when and where necessary.

Efficiency means various things, but on an average day it's the 
programmer efficiency that's most important.

Edward Reid
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Variable length Params in Linkage section

```
This is the point I was trying to reach... in an email I sent on this 
subject I stated it thusly:

--begin quoted text:


 Exactly... for the comfort and ease of all involved (especially the one
 who gets the 3:am Prod Support call) the code should read:

 LINKAGE SECTION.
      COPY LINKPARM.
 ...
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 ...
      COPY WSLKPARM.
 ...
 PROCEDURE DIVISION USING SYSTEMWIDE-LINKPARMS.
 ...
      MOVE SYSTEMWIDE-LINKPARMS  TO  WS-SYSTEMWIDE-LINKPARMS.

 ... or so I was taught that The Professionals do it.

--end quoted text

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Variable length Params in Linkage section

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-25T01:18:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<APIx8.9198$d17.390006@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<f0bf00c.0204241105.4467dc29@posting.google.com> <aa7i31$3tm$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Actually, I do some of that here, though in a slightly more sophisticated
way.
I simply pass the address in with the Linkage section though.

I often have to deal with an unknown number (to the called program) of
arguments.
In a most un-COBOLish way, I simply pass one argument, and do some 'pointer
arithmetic'
till I find a null, and then I have the end of my list. It is much simpler
to code and understand than
the alternative of passing in several hundred arguments, and copying them in
till I
hit a NULL. Then copying them back to the linkage section when it is time to
return.


-Paul

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:aa7i31$3tm$1@panix1.panix.com...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Variable length Params in Linkage section

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-04-25T11:50:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<14Sx8.9690$d96.4374@nwrddc04.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<f0bf00c.0204241105.4467dc29@posting.google.com> <aa7i31$3tm$1@panix1.panix.com> <APIx8.9198$d17.390006@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <aa7oat$ik5$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:aa7oat$ik5$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <APIx8.9198$d17.390006@typhoon.austin.rr.com>,
> Paul Raulerson <praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> so again?

I do not find this weird at all; I do it all the time.

If you want a library call or "internal utility" usable by many many
programs or jobs,  it only stands to reason that some programs may need
extra or other options than needed by some other programs.

Look at a simple example: such a utility program may have a "purge" or
"update" option in addition to a report option. You might run the job ...

EXEC PGM=FOO, PARM='REPORT'      << report only
or
EXEC PGM=FOO,PARM='REPORT,UPDATE'  << report and update

Variable number of options: Reasonable, yes?

We might be getting into semantics here about what is an 'argument' versus
what is an 'option' ... I see no reason for a variable number of 'arguments'
mostly because it's darn near impossible (for me) to set up the calls and
handle the parameter chain. (This refers to CALLs rather than EXECs. But as
I understand it, walking the argumment chain in linkage is possible).

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Variable length Params in Linkage section

```
>Variable number of options: Reasonable, yes?

The picayune rebuttal would be 'Reasonable, no; it violates the dictum of 
'Thou Shalt Not Have A Report That Updates'... but such things aside your 
program can perform only (m) functions, each of which is related to a parm 
of (n) characters.  Variable, yes... unknown, no.

>
>We might be getting into semantics here about what is an 'argument' versus
>what is an 'option'

Perhaps there might be a confusion between 'variable' and 'unknown'.

DD
k
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Variable length Params in Linkage section

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-04-25T02:30:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CC769C0.97188600@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<f0bf00c.0204241105.4467dc29@posting.google.com> <aa7i31$3tm$1@panix1.panix.com> <APIx8.9198$d17.390006@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```


Paul Raulerson wrote:

> Actually, I do some of that here, though in a slightly more sophisticated
> way.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

If only <G>. Extending your description and using OO COBOL -

Sending Class :
- create a collection, CollectionA, containing the individual arguments as
separate elements. Invoke  the receiver :-

 invoke ReceivingClass "thismethodX" using CollectionA

Receiving Class:
Working-storage section.
01 os-CollectionC                                object reference.

method-id. "thisMethodX".
   Linkage section.
    01 lnk-CollectionB                object reference.
    Procedure Division using lnk-CollectionB

    set os-CollectionC to lnk-CollectionB
    invoke lnk-CollectionB/os-CollectionC "getsize" returning thisSize
            (it automatically knows its own size)

.........then you go on from there ..........

Actually if I'm only going to action the recipient collection in the one method,
I don't even bother to set up the reference for os-CollectionC - the latter is
only necessary globally if it is referenced in several different methods.

Now unlike what you are doing there is no need to return the list of changed
arguments back through linkage. CollectionA, lnk-CollectionB or os-CollectionC
are all references to the same object, each referencing the one object in its
current state..

'Course you knew this, 'cos you use other OO languages. <G>

Jimmy
```

```
code Jimmy... :)
Unfortunately, the inbound argument list is from two other languages, PERL,
and C.
What comes in is just a set of pointers.

In fact, that is exactly what you get with the copying thing, except you
cannot use normal
operations like ADD on a linkage section variable. What a nuisance.

-Paul
```

<a id="t-6ea8c16f23"></a>
## Thread: Limitation in SQL SELECT statement — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Limitation in SQL SELECT statement

- **From:** klaus.duttle@icongmbh.de (Klaus Duttle)
- **Date:** 2002-04-25T05:15:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c1202264.0204250415.2b6a4b1@posting.google.com>`

```
Actually I'm porting a cobol host application with embedded SQL
to Fujitsu Cobol Version 3.0 on Windows 2000. The DBMS is IBM's
DB2 in Version 7.2.

It seems that there is a awful limitation in the SQL SELECT
statement. The number of selected columns in the database table
is restricted to a magic value (lower than 59).

      EXEC SQL
         SELECT
            column1,
            column2,
            ...
            column59
         INTO :host-var-structure
         FROM table1
         WHERE column1 = :SELECT-REC-ID
      END-EXEC

When I reduce the number of selected table columns to 30 or 40
columns my program runs properly, otherwise it will be stopped
by the OS in order to a memory violation. I have changed the
subset of selected columns to be sure there is no other problem.

Any help will be appreciated. 

Please send a copy of Your response to my eMail address:
klaus.duttle@icongmbh.de

thanx

Klaus Duttle
```

#### ↳ Re: Limitation in SQL SELECT statement

- **From:** "Leahy, Don" <zzdlf@tdbank.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-04-25T09:11:44-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lgTx8.75803$QR1.2291623@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<c1202264.0204250415.2b6a4b1@posting.google.com>`

```
Have you tried replacing :host-var-structure with a list of host variable
names?

          EXEC SQL
             SELECT
               column1,
               column2,
               ...
               column59
             INTO
               :host-column1
              ,:host-column2
              ...
              ,:host-column59

> to Fujitsu Cobol Version 3.0 on Windows 2000. The DBMS is IBM's
```

```
On 25 Apr 2002 05:15:56 -0700 klaus.duttle@icongmbh.de (Klaus Duttle) wrote:

:>to Fujitsu Cobol Version 3.0 on Windows 2000. The DBMS is IBM's

:>It seems that there is a awful limitation in the SQL SELECT
:>statement. The number of selected columns in the database table
:>is restricted to a magic value (lower than 59).

:>      EXEC SQL
:>         SELECT
:>            column1,
:>            column2,
:>            ...
:>            column59
:>         INTO :host-var-structure
:>         FROM table1
:>         WHERE column1 = :SELECT-REC-ID
:>      END-EXEC

:>When I reduce the number of selected table columns to 30 or 40
:>columns my program runs properly, otherwise it will be stopped
:>by the OS in order to a memory violation. I have changed the
:>subset of selected columns to be sure there is no other problem.

I would suggest trying column by column until you figure out which one is
causing the error and then examine the storage definition for that column.
```

## Thread: Sneaking OO into the Mainframe — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Sneaking OO into the Mainframe

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-04-25T14:27:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aa93f8$70s$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Does anybody have suggestions or examples of OO CoBOL that one might have a
chance of getting accepted to do tasks in an IBM mainframe environment?   To
get a foothold.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sneaking OO into the Mainframe

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-04-25T17:54:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CC84248.6E02C253@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<aa93f8$70s$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> Does anybody have suggestions or examples of OO CoBOL that one might have a
> chance of getting accepted to do tasks in an IBM mainframe environment?   To
> get a foothold.

You knew I had to respond <G>. Hmmmm. Softly, softly, catchee monkey. Obviously
don't go for a 'biggie'. Let's take your recurring theme, maintenance and the
necessity of testing umpteen programs as a result of a change.

I don't knw how you are setup or your breakdown between  the following :-

1 - calling 'functions' via sub-programs or stand-alone programs
2 - copyfiles, slipped into place in an exisiting program with a routine (
copies of  PERFORM(s) doing functions).

There's no immediate merit in trying to do #1 but #2 is an 'OBVIOUS'. If you do
use #2, is it perhaps also broken down into separate copyfiles - do some of
these 'separates' have an association ( in the lingo 'are they operating on the
same type of object' ?). Given say, that you had copyfiles which are the
equivalent of my routines for Imperial/Metric and Metric/Imperial conversions,
that would fit ideally into OO.

Hopefully you've picked up from me and others that you can have instances, one
source but many different 'carbon copies' functioning. My above Conversion
routines are written this way - purely because I hadn't realized that there can
be a difference between FACTORY methods and INSTANCE (Object Section methods).

In essence, you ONLY need instances when you are creating different objects to
invoke a common class. Example, I need three screens in a program (Dialogs) - I
can invoke the common support class MyDialog three times :-

invoke MyDialog "new" returning Dialog1
invoke MyDialog "new" returning Dialog2
invoke MyDialog "new" returning Dialog3

They all reference the class MyDialog, but I have three using the common methods
contained in the OBJECT SECTION of the program MyDialog.

(Now - I only truly got the significance of when you would use instances, from a
message that Bill wrote. Instead of the Bible on his bedside table, he has a
copy of the J4 Standards  <G>).

Now back to you invoking classes and my Conversion class above. As written it
does invoke "new" - but it need not have be written that way. You aren't
interested in several instances of Conversion, one will suffice for your needs.
A class can be designed to ONLY contain methods in the FACTORY - WITHOUT any
invoking "new", (which has to be a Factory Method), plus the program doesn't
have to have an active OBJECT SECTION. So........

*>----------

Class-id.                    ConvertValues
                                  inherits from IBM-Base (whatever it is)

Class-Control            ConvertValues is class "convert". *> = name of your
source
*>----------------------
FACTORY.
*>---------------------
       Method-id. "ImpToMet".
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       Linkage section.
       01 lnk-inches                   pic 9(03)v9(03) comp-3.
       01 lnk-mms                      pic 9(03)v9(02) comp-3.

       Procedure Division using     lnk-inches
                          returning lnk-mms.

          if  lnk-inches <> zeroes
              compute lnk-mms rounded = lnk-inches * 25.4

          else move zeroes to lnk-mms
          End-if

       End Method "ImpToMet".
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "MetToImp".
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "CtoF".
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "FtoC".
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "KpaToPsi".
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "PsiToKpa".
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       OBJECT.
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
        *> Note : doesn't contain any methods
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       END OBJECT.
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------

       END CLASS ConvertValues.
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------

You want to use the 'Imp-to-Met" method from an exisitng program :-

*>---------------------- your caling program ---------------

Program-id.            ?????

Class-Control            ConvertValues is class "convert".

Working-storage Section.
01 ws-inches                   pic 9(03)v9(03) comp-3.
01 ws-mms                      pic 9(03)v9(02) comp-3.

Procedure Division.

move in-value to ws-inches
invoke ConvertValues "Imp-to-Met" using ws-inches returning ws-mms

move in-value2 to ws-mmms
invoke ConvertValues "Met-to-Imp" using ws-mms returning ws-inches

- See - No object reference - you are invoking a Factory method directly by the
name ConvertValues.

Food for thought - and it is a starting point. Sing out for more help as
necessary.

PS: Should you get a copy of Will Price's book "Elements of OO", for tutorial
purposes and to avoid confusing students, he tends to do it all in the FACTORY.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sneaking OO into the Mainframe

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-04-25T20:56:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CC86D16.5FF07AC5@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<aa93f8$70s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <aa9in9$ef0$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <aa9k8n$g7g$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
That kind of kyboshes it for you in the real world. As to a cheap testing
compiler - Net Express University Edition $100 US ?? And while not yet part of
the Standard, also contains comprehensive collections/dictionaries. You would
definitely find the debugger (Animator) to be 'sexy'.

So you are from U of C - tell 'em, that should do the trick <G>

Not that I think you are interested currently, but the N/E UE doesn't contain
the Java classes that Version 3.1 does. But I am getting the very strong feeling
that Java is going to be invoked extensively by OO COBOL users, with a total
disregard for any GUI classes currently built into either Fujitsu or Net
Express..( A corollary - I used VISOC,  I had to move to Net Express. I use
COBOL GUIs, I will have to move to Java GUIs). It's a pain in the ass, but it
doesn't look long term that we will have much option.

The above - here we come back to the bloody Standards again. Java is open ended,
so you can make it do what you want in different OS environments, plus it is, at
least initially, a FREEBIE. On the other hand, COBOL vanilla flavoured and
shut-in, refusing to acknowledge OS, unless you pay your bucks for a compiler
that nods at a particular OS.

And people seriously question that COBOL will reduce in popularity ? ("I've used
COBOL since Pontius was a Pilot, and it will still be around in my grandson's
time, when all those sexy languages have disappeared, Etcetera, Etcetera
..".(Yul Brynner)
 Do one of those four-option quizzes :-

Student leaves school, wants to work in an interesting Environment :-

(a) Buys COBOL for around $3,500 Windows, $4,000 .Net, $4,000 Linux
(b) Uses Java - it's free
(c) Buys COBOL and uses Java
(d) Doesn't buy COBOL, doesn't use Java

Re: above - disregard the mainframes, they are in a different questionnaire.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sneaking OO into the Mainframe

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-25T19:46:12-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aaa80t$daq$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<aa93f8$70s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <aa9in9$ef0$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <aa9k8n$g7g$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
(as I indicated in my original note), I can't recommend too much,

   DO NOT try, use, play with, or anything else

the OO support in IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM.

It relies on SOM (denegrated by IBM - and with support removed in current
LE); its syntax doesn't even match IBM's direction (much less the proposed
ANSI/ISO Standard); and generally won't help you get into OO COBOL for IBM
environments.

*IF* you want to "play with" IBM OO COBOL (in a "supported") environment,
try working with VisualAge COBOL.

If you are SO masochistic that you feel compulsed to use the OO support in
IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM,

1) Check out the sample application (based on the NEW syntax and support) at
(and around):
   http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/IGY3PG00/5.1.1?
you will need to modify it (slightly) for the old SOM based environment, but
it can show you how IBM sees its OO can be used.

2) CAREFULLY read "4.5.2 Upgrading SOM-based object-oriented (OO) COBOL
programs"
at
 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3mg00/4.5.2?
```

#### ↳ Re: Sneaking OO into the Mainframe

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>

- **References:** `<aa93f8$70s$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
I see you are using IDMS. Could you use some IDMS "objects"? (This would be
encapsulated functionality where the linked Master/Slave sets would be
incorporated into an Object. Say, a Payment Object (from Bursars). All set
navigation and linkage could be incorporated into a single Payment Object
and the Payment Object could have methods like:

Make a Payment.
Reverse a Payment.
Report payments between two dates.
Get Sum of payments between two dates.
Create audit trail.
Show all payments from a given Bursar (within a given period).

It would probably involve setting up a Bursar Class and a Payments Class.

If this sounds feasible I'd be happy to write something and give you the
Source, if you give me details of the sets involved.

Alternatively, what about some current Objects that are components? I can
fairly easily "unwrap" some of my existing components down to the OO COBOL
source. Data entry numeric validation and comprehensive date handling come
to mind.

The question I have to ask though, Howard is: Can you compile and execute OO
COBOL in your existing environment?

I understood from what I've read here that IBM are withdrawing support for
it due to lack of response.

Pete.
```

## Thread: Processing Variable Length Files — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Processing Variable Length Files

- **From:** smlucas@flashmail.com (Crimefighter)
- **Date:** 2002-04-25T13:03:05-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c0ff08c.0204251203.645d483a@posting.google.com>`

```
I am in the process of writing a program that will read in a variable
length record of streaming data which happens to be the HIPAA 837
definition, of which the fields are seperated by the '*' character and
the end of record is defined by the "~" character.  Biggest question I
have is how to code it to read in the record then split it up into
seperate fields to be inserted into the segments they belong to.  The
COBOL books I have don't give me much help at all, they all deal with
flat and indexed files.
```

#### ↳ Re: Processing Variable Length Files

- **From:** "Ron" <Ron@NoSpamForMe.Com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-25T15:53:54-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aa9qff$bnt$1@suaar1ac.prod.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<c0ff08c.0204251203.645d483a@posting.google.com>`

```
Look at the UNSTRING verb which is designed to do this,
as in:  unstring input-record delimited by '*' into fld1 fld2 fld3....


"Crimefighter" <smlucas@flashmail.com> wrote in message
news:c0ff08c.0204251203.645d483a@posting.google.com...
> I am in the process of writing a program that will read in a variable
> length record of streaming data which happens to be the HIPAA 837
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> flat and indexed files.
```

#### ↳ Re: Processing Variable Length Files

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-04-25T21:02:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<S9_x8.44387$t65.14295@nwrddc02.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<c0ff08c.0204251203.645d483a@posting.google.com>`

```
Crimefighter <smlucas@flashmail.com> wrote in message
news:c0ff08c.0204251203.645d483a@posting.google.com...
> I am in the process of writing a program that will read in a variable
> length record of streaming data which happens to be the HIPAA 837
> definition, of which the fields are seperated by the '*' character and
> the end of record is defined by the "~" character.

Actually, the fields are delimited by the element separator, defined as the
first character following the 'ISA' segment identifier of the Interchange
Header segment and ended by the segment terminator, defined as the first
character following element 16 of that segment. Fields are also delimited by
the subelement separator, which is element 16 of that segment, but only if
the Interchange Standard (element 15) is at least '00302'

But go ahead and hard-code it if you like.

> Biggest question I
> have is how to code it to read in the record then split it up into
> seperate fields to be inserted into the segments they belong to.  The
> COBOL books I have don't give me much help at all, they all deal with
> flat and indexed files.

UNSTRING is good, except that assumes you have a count of the elements in a
segment or do it one element at a time using the POINTER option of UNSTRING.
So is reading the input byte-by-byte until you hit a magic character. That's
about the limit of your choices.

In fairness, this is NOT simple code to write. And just wait until you have
to code for malformed EDI!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Processing Variable Length Files

- **From:** Steven Lucas <smlucas@famvid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-25T18:20:58-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CC88F59.E94C61B9@famvid.com>`
- **References:** `<c0ff08c.0204251203.645d483a@posting.google.com> <S9_x8.44387$t65.14295@nwrddc02.gnilink.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:

> Crimefighter <smlucas@flashmail.com> wrote in message
> news:c0ff08c.0204251203.645d483a@posting.google.com...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> the Interchange Standard (element 15) is at least '00302'

Well, thanks for that tip, this whole HIPAA mess was thrown in my lap and I've
only gotten as far as the data division definitions of all the loops.

> > Biggest question I
> > have is how to code it to read in the record then split it up into
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

Great...I know it was much easier to split strings with perl and never done it
with COBOL.

> In fairness, this is NOT simple code to write. And just wait until you have
> to code for malformed EDI!

Malformed EDI?  Well, I'm not familiar with it...explain?
```

#### ↳ Re: Processing Variable Length Files

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-25T19:52:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aaa8c7$g8q$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<c0ff08c.0204251203.645d483a@posting.google.com>`

```
Have you told us what platform and compiler you are using?

Everyone who has recommended UNSTRING is giving "reasonable" advice - but
only when you have data "read" into your program.

The suggestion about reading "byte-by-byte" is PROBABLY the best way to get
your data into your program, but depending on the operating system and
COBOL, there MIGHT be better ways to do it (for example, if they support -
which most compilers don't) a useful version of the PADDING character phrase
to "delimit" your records.
```

```
>

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

See UNSTRING:

        MOVE SPACES   TO field-block			
        UNSTRING Stream-Data
            DELIMITED BY "*" OR "~"
            INTO field1
                 field2
                 etc
```

#### ↳ Re: Processing Variable Length Files

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2002-04-26T12:46:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cc94a6e.1173339@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<c0ff08c.0204251203.645d483a@posting.google.com>`

```
smlucas@flashmail.com (Crimefighter) wrote:

>I am in the process of writing a program that will read in a variable
>length record of streaming data which happens to be the HIPAA 837
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>flat and indexed files.

Crimefighter:

One of my customers has already developed a tool which translates
records into HIPPAA compliant format.

It supports variable length records and translates the record in real
time as well.  The tool was designed to allow you to use it in any
COBOL application.

You can contact him at "Dario Navarro" <dtnavarro@yahoo.com>

I'm not sure if this helps, but I figured that if it already exists,
why re-invent the wheel?




Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Processing Variable Length Files

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-04-26T16:42:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<srfy8.1353$mF4.974@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<c0ff08c.0204251203.645d483a@posting.google.com> <3cc94a6e.1173339@news.epix.net>`

```
Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote in message
news:3cc94a6e.1173339@news.epix.net...
> smlucas@flashmail.com (Crimefighter) wrote:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> COBOL application.

There are a ton of software products available to parse and map ANSI ASC X12
EDI and have been for years. Unfortunately, they are all expensive to
license and require you learn a whole suite of products, and all the "big
guys'" products are designed to support all standards, all documents and
about eighteen thousand options; there really is a dearth of limited-purpose
stuff available. However, I expect there will be a bunch of
reasonably-priced "HIPPA-EDI-only" libraries offered over the next two
years.

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: Processing Variable Length Files

- **From:** jnjsle@aol.com (Jnjsle)
- **Date:** 2002-04-27T21:33:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020427173355.22942.00004469@mb-cn.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<c0ff08c.0204251203.645d483a@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi SM,

Here are a few ques that need answers before you can begin devising a solution:

1)  Do the "variable" recs contain  RDWs? These are 4 byte prefixes to the data
portion of the rec that contain the block and rec lengths. These will guide you
in defining the file to your compiler. If you choose the "unstring" solution
this may not be important, but if you choose the "byte-by-byte" solution,
knowing the length of the field can be helpful, if not downright crucial. 

2)  I presume that each variable length rec contains a fixed # of fields
(including the null fields, defined as ...**...). Is that correct?

3)  How does the data provider handle separator chars (* ~) in the text string?
Most "wrap" the text in yet another separator char.  Is this situation possible
in your case? 

Regards, Jack.
```

## Thread: Dynamic file naming & HP3000 Buffer size issues — 4 msgs

### Dynamic file naming & HP3000 Buffer size issues

- **From:** marydegallo@yahoo.com (Mary Degallo)
- **Date:** 2002-04-26T09:12:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.sys.hp.mpe,alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f0bf00c.0204260812.7579f4d1@posting.google.com>`

```
hi Everybody!

   thanx for those participating in the discussion and providing valuable 
   help to newbies like me. 

   ---------------------------------------
   FILENAME known at RunTime
   FILENAME is NOT known at COMPILE time.
   ---------------------------------------
   C, C++, Java provide a file name that can be specified at RunTime. 
   Where as the way the FD and File Sections in COBOL are, 
    we have to specify the file name at COMPILE time. 
   We have to design our applications around this "feature/limitation". 
   Has anybody, worked around this, using some other "tricks".
   JCL is one way, but we donot want to get into JCL stuff. 
   How does one do it in a more elegant way? 

   

   ----------------------------------------
   HP3000 buffer sizes etc,...
   ----------------------------------------
   We have to read this data from a THIRD PARTY software objects. 
   And these Objects can run into 1 or 2 or 4 or somtimes even 7 Megs of Data.
   Do I have to define such a HUGE working Storage Area, to read the 
   Object data Into?

   I could define a 1K or 2K kind of Buffers and tested my code on AS400. 
   
   How will the scenario change when it becomes, a 1 MEG buffer?
    1.   What are the issues involved. 
    2.   Can we do it? 
    
   Some geeks in my office are playing "C++, JAVA vs  COBOL"  politics  in 
    issues like these.    Well its not new anyways.      :) 

   thanx in advance. 
-Mary
```

```
While you might be able to fix the problem by fiddling about with paths,
that is not the way I would do it.  I would set up a project library for the
source code, and place a copy of the QPR file in that area.  While that may
seem to defeat the purpose of the copy, that particular file may evolve as
you develope your approach to using Formprint.  It also means that you can
convert systems separately later if you upgrade versions.

Donald
```

## Thread: OT : Decision Tables — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT : Decision Tables

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-04-27T08:35:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CCA6270.442153C0@shaw.ca>`

```
What a rip-off. Couldn't sleep so got up for a drink. There was one of
those Scratch and Win cards on the table when I bought some Cadbury's
chocolate. What the hell, I'll give it a shot. Rules scratch three and
you could possibly win. Scratch a fourth one and you are SOL - your card
is voided..

The possible wins :     (H) - Sony Home Theatre System
                                  (M) - Sony Mini Component System
                                  ($)   - Dollars
                                  (S)  - Sorry you are SOL

My card            A1 = H    A2 = M    A3 = H
                         B1 = $     B2 = S      B3 = $
                         C1 = $     C2 = S     C3 = M

Me - I picked A2, B3 and C2 - zilch. As you can see, the only 'win'
possibility is B1, B3 and C1.

Not strictly a Decision table, rather it is three-dimensional and I
suppose it could be applied to something like a technical control panel
with lights, Red = Off and Green = On.

Not being a math whiz (by a loooong shot), just how many permutations
are there ?
Assuming it *was* a control panel, how could it be handled coding in
COBOL ?

Have fun - and for Gawd's sake don't knock somebody if they use GO TO or
EXIT !!!!

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Decision Tables

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-27T14:06:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gfyy8.4388$Q42.167787@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3CCA6270.442153C0@shaw.ca>`

```
Figuring it out the old fashioned way - the logic would be:

   Three rows of three with each position able to assume one of 4 possible values:
   In each row, that means there would be 4^3 possible choices. (64 permutations
   in each row.) Three rows would mean there are 64^3 possible permutations in all,
  meaning you had 262,144 possible permutations.

  The interesting question is how many of those permutations are winning permutations?
  For each possible value, there would be 81 possible winning combinations ( I think,
  someone check me on that please?) and that means four values per slot * 3, or something
  like 972 possible winning combinations. That means you had a 1 in about 270 chance of
  winning something. The odds are much much lower of winning a particular prize of course.
  Then things shift when you count in a large number of cards.

   Back in the 80s a friend of mine got an idea about winning sweepstakes from some
   teenage movie where a 'computer genius' had the computer figure out the most likely
   combinations and sent in all the most likely winning combinations. He came up with a
   program that would do things like calculate the above stuff and come up with the most
   likely combinations. It sometimes came amazingly close, but when analyzed, it turned
   out to be just slightly better than pure chance. That turkey makes a lot of bucks working
   for some securities firm now.

   Anyway, if I goofed up the math above, it is purely because I am an idiot, and was amused
   by it. :)

-Paul


"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message news:3CCA6270.442153C0@shaw.ca...
> What a rip-off. Couldn't sleep so got up for a drink. There was one of
> those Scratch and Win cards on the table when I bought some Cadbury's
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Decision Tables

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-04-27T14:30:26-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B8F066820171284A0DB3DC60@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<3CCA6270.442153C0@shaw.ca> <gfyy8.4388$Q42.167787@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
On Sat, 27 Apr 2002 10:06:04 -0400, Paul Raulerson wrote
>   The interesting question is how many of those permutations are winning 
> permutations?

You can't assume that the symbols are randomly distributed, because 
they aren't. These kinds of "games" are carefully rigged so that they 
know in advance what prizes they are going to give out. In this case 
they probably give a lot of $1 prizes and very very few Sony Home 
Theater Systems.

The only way to determine the probability is to read the very fine 
print where it says how many cards they will give out and how many 
prizes they expect to give away. Otherwise you are just guessing, since 
you don't know how the game designers have set it up.

This kind of game plays on psychology. Lot of cards will have TWO Home 
Theater Systems and TWO Mini Component Systems -- the prizes people 
want to get. Funny thing, that's exactly what Jimmy's card had. Thus 
people see these symbols a lot, and psychologically they feel they are 
close to winning one of those prizes, and so they go and buy more of 
the product that the cards come with. They don't stop to think that it 
makes no difference how often they see SINGLE symbols -- only how many 
cards have enough symbols to win. And that number is limited by the 
game designers -- they print enough of those so that when multiplied by 
the probability of actually scratching off the right three (1 in 84), 
they'll award the expected number of prizes. Most likely they also know 
which combinations of three people will scratch more often -- for 
example, few people will scratch all three on the top row.

Nah, come to think of it, I'm sure that they determine the results even 
more tightly. They decide how many Home Theater Systems they will give 
away, and then print that many cards with ALL "H" symbols. Same with 
Mini Systems and "M" symbols. No one will notice, since after all, 
anyone who scratches off three alike isn't going to scratch off another 
and thus won't realize that the card was a predetermined winner. Every 
other card has exactly 2 Hs and 2 Ms -- no more, no less. They let 
probability manage the larger number of small prizes -- for every 84 
cards they print with 3 "$", they'll give away one prize 
(approximately). But no probability in the large prizes -- they know 
how many winning cards they printed, and that's how many they'll give 
away.

Edward Reid
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Decision Tables

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-04-27T18:44:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YjCy8.3211$9F5.211078@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3CCA6270.442153C0@shaw.ca> <gfyy8.4388$Q42.167787@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <01HW.B8F066820171284A0DB3DC60@news-east.usenetserver.com>`

```
Yep, you are most certainly right about that one. Isn't that how McDonalds (or rather a McDonalds subcontractor) just got into
some big trouble recently?  It's amazing how rigged most of these games are.
-Paul
```

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-04-27T18:25:34-04:00

- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B8F09D9E017E1ABD0DB3DC60@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<3CCA6270.442153C0@shaw.ca> <gfyy8.4388$Q42.167787@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <01HW.B8F066820171284A0DB3DC60@news-east.usenetserver.com> <YjCy8.3211$9F5.211078@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

#### ↳ Re: Decision Tables

- **From:** "DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net>
- **Date:** 2002-04-28T03:00:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aaga92$a5btb$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de>`
- **References:** `<3CCA6270.442153C0@shaw.ca>`

```
Other people have answered the odds, so I'll take a crack at the code:

*  table of the card combinations that our sponsor wants
*  total of 106,175 card created
*  total of 1060 guaranteed winners
*  total of 5115 others that can win
*  100,000 cards that have no chance
 01  possibility-table.
*  our sponsor has guaranteed 10 home theatre winners
    05 pic x(18) value 'hhhhhhhhh000000010'.
*  our sponsor has guaranteed 50 mini component systems
    05 pic x(18) value 'mmmmmmmmm000000050'.
*  our sponsor has guaranteed 1000 money winners
    05 pic x(18) value '$$$$$$$$$000001000'.
*  our sponsor has allowed for 15 other possible home theatre winners
    05 pic x(18) value 'hhhmm$$ss000000015'.
*  our sponsor has allowed for 100 other possible mini component winners
    05 pic x(18) value 'mmmhh$$ss000000100'.
*  our sponsor has allowed for 5000 other possible money winners
    05 pic x(18) value '$$$hhmmss000005000'.
*  the rest can't win, but it will be close (let's make 100,000 more)
    05 pic x(18) value 'ssshhmm$$000100000'.
01  filler redefines possibility-table.
    05 possibilities             occurs 7 times.
        10 tbl-values            pic x(9).
        10 tbl-remaining       pic 9(9).

*  work table of each value
01  work-values.
    05 work-value             occurs 9 times
                                        pic x.

*  the card layout (3 rows with 3 columns)
*  we redefined it to make it easy to move to (9 possible places)
01  card.
    05 rows                       occurs 3 times.
        10 columns              occurs 3 times
             15 card-value     pic x.
01 filler redefines card.
    05 card-placement       occurs 9 times
                                        pic x.

*  the number of possible card combinations
01  poss                           pic s9(9) comp value +7.

*  the subscript of the card combo table that we have randomly selected
01  tbl-sub                        pic s9(9) comp value zero.

*  subscript for the actual values
01  work-sub                    pic s9(9) comp value zero.

*  subscript for the placement on the card
01  card-sub                     pic s9(9) comp value zero.



*  there are seven possible card combinations
move +7 to poss.

*  run through card combinations, creating as many cards as requested
*  (note) - there should be code above to make that tbl-remaining for the
first card type is not zero
perform until tbl-remaining (1) = zero
*  space out the card values
    move spaces to card

* determine which card combo to use (randomly, unless only one left)
    if poss > 1
        compute tbl-sub = function random(poss)
        end-compute
    else
        move 1 to tbl-sub
    end if

*  if that combo did not come in zero (which really should be handled before
this routine),
*  move it to the work area (mostly for readability, you could have defined
the work table
*  within the possibility table)
    if tbl-remaining (tbl-sub) not = zero
        move tbl-values (tbl-sub) to work-values
*  subtract one from the remaining count
        compute tbl-remaining (tbl-sub) =  tbl-remaining (tbl-sub) - 1.
        end-compute
*  move the values, one by one, to randomly determined placement on the card
*  (there are faster ways to do this, but this is easy to read)
        perform varying work-sub from 1 by 1 until work-sub > 9
*  get random placement
            compute card-sub = function random(9)
            end-compute
*  make sure we haven't already put something there,
*  if we have, set the work-sub back down and try again
            if card-placement (card-sub) = spaces
                move work-value (work-sub) to card-placement (card-sub)
            else
                compute work-sub = work-sub - 1
                end-compute
            end-if
        end-perform
    end-if

*  if the remaining is zero, move it to the end of the table and set poss
down by one
    if tbl-remaining (tbl-sub) = zero
        move tbl-values (poss) to tbl-values (tbl-sub)
        move tbl-remaining (poss) to tbl-remaining (tbl-sub)
        compute poss = poss -1
        end-compute
    end-if
end-perform.


This would be for a "fixed" setup.  If we were to put truly random values on
the card, then you could do this:

01 chars                                 pic x(4) value 'hm$s'.
01 char-sub                            pic s9(9) comp value zero.
01 number-of-cards                pic s9(9) comp value zero.
01 number-of-cards-wanted   pic s9(9) comp value +100000.
< using card description above >

perform varying number-of-cards from 1 by 1 until number-of-cards >
number-of-cards-wanted
    move spaces to card
    perform varying card-sub from 1 by 1 until card-sub > 9
        compute char-sub = function random(4)
        end-compute
        move char (char-sub) to card-placement (card-sub)
    end-perform
end-perform.

This should give you the general idea (at least one way to do it)....

news:3CCA6270.442153C0@shaw.ca...
> What a rip-off. Couldn't sleep so got up for a drink. There was one of
> those Scratch and Win cards on the table when I bought some Cadbury's
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
the one now need a help and hope you can give me a advice. I need a MF
Cobol compiler for os/2  to do our project and can
not find one. I tried everywhere but no luck. Please be help and give me
reply ASAP.

Thank you for your time.

Kevin Li
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

I wrote toBill Klein and asked. There are no M/F compilers for OS2.

My attempt to use the e-mail address you gave me failed for two days,
being notified "Undelivered"  both times..

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Kevin Li

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-04-28T07:31:13+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CCB9731.8E79C5A9@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3CCA6501.6B679005@shaw.ca>`

```
> I need a MF
> Cobol compiler for os/2  to do our project and can
> not find one. 
> 
> I wrote toBill Klein and asked. There are no M/F compilers for OS2.

MF versions 2.5 to 3.4 included ability to run on OS/2 and create
programs for this, but was 16 bit only.  There was a MF OS/2 32bit
compiler released and withdrawn.

Microsoft Cobol version 4.5, being derived from MF 2.5, did support
OS/2.  MS 5.0 dropped this support (wonder why).

None of these are currently available AFAIK.
```

```
Support for version 2.2 ended January 31, 2001 - see:

http://www2.ibmlink.ibm.com/cgi-bin/master?xh=6a9z1WI$Lj1bn$1USenGnN9332&req
uest=announcements&parms=G3+298%2d099&xhi=usa%2emain%7cusa%2einfolink&xfr=N#
6zm

(and look for "Program Services")

Version 3.0 is still sold and supported but does NOT support OS/2, see:


http://www1.ibmlink.ibm.com/cgi-bin/master?xh=rzbdZRlgSb9rzX1USenGnN9332&req
uest=announcements&parms=H%5f200%2d107&xhi=usa%2emain%7cusa%2einfolink&xfr=N

    ***

Again, remember this question is about a COBOL product that is SUPPORTED for
OS/2. I believe that PERCOBOL meets this criteria - but I know of no others.
See:
  http://www.legacyj.com/perc_plat.html
```

```
Mark Dodel wrote:

>
> Yea what about that?  I have VA COBOL 1.1 which was out before
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> anymore.

Whether or not we consider the monopoly distasteful, Bill has done a TREMENDOUS
job with Windows, even if we feel locked in.

Don't forget, his opportunity came about as the result of a succession of events.

IBM's first choice was Gary (????? who wrote CP/M ?????). As the tale goes, two
IBM suits arranged to meet him, and he left them fuming on the ground while he
whistled around in his airplane above on a jaunt. (Gary ? used to do guest shots
on the old PBS computer program).

IBM's reaction wasn't the least bit unreasonable - they looked for an alternative
- Bill Gates. The incredibly stupid thing was the 'open ended' contract they gave
him.
Don't knock poor Gerstner, (well he aint really 'poor' is he ?). Whereas you are
a 'loyalist' to OS/2, IBM had to become 'realists' - OS/2 was going nowhere.

It bears thinking about. If Bill Gates hadn't opened up the world with Windows,
due to a contract restriction, would Big Blue, somewhat of a pondering giant,
have gotten us to where we are today ? Consider their approach to OO COBOL, they
keep shuffling it like a pack of cards.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
sham trial known as the USDOJ vs. MSFT?  Many of those events were

-)IBM's first choice was Gary (????? who wrote CP/M ?????). As the tale goes, two
-)IBM suits arranged to meet him, and he left them fuming on the ground while he
-)whistled around in his airplane above on a jaunt. (Gary ? used to do guest shots
-)on the old PBS computer program).
-)
-)IBM's reaction wasn't the least bit unreasonable - they looked for an alternative
-)- Bill Gates. The incredibly stupid thing was the 'open ended' contract they gave
-)him.

Yea, IBM contracted to use a copy of DOS which gates bought from 
Seattle Software, which Seattle Software pirated from DR.  So what's 
your point?  IBM's lawyers were either idiots, or were just really bad
chess players and had no ability to see how they were screwing IBM in 
the future.  Maybe both.

-)Don't knock poor Gerstner, (well he aint really 'poor' is he ?). Whereas you are
-)a 'loyalist' to OS/2, IBM had to become 'realists' - OS/2 was going nowhere.
-)

The cancer/cracker salesman was loyal only to himself.  He retired 
with his huge compensation pack just a week or two before the bottom 
fell out.  Have you seen the price of IBM's stock of late?  I have.  I
own a few hundred shares of it.

-)It bears thinking about. If Bill Gates hadn't opened up the world with Windows,
-)due to a contract restriction, would Big Blue, somewhat of a pondering giant,
-)have gotten us to where we are today ? Consider their approach to OO COBOL, they
-)keep shuffling it like a pack of cards.
-)

No monopoly is good, whether it be a government run/regulated one 
(dispite Mr. Mouse's nibble to the contrary, I am a conservative 
individual and I hate big government) or a criminal private one like 
m$.  windoze is crap, and has been since its inception.  What 
"innovation" there was was for the most part was stolen or bought from
others.

Anything would have been better then the bloated, mediocre drivel and 
pap that msft slops out year in, year out.  Don't you have any 
appreciation for clean, quality code that actually works?  What m$ has
done is a flatout sin.  All in the name of "windoze everywhere" and 
eliminating all competition.  Sorry but even if the latest wincrap is 
decent, I want alternatives so we wouldn't be in the mess we are today
where the weenie gates can pull all the puppet strings and get 
everyone to dance to his demented tune. Do you really think .net will 
improve things?  Once they have you locked in to their one and only 
platform even tighter then you are today, you can kiss anything but 
microslop goodbye.  And on the pc platform, that would pretty much do 
in COBOL.

Whew, boy you should never have gotten me started.  ;-)


Mark
```
